```mermaid
    T -->|No| NG[Cybersickness Risk]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern, Module 1**

### Question 1: Define a Video Game in the context of Virtual Reality. List the three foundational pillars. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
A VR video game is an interactive real-time 3D simulation that immerses the player inside a computer-generated environment using HMDs, spatial audio, and motion tracking. The three foundational pillars are:

1. **Immersion** — Sensory engagement of the player.
2. **Interactivity** — Real-time player agency with sub-20 ms latency.
3. **Game Mechanics** — Rule-based engine providing goals, feedback, and reward.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners award 1 mark per pillar. Always state the *technical term first*, then a one-line *engineering meaning*.

### Question 2: What is Motion-to-Photon Latency? State the industry-standard threshold. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Motion-to-Photon Latency is the total time elapsed between a real-world user movement and the corresponding updated pixel becoming visible on the HMD display.

- Formula: $L_{total} = L_{sensor} + L_{compute} + L_{render} + L_{display}$
- **Standard threshold: ≤ 20 ms** (above this, cybersickness occurs).
- For 90 FPS VR, the entire frame budget is **11.11 ms**.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU University Exam – End Semester Pattern**

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — `[14 Marks]`**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a VR video game system. Label all major subsystems and describe the function of each. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** Derive the total motion-to-photon latency for a system with the following sub-stage latencies and verify whether it satisfies the KTU/Industry standard of 20 ms:
- $L_{sensor} = 3 \text{ ms}$
- $L_{compute} = 2 \text{ ms}$
- $L_{render} = 9 \text{ ms}$
- $L_{display} = 3 \text{ ms}$ `[7 Marks]`

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

A VR video game system consists of three major subsystems:

**1. Input Subsystem (3 marks for diagram + 1 for explanation):**
- Head tracking IMU (3DOF rotation) and external cameras (6DOF positional).
- Hand controllers with capacitive buttons, triggers, and haptic actuators.
- Optional: eye tracking, voice input, full-body tracking.

**2. Game Engine Subsystem (2 marks):**
- **Physics Engine** — handles collisions, gravity, rigid body dynamics.
- **AI Engine** — drives NPC behavior, pathfinding, decision trees.
- **Rendering Engine** — performs stereoscopic rasterization for both eyes.
- **Scripting Layer** — defines game rules using C# (Unity) or Blueprint (Unreal).

**3. Output Subsystem (1 mark):**
- HMD with two LCD/OLED panels rendering left and right eye views.
- Spatial audio DSP for HRTF-based 3D sound.
- Haptic motors in controllers and suit.

*(A block diagram showing these three subsystems connected in a closed loop must be drawn for full marks. Refer to SECTION 4 diagram.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Governing equation.**

$$L_{total} = L_{sensor} + L_{compute} + L_{render} + L_{display}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$L_{total} = 3 + 2 + 9 + 3$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$L_{total} = 17 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Verification.**

$$17 \text{ ms} < 20 \text{ ms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{System PASSES}$$

**Step 5 — Safety margin.**

$$\text{Margin} = 20 - 17 = 3 \text{ ms}$$

The system meets the standard with a 3 ms safety buffer.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — `[14 Marks]`**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Compare traditional flat-screen video games with VR video games across at least six parameters. Justify why VR games require higher hardware specifications. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** A VR game is targeted to run at 90 FPS. Calculate the frame budget in milliseconds. If the GPU render stage takes 8 ms, sensor read takes 2 ms, compute filter takes 1 ms, and display takes 4 ms, find the *frame budget violation* (if any) and suggest one remedy. `[7 Marks]`

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Parameter | Traditional | VR | Why VR is Harder |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| FPS | 30–60 | ≥ 90 | Reduces vestibular mismatch |
| Views | 1 | 2 (stereo) | ~1.8× GPU pixel cost |
| Latency | ~100 ms OK | ≤ 20 ms | Stricter real-time bound |
| Input | 2D keyboard/mouse | 6DOF head + hands | More sensor fusion |
| FOV | ~60° | 100°–120° | Wider geometry to render |
| Comfort | None | Anti-nausea design | Restricts game mechanics |

**Justification (2 marks):** VR games require nearly **1.8× the GPU pixel throughput**, **2× the CPU physics load** (stereo simulation), and **5× tighter latency constraints**, all of which demand higher-end GPUs (RTX 4070+), faster SSDs, and high-refresh displays.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Frame budget calculation.**

$$T_{frame} = \frac{1000}{90} = 11.11 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the given latencies.**

$$L_{total} = 2 + 1 + 8 + 4 = 15 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Violation check.**

$$15 \text{ ms} > 11.11 \text{ ms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Violation of 3.89 ms}$$

**Step 4 — Remedy.** Enable **Asynchronous Spacewarp / Reprojection** to halve the effective render cost, or reduce polygon counts using **Level-of-Detail (LOD)** optimization.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students often write frame time in *seconds* instead of *milliseconds*. Always convert explicitly.
> 2. **Latency vs Frame Time:** These are *related but different* concepts. Latency is a sum; frame time is an inverse of FPS. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 3. **Missing Justification:** When asked to compare, students list differences but forget the *engineering reason*. Always add a "because..." clause.
> 4. **Diagram Labels:** Unlabeled block diagrams fetch 0 marks. Label *every block* with subsystem name and one functional phrase.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A VR Video Game = **Immersion + Interactivity + Game Mechanics** (I-I-G).
- ✅ The **Core Game Loop** is INPUT → UPDATE → SIMULATE → RENDER → DISPLAY → SENSE.
- ✅ **Frame Time** = $1000 / FPS$ ms. For 90 FPS, this is **11.11 ms**.
- ✅ **Motion-to-Photon Latency** must be **≤ 20 ms** to avoid cybersickness.
- ✅ VR requires **2× stereo rendering** (separate view per eye).
- ✅ **3DOF** = rotation only; **6DOF** = rotation + translation.
- ✅ Comfortable VR FOV is **100°–120°**, while human eye FOV is ~180°.
- ✅ **IPD** (Inter-Pupillary Distance) is ~64 mm and is a *biological* parameter, not a display parameter.
- ✅ Key game engines: **Unity (C#)** and **Unreal Engine (C++/Blueprint)**.
- ✅ Latency stages are **additive** (causal chain), not multiplicative.
- ✅ **Asynchronous Reprojection** is a standard remedy for frame budget violations.
- ✅ **PPD (Pixels Per Degree)** ≥ 15 is the modern HMD benchmark.
- ✅ VR games are categorized into FPS, Rhythm, Puzzle, Simulation, Social, Fitness, and Educational genres.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question on Video Games, structure your answer as: **(1)** Definition → **(2)** Block Diagram → **(3)** Three Pillars → **(4)** Latency derivation → **(5)** Comparison Table → **(6)** Conclusion. This six-part structure guarantees full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beyond Staring at a Rectangle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT504 - VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Beyond Staring at a Rectangle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Beyond Staring at a Rectangle"** is a foundational concept in Virtual Reality (VR) that addresses the limitations of conventional 2D computer monitors (flat rectangular screens) and motivates the transition to **immersive, spatially-aware display systems**. In the context of KTU's PBCNT504 syllabus, this topic is concerned with the design rationale, perceptual consequences, and engineering alternatives to the traditional WIMP (Windows, Icons, Menus, Pointer) graphical interface displayed on a flat rectangle.

In the canonical taxonomy proposed by **Mel Slater and Martin Usoh** (and elaborated by Steve Bryson in *The Digital Apollo* and related VR literature), the "rectangle" represents the **desktop metaphor** — a fixed, small, framed window into a virtual world. The "beyond" refers to display systems that:

1. Enlarge the user's field of view (FOV) toward the natural human range (**≈ 200°** horizontally, **≈ 130°** vertically with overlap).
2. Track the user's head, eyes, or body to update the rendered image in real time.
3. Present stereoscopic imagery to provide binocular depth cues.
4. Optionally, surround the user on multiple sides (CAVE, workbenches, HMDs).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (CO1):** This topic directly maps to Course Outcome 1 — *"Understand the fundamental concepts, historical evolution, and the perceptual basis of Virtual Reality systems."* It also seeds Module 2 (VR Hardware) and Module 3 (Rendering Pipelines).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **looking through a small window (say, 50 cm × 30 cm) in a wall**. Outside the window lies a beautiful garden, but you can only ever see a small slice of it. No matter how you move your head, the world appears as if painted on that single rectangular pane of glass. You can turn the window, but the world stays on the other side of the glass — flat, framed, and at a fixed distance.

Now, **imagine the wall dissolves**. The garden surrounds you. Turn your head left, and you see the roses. Look up, and you see the sky. Walk forward, and the path unrolls beneath your feet. This is the leap *"beyond staring at a rectangle."* The "rectangle" is the metaphor for **any 2D screen**, and "beyond" is the move toward **stereoscopic, wide-FOV, head-tracked, body-centered immersive displays**.

| Metaphor Element | Real World Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Small window pane | Desktop monitor / TV screen |
| Painted scene on glass | Rasterized 2D graphics |
| Wall dissolving | CAVE / HMD / Powerwall |
| Head turning reveals new scenery | **Head-tracking** updates the view matrix |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering takeaway:** The rectangle is not evil — it is a *trade-off* between **immersion, cost, ergonomics, and fidelity**. A good VR engineer chooses the right display configuration for the application (training, gaming, scientific visualization, telepresence).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in This Topic

- **Field of View (FOV):** The angular extent of the visual field covered by the display. Desktop monitor ≈ **30°–60°**; immersive HMD ≥ **100°**; natural human vision ≈ **200°** (horizontal, monocular + binocular overlap).
- **Resolution:** Pixels per degree (**PPD**). A 1920×1080 panel spanning 110° ≈ **17.5 PPD**.
- **Refresh rate:** **60 Hz, 90 Hz, 120 Hz** (modern HMDs target ≥ 90 Hz to avoid VR sickness).
- **Binocular overlap:** Region seen by both eyes, typically **≥ 90°** in good HMDs.
- **Latency (Motion-to-Photon):** End-to-end delay from user motion to updated pixels, ideally **≤ 20 ms** (≤ 11 ms for elite systems).
- **Interpupillary Distance (IPD):** ≈ **63 mm** average human range, **50–75 mm** adjustable in HMDs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Rectangle" Problem — Why 2D Screens Limit VR

When a user stares at a 2D rectangle (a monitor, a TV, a phone), the visual system is presented with a **fundamentally conflicting set of cues**:

1. **Perspective & motion parallax conflict** — The brain expects that moving the head should change the view, but on a flat screen the world appears glued to the rectangle.
2. **Lack of stereopsis** — Both eyes see essentially the same image, so binocular depth cues are absent.
3. **Limited FOV** — A 24" monitor at 60 cm distance gives a horizontal FOV of roughly **44°**, far below the natural human range.
4. **Fixed eye focus (vergence–accommodation conflict)** — Eyes accommodate to the screen surface (e.g., 60 cm) but converge to a different depth in the rendered scene. This is a major source of visual fatigue in non-stereo or even stereo 2D displays.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Beyond the rectangle** means systematically addressing each of these conflicts through hardware and software innovations.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Display Systems Beyond the Rectangle

| Display Class | FOV (approx.) | Stereo | Tracking | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Desktop Monitor (FishTank VR)** | 30°–60° | Optional | Optional (face/head tracker) | Low-cost VR, CAD review |
| **Workbench / Responsive Workbench** | 60°–100° | Yes | Head + hand | Scientific viz |
| **Powerwall / Large Screen** | 60°–120° (mono or stereo) | Yes (active/passive) | Head + wand | Collaborative viz |
| **CAVE (Cave Automatic Virtual Environment)** | Surround (all walls + floor) | Yes (active shutter glasses) | Full body | Engineering simulation |
| **HMD (Head-Mounted Display)** | 90°–210° | Yes (built-in) | Built-in IMU + cameras | Consumer VR, training |
| **Holographic / Light Field** | Variable | Multi-focal | Eye-tracking | Future AR/VR |

## 2.3 Core Theory — The Geometry of a Rectangle → Immersive Display

### 2.3.1 Field of View (FOV) of a Rectangular Screen

For a rectangular screen of **width $W$** and **height $H$**, viewed from a perpendicular distance $d$ (eye to screen):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FOV}_{\text{horizontal}} &= 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{W/2}{d}\right) \\
\text{FOV}_{\text{vertical}} &= 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{H/2}{d}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $W, H$ are the screen width and height (in **meters**).
- $d$ is the perpendicular eye-to-screen distance (in **meters**).
- The result is in **radians** (multiply by $\frac{180}{\pi}$ for degrees).

### 2.3.2 Pixels Per Degree (PPD)

$$
\text{PPD} = \frac{\text{Resolution}_{\text{horizontal}}}{\text{FOV}_{\text{horizontal}}} \quad \text{(pixels per degree)}
$$

A PPD of **≥ 20** is generally considered the **retinal resolution threshold** (the level above which a person with 20/20 vision cannot resolve individual pixels).

### 2.3.3 Motion-to-Photon Latency Budget

For comfortable VR, total end-to-end latency $T_{\text{total}}$ should satisfy:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{sensor}} + T_{\text{tracking}} + T_{\text{simulation}} + T_{\text{render}} + T_{\text{display}} \;\leq\; T_{\text{max}}
$$

Where the **upper bound** is empirically:

$$
T_{\text{max}} \approx 20 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(acceptable)}, \qquad T_{\text{max}} \approx 11 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(elite VR)}
$$

### 2.3.4 Vergence–Accommodation Conflict Distance

For a stereo display, the **vergence distance** $z_v$ (where both eyes converge) and the **accommodation distance** $z_a$ (where the eyes focus) differ. For a Wheatstone-type stereoscope with image plane at $z_a$ and rendered object at $z_v$:

$$
\Delta z = \vert z_v - z_a \vert
$$

The visual comfort zone typically requires $\Delta z \lesssim 0.5$ m (varies with age and user).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{FOV} = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{S/2}{d}\right)$ | Field of view of a screen of size $S$ at distance $d$ | **degrees / radians** |
| 2 | $\text{PPD} = \dfrac{\text{pixels}}{\text{FOV}}$ | Display angular resolution | **pixels/degree** |
| 3 | $T_{\text{total}} = \sum T_i \le 20$ ms | Motion-to-photon budget | **milliseconds** |
| 4 | $\Delta z = \vert z_v - z_a \vert$ | Vergence–accommodation mismatch | **meters** |
| 5 | $I = \dfrac{\text{Resolution}_{\text{linear}}}{\text{FOV}_{\text{linear}}}$ | Visual acuity metric | **arcmin/pixel** |
| 6 | $\text{Frame budget} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Refresh Rate}}$ | Time per frame (e.g., 90 Hz → 11.11 ms) | **ms** |
| 7 | $f_{\text{eye}} = \dfrac{17\text{ mm}}{N}$ | Eye focal length (N typically 60–70) | **mm** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** These formulas drive HMD lens design, panel selection, GPU pipeline budgets, and content authoring decisions in every VR system from Meta Quest to medical surgery simulators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Field of View of a Rectangular Screen

We derive the horizontal FOV of a flat screen of width $W$ viewed from perpendicular distance $d$.

**Step 1: Set up the geometry.** Place the eye at the origin. The screen lies in the plane $z = d$, occupying $x \in [-W/2, +W/2]$ and $y \in [-H/2, +H/2]$.

**Step 2: Compute the half-angle to the screen edge.** The ray from the eye to the right edge of the screen has horizontal half-angle $\theta_h$ such that:

$$
\tan(\theta_h) = \frac{W/2}{d}
$$

This is simply the ratio of the *opposite* (half-width) to the *adjacent* (eye-to-screen distance) in the right-triangle formed by the eye, the center of the screen, and the right edge.

**Step 3: Solve for the half-angle.** Apply the inverse tangent:

$$
\theta_h = \arctan\!\left(\frac{W}{2d}\right)
$$

**Step 4: Double the half-angle to get the full FOV.** The total angular extent is symmetric on both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FOV}_{\text{horizontal}} &= 2\,\theta_h \\
&= 2\,\arctan\!\left(\frac{W}{2d}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Analogous derivation for vertical FOV** (replace $W$ with $H$):

$$
\text{FOV}_{\text{vertical}} = 2\,\arctan\!\left(\frac{H}{2d}\right)
$$

**Step 6: Numerical example.** Consider a 24-inch diagonal monitor (16:9 aspect ratio) at distance $d = 0.6$ m.

- Diagonal $= 24 \text{ in} = 0.6096 \text{ m}$.
- Width $W = 0.6096 \times \dfrac{16}{\sqrt{16^2 + 9^2}} = 0.6096 \times 0.8714 \approx 0.5312$ m.
- Height $H = 0.6096 \times \dfrac{9}{\sqrt{16^2 + 9^2}} = 0.6096 \times 0.4904 \approx 0.2989$ m.

Now:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FOV}_{\text{horizontal}} &= 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{0.5312/2}{0.6}\right) \\
&= 2\arctan(0.4427) \\
&= 2 \times 0.4158 \text{ rad} \\
&= 0.8316 \text{ rad} \\
&\approx 47.65^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

And:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FOV}_{\text{vertical}} &= 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{0.2989/2}{0.6}\right) \\
&= 2\arctan(0.2491) \\
&= 2 \times 0.2447 \text{ rad} \\
&\approx 0.4894 \text{ rad} \\
&\approx 28.04^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even at a comfortable 60 cm viewing distance, a typical 24" monitor only delivers about **48° × 28°** of FOV — roughly **one-quarter** of the natural human horizontal FOV. This is the core geometric reason why staring at a rectangle feels *non-immersive*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pixels-Per-Degree (PPD) of a Display

**Step 1: Recall FOV from derivation 1.** The horizontal FOV is $\text{FOV}_h = 2\arctan(W/2d)$.

**Step 2: Define pixel density.** A modern display has a horizontal resolution of $R_h$ pixels across width $W$.

**Step 3: Compute pixels per radian:**

$$
\text{PPR} = \frac{R_h}{\text{FOV}_h} \quad \text{(pixels per radian)}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to pixels per degree** using the conversion factor $\dfrac{180}{\pi} \approx 57.2958$:

$$
\text{PPD} = \frac{\text{PPR}}{57.2958} = \frac{R_h}{\text{FOV}_h \times 57.2958}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical example.** A 1920×1080 panel covering a horizontal FOV of $47.65°$:

$$
\text{PPD} = \frac{1920}{47.65} \approx 40.3 \text{ pixels/degree}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a wider FOV HMD (e.g., 110°) at the same resolution, the PPD would drop to $\dfrac{1920}{110} \approx 17.5$ — explaining the "screen door effect" seen on early HMDs.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Motion-to-Photon Latency Budget

**Step 1: Decompose the latency pipeline** into sequential stages, each adding its own delay:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{sensor}} + T_{\text{filter}} + T_{\text{sim}} + T_{\text{render}} + T_{\text{scan-out}} + T_{\text{phosphor/LC}}
$$

Typical values (modern HMD):

| Stage | Typical Latency | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{\text{sensor}}$ (IMU sample) | 1 – 2 ms | 1000 Hz IMU |
| $T_{\text{filter}}$ (sensor fusion) | 1 – 3 ms | Kalman / Madgwick |
| $T_{\text{sim}}$ (physics, anim) | 1 – 5 ms | App dependent |
| $T_{\text{render}}$ (GPU) | 4 – 8 ms | 90 Hz → 11.1 ms total |
| $T_{\text{scan-out}}$ (display) | 2 – 5 ms | Depends on panel |
| $T_{\text{LC response}}$ | 2 – 5 ms | LCD only (OLED ≈ 0) |

**Step 2: Sum for a typical system:**

$$
T_{\text{total}} \approx 2 + 2 + 3 + 6 + 4 + 3 = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Frame budget check** at 90 Hz refresh:

$$
T_{\text{frame}} = \frac{1}{90} \approx 11.11 \text{ ms}
$$

Since $T_{\text{total}} = 20$ ms > $T_{\text{frame}} = 11.11$ ms, the system must use **prediction** (a.k.a. *Asynchronous Time Warp* or *Reprojection*) to estimate the user's head pose at scan-out time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Predictive rendering equation:** Render at pose $\hat{P}(t + \Delta t)$ instead of $P(t)$, where $\Delta t$ is the predicted delay.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Desktop Rectangle | FishTank VR | HMD | CAVE |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Horizontal FOV | 30°–60° | 60°–100° | 90°–210° | 360° surround |
| Stereoscopy | No (native) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Head tracking | No | Yes (1 DOF) | Yes (6 DOF) | Yes (6 DOF) |
| Immersion rank | 1 | 2 | 4 | 5 |
| Cost rank | 1 | 2 | 3 | 5 |
| Social presence | High | High | Low | Medium |
| VR sickness risk | None | Low | Medium–High | Low–Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture — From "Rectangle" to Immersive Display

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **functional flow** of how a 2D rectangle-based display system is upgraded to a fully immersive VR system, and the engineering sub-systems involved at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Head and Eyes] --> B[Sensors: IMU + Cameras]
    B --> C[Tracking and Prediction Module]
    C --> D[Pose Estimator: P of t]
    D --> E[Scene Graph and Physics Simulation]
    E --> F[Stereo Renderer: Left + Right Eye]
    F --> G[Display Subsystem]
    G --> H{Display Type}
    H --> H1[Desktop Monitor Rectangle]
    H --> H2[Powerwall Large Screen]
    H --> H3[CAVE Surround]
    H --> H4[HMD Head Mounted]
    H1 --> I[User Perception]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I --> J[Feedback Loop: Vergence Accommodation Conflict Check]
    J --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The rectangle is represented by node **H1**; the four display branches correspond to the taxonomy in Section 2.2. The closed feedback loop (J → C) is what makes the system *immersive* — the user's perception feeds back into the next pose estimate.

## 4.2 Diagram: Geometry of a Rectangular Screen vs. Immersive HMD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RECT[Rectangle World 2D Screen]
        A1[Eye] --- A2[Screen Center]
        A2 --- A3[Right Edge of Screen]
        A3 --> A1
    end
    subgraph IMM[Immersive World HMD or CAVE]
        B1[Eye] --- B2[Virtual Object at depth z]
        B2 --- B3[Surround Display Hemisphere]
        B3 --> B1
    end
    RECT --> IMM
```

## 4.3 Diagram: Motion-to-Photon Latency Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[User Head Moves] --> S2[IMU Sensor Reads]
    S2 --> S3[Sensor Fusion Filter]
    S3 --> S4[Pose Prediction for t plus dt]
    S4 --> S5[Render New Stereo Frame]
    S5 --> S6[Display Scanout]
    S6 --> S7[Photons Reach Retina]
    S7 --> S8[User Sees Updated View]
```

Each block in this pipeline corresponds to a delay term $T_i$ from Section 3.3. The sum of all block times is $T_{\text{total}}$, and the system's goal is $T_{\text{total}} \le 20$ ms.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"What is meant by 'Beyond Staring at a Rectangle' in the context of Virtual Reality? List any two limitations of using a conventional 2D monitor for VR applications."**

**Model Answer:**

"Beyond Staring at a Rectangle" refers to the transition in VR from traditional 2D rectangular displays (monitors, TVs) to immersive display systems such as **HMDs, CAVEs, and Powerwalls** that surround and respond to the user. It emphasizes adding wide FOV, stereoscopy, head-tracking, and body-centered interaction to overcome the constraints of the flat rectangle.

**Two limitations of a 2D monitor for VR:**

1. **Limited FOV** — A typical 24" monitor at 60 cm gives only **≈ 48° × 28°** of FOV, far below the natural **≈ 200°** human horizontal FOV, breaking immersion.
2. **No stereoscopic depth** — Both eyes see the same image, so the binocular disparity cue is absent, weakening depth perception.

*(Acceptable additional answers: lack of head tracking, vergence–accommodation conflict, no motion parallax, no sense of "being there".)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define 'Field of View' (FOV) as applied to a VR display. Compute the horizontal FOV of a screen of width 0.5 m placed 0.6 m from the eye.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Field of View (FOV) is the angular extent of the visual field, expressed in degrees or radians, that a display covers as seen from the viewer's eye position.

**Computation:** Using the formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FOV}_{\text{horizontal}} &= 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{W}{2d}\right) \\
&= 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{0.5}{2 \times 0.6}\right) \\
&= 2\arctan(0.4167) \\
&= 2 \times 0.3948 \text{ rad} \\
&\approx 0.7896 \text{ rad} \\
&\approx 45.24^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the horizontal FOV is approximately **45.24°**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**a)** Explain the concept of "Beyond Staring at a Rectangle" with reference to immersive VR displays. Compare any **three** display technologies (HMD, CAVE, Powerwall) in terms of FOV, stereo support, and tracking capability. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Derive the formula for the horizontal Field of View of a rectangular screen. Compute the horizontal and vertical FOV of a 27-inch 16:9 monitor viewed from 0.7 m. Comment on whether this configuration is suitable for an immersive VR application. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**

The phrase "Beyond Staring at a Rectangle" captures the paradigm shift in VR from 2D rectangular displays to immersive, multi-sensory systems. A 2D screen confines the user to a small framed window, breaking the illusion of presence. Going "beyond" implies:

- Enlarging FOV to approach the natural human range (~200°).
- Adding head/eye tracking for view-dependent rendering.
- Supporting stereoscopic rendering for binocular depth.
- Surrounding the user (CAVE) or mounting the display on the head (HMD).

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | HMD | CAVE | Powerwall |
|---|---|---|---|
| FOV | 90°–210° (per eye) | 360° surround (limited by wall count) | 60°–120° |
| Stereo Support | Built-in dual displays | Active shutter glasses | Active or passive glasses |
| Tracking | IMU + inside-out cameras (6 DOF) | Optical outside-in (full body) | Optical + wand |
| Mobility | High (untethered options) | Limited to room | Stationary |
| Cost | Low–Medium | Very high | Medium–High |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Derivation (4 marks):** *(as in Section 3.1)*

Place the eye at the origin, screen of width $W$ in the plane $z=d$. The half-angle to the edge is $\tan(\theta) = \dfrac{W/2}{d}$, so $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{W}{2d}\right)$, and the full horizontal FOV is:

$$
\text{FOV}_h = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{W}{2d}\right)
$$

**Numerical Computation (2 marks):**

For a 27" 16:9 monitor at $d = 0.7$ m:

- Diagonal $= 0.6858$ m; width $W = 0.6858 \times \dfrac{16}{\sqrt{337}} = 0.5976$ m; height $H = 0.6858 \times \dfrac{9}{\sqrt{337}} = 0.3361$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FOV}_h &= 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{0.5976/2}{0.7}\right) \\
&= 2\arctan(0.4269) \\
&\approx 2 \times 0.4034 \text{ rad} \\
&\approx 46.13^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FOV}_v &= 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{0.3361/2}{0.7}\right) \\
&= 2\arctan(0.2401) \\
&\approx 2 \times 0.2353 \text{ rad} \\
&\approx 26.97^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Suitability Comment (1 mark):** A FOV of **46.13° × 26.97°** is **insufficient for immersive VR**, as it is only about **one-fifth** of the natural human horizontal FOV. A wide-FOV HMD or CAVE is required for true immersion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**a)** What is meant by **Pixels Per Degree (PPD)**? For a 1920×1080 HMD covering a horizontal FOV of 110°, compute the PPD. Is this value above or below the retinal resolution threshold? Comment. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain the **motion-to-photon latency** pipeline. List any **four** stages and state the typical upper bound for comfortable VR. Why is low latency critical in immersive displays? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition (2 marks):** Pixels Per Degree (PPD) is the angular display resolution, defined as the number of pixels subtending one degree of the user's visual field. It is a key metric of display sharpness.

$$
\text{PPD} = \frac{R_h}{\text{FOV}_h \times 57.2958}
$$

*(The factor 57.2958 converts radians to degrees.)*

**Computation (3 marks):**

$$
\text{PPD} = \frac{1920}{110} \approx 17.45 \text{ pixels/degree}
$$

**Comparison and Comment (2 marks):** The retinal resolution threshold is approximately **60 PPD** (20/20 vision resolves ~1 arcmin = 1/60°). The computed **17.45 PPD** is **well below** the threshold, meaning the user will perceive individual pixels (the **"screen-door effect"**) and fine detail will be lost. Modern headsets address this with higher-resolution panels (e.g., 2160×2160 per eye, ≥ 25 PPD) and advanced sub-pixel layouts.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Definition (2 marks):** Motion-to-photon latency is the total time elapsed from a user's physical motion (e.g., head turn) to the corresponding updated pixels being displayed on the screen.

**Four Pipeline Stages (3 marks):**

1. **Sensor sampling** (IMU / camera) — $T_{\text{sensor}}$ ≈ 1–2 ms.
2. **Sensor fusion and pose estimation** — $T_{\text{filter}}$ ≈ 1–3 ms.
3. **Scene simulation and rendering** — $T_{\text{render}}$ ≈ 4–8 ms.
4. **Display scan-out and pixel response** — $T_{\text{display}}$ ≈ 2–5 ms.

**Typical upper bound (1 mark):** **≤ 20 ms** for acceptable VR; **≤ 11 ms** for elite, sickness-free VR (corresponds to ≥ 90 Hz frame rate).

**Why low latency is critical (1 mark):** High latency causes a mismatch between **vestibular (inner-ear) and visual motion cues**, leading to **vection**, **VR sickness (cybersickness)**, and broken presence. In head-tracked systems, latency above 20 ms is perceived as the world "lagging" or "swimming" with head motion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse **FOV (angular extent)** with **PPD (angular resolution)**. They are *inverse* concepts in the context of fixed panel resolution: increasing FOV *reduces* PPD for the same display. Marks are commonly lost when students fail to convert between **radians and degrees** ($180/\pi \approx 57.2958$) in PPD calculations. Also, do **not** confuse the *diagonal* dimension of a monitor with its *width* — always extract $W$ and $H$ from the aspect ratio.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for the KTU exam:**

- **"Beyond Staring at a Rectangle"** = transition from 2D monitors to immersive VR displays.
- **Natural human FOV** ≈ **200°** horizontal, **135°** vertical, binocular overlap ≈ **120°**.
- **Desktop monitor FOV** is typically **30°–60°**, far below natural vision.
- **Immersive displays** include **HMD, CAVE, Powerwall, Workbench, FishTank VR**.
- **FOV formula:** $\text{FOV} = 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{S}{2d}\right)$ — used for *both* width and height.
- **PPD formula:** $\text{PPD} = \dfrac{\text{Resolution}}{\text{FOV}}$ (in degrees); retinal threshold ≈ **60 PPD**.
- **Latency budget:** $T_{\text{total}} = \sum T_i \le 20$ ms (acceptable), **≤ 11 ms** (elite, 90 Hz).
- **Vergence–accommodation conflict** is a key comfort issue in all stereoscopic displays.
- **Six Degrees of Freedom (6 DOF)** = 3 translations + 3 rotations — required for full immersion.
- **Head tracking + prediction (Asynchronous Time Warp)** compensates for the unavoidable rendering delay.
- **Always convert** trig results from **radians to degrees** (or vice versa) — examiners test this.
- **Know the differences** between HMD, CAVE, Powerwall, and Workbench in terms of FOV, stereo, tracking, and cost.
- **Practical engineering trade-off:** More FOV = lower PPD (for the same panel) and higher GPU load.
- **KPI for immersion ranking:** FOV > Stereo > Tracking > Resolution > Audio (rough order of perceptual impact).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: VR Headsets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT504 - VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** VR Headsets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VR Headset (Virtual Reality Headset)** — A **Head-Mounted Display (HMD)** device that delivers stereoscopic, wide-Field-of-View (FOV) visual content to the user, combined with head-motion tracking (typically **6 Degrees of Freedom (6DoF)**) to render an immersive, first-person virtual environment in real time.

In the strict KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, a VR Headset is the **primary visual output hardware** of any VR system. It is fundamentally composed of:

- **Two micro-displays** (one per eye) — to provide stereoscopic 3D perception.
- **Optical lenses** (typically **Fresnel** or **pancake** lenses) — to focus the close display screen and project a virtual image at "optical infinity."
- **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** — comprising a 3-axis gyroscope, accelerometer, and magnetometer.
- **Tracking sensors** — either **active IR LEDs**, **passive markers**, or **onboard cameras** (inside-out tracking).
- **Processing unit** — either an external PC (tethered) or an embedded SoC (standalone).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a pair of **ski goggles** that someone has surgically converted into two tiny, private movie theaters. Each goggle lens covers a **television screen** floating millimeters from your eye. When you turn your head, the movie cameras (the render engine) also turn, so the image you see is *always* matched to your gaze — tricking your brain into believing you are *inside* the scene.

A simpler way to think about it:
- A **VR Headset = Stereoscopic TV strapped to your face + motion tracker.**
- Your left eye sees an image shifted slightly to the right, your right eye sees an image shifted slightly to the left. Your brain fuses them into one 3D image — this is called **stereopsis**.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The performance of a VR headset is universally quantified by these five metrics:

| Metric | Typical Range | Importance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Resolution (per eye)** | 1832 × 1920 to 2160 × 2160 px | Image sharpness |
| **Refresh Rate** | 72 Hz to 120 Hz | Motion smoothness |
| **Field of View (FOV)** | 90° to 130° | Immersion |
| **Interpupillary Distance (IPD)** | 58 mm to 72 mm | Eye-comfort |
| **Tracking DoF** | 3DoF or 6DoF | Positional realism |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3 Pillars of Immersion in a VR Headset:**
> 1. **Wide FOV** (≥ 90°) — replicates the human binocular FOV of ~114°.
> 2. **High Refresh Rate** (≥ 90 Hz) — prevents motion sickness.
> 3. **Low Persistence Display** (< 3 ms) — reduces motion blur.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Modern VR Headset

A VR Headset is not a single component — it is a **system of subsystems** working in tight synchronization. Below is the canonical architectural breakdown used in KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus:

### 2.1.1 Display Subsystem
- The two micro-displays are typically **AMOLED** or **fast-switch LCD** panels.
- The choice of display technology directly impacts **pixel response time**, **contrast ratio**, and **screen-door effect (SDE)**.
- The **screen-door effect** is the visible gap between pixels seen through the lens; mitigated by:
  - Higher pixel density (PPD > 20).
  - **Diffusion layers** in advanced pancake-lens designs.

### 2.1.2 Optical Subsystem
- A single **convex lens** per eye acts as a **magnifier** for the micro-display.
- The lens must be placed at its **focal length (f)** from the display so the virtual image appears at infinity.
- **Fresnel lenses** are concentric grooved replacements for thick convex lenses, reducing weight.
- **Pancake lenses** use **polarized light folding** to achieve even shorter optical paths.

### 2.1.3 Tracking Subsystem
- **3 Degrees of Freedom (3DoF):** Tracks **rotational motion only** (yaw, pitch, roll). Example: Google Cardboard, Samsung Gear VR.
- **6 Degrees of Freedom (6DoF):** Tracks **rotational + translational** motion (x, y, z). Example: Meta Quest 3, HTC Vive Pro 2.
- **Inside-out tracking:** Cameras on the HMD observe the external world.
- **Outside-in tracking:** External base stations/cameras observe the HMD.

### 2.1.4 Audio & Haptic Subsystem
- **Spatial (3D) audio** is delivered via on-ear speakers or off-ear drivers using **HRTF (Head-Related Transfer Function)**.
- **Haptic feedback** is provided via handheld controllers with **linear resonant actuators (LRAs)**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is a wide FOV critical?**
> The human eye has a horizontal FOV of **~210°** (with peripheral vision) and a binocular overlap of **~114°**. If the HMD FOV is below this, the user perceives a **black border (tunnel vision)** — a phenomenon called the **"porthole effect,"** which immediately breaks immersion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why must refresh rate exceed 90 Hz?**
> Below 90 Hz, the brain detects the discrepancy between **vestibular input** (motion of inner ear fluid) and **visual input** (frame on screen), causing **simulator sickness / VR motion sickness**. High refresh rate keeps this mismatch under the **vection threshold.**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam essentials for Module 1.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Unit | Application |
| :- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $FOV = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{d}{2f}\right)$ | $d$ = display half-diagonal, $f$ = focal length | degrees (°) | Lens–display FOV design |
| 2 | $PPD = \dfrac{\text{Resolution}_x}{FOV_x}$ | Pixels per degree | px/° | Image sharpness metric |
| 3 | $S_{stereo} = 2 \cdot f \cdot \tan\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$ | Stereo separation, $\theta$ = convergence angle | mm | Stereoscopic geometry |
| 4 | $T_{frame} = \dfrac{1}{f_{refresh}}$ | Frame time | seconds (s) | Latency budget |
| 5 | $T_{motion-to-photon} = T_{sensor} + T_{render} + T_{display}$ | End-to-end latency | ms | Comfort threshold (< 20 ms) |
| 6 | $V = \dfrac{IPD}{2 \cdot f}$ | Vergence angle helper | dimensionless | Lens–eye alignment |
| 7 | $PPD_{req} = \dfrac{1.0'}{60''/deg} \approx 60$ | Retina resolution target | px/° | "Retina-quality" VR |
| 8 | $D_{lens} = \dfrac{d}{2 \cdot \tan(FOV/2)}$ | Required lens diameter | mm | Optical hardware design |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use the vertical bar `|` in KTU answer sheets for absolute value.** Write $\vert x \vert$ or simply *mod x* when handwriting.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

VR Headsets are foundational to:
- **Medical training** (surgical simulation in neurosurgery, e.g., Surgical Theater).
- **Aerospace** (astronaut training, NASA hybrid reality).
- **Automotive** (BMW, Volvo use VR for design reviews).
- **Architecture & Real Estate** (immersive walkthroughs).
- **Military** (dismounted soldier training using IVAS / HoloLens derivatives).
- **Education** (zSpace, Engage VR for distance learning).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Field of View of a VR Headset Lens

**Problem Setup:** A micro-display of half-diagonal $d$ is placed at the focal length $f$ of a thin convex lens. Derive the horizontal Field of View (FOV) seen by the eye.

### Step 1 — Geometric Setup
Place the eye at the focal point of the lens. Light rays from the edge of the display exit the lens as **parallel rays** (since the object is at focal length, the image forms at infinity).

### Step 2 — Half-Angle Calculation
The angle $\theta$ subtended at the eye by the half-diagonal $d$ is given by the basic tangent definition:

$$
\tan(\theta) = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \dfrac{d}{f}
$$

### Step 3 — Double the Half-Angle
The total Field of View is the **full angle** (both edges of the display):

$$
FOV = 2\theta = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{d}{f}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Adjust for Half-Diagonal
If we use the **full display diagonal** $D_{full} = 2d$, then the half-diagonal $d = D_{full}/2$:

$$
FOV = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{D_{full}/2}{f}\right) = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{D_{full}}{2f}\right)
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{FOV = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{D_{full}}{2f}\right)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Decreasing the focal length $f$ (using a more powerful lens) or increasing the display size $D$ *both* widen the FOV — but a smaller $f$ brings the lens closer to the eye, which is mechanically constrained by the human nose bridge (~ 30 mm minimum).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pixels Per Degree (PPD) Calculation

**Given:**
- Display resolution: 2160 × 2160 per eye (Meta Quest 3 standard).
- Horizontal FOV of the headset: 110°.

**Find:** Pixels per degree (PPD).

### Step 1 — Identify the Axis
We work along the **horizontal axis**:
- Pixels horizontally: $R_x = 2160$.
- Angular spread: $FOV_x = 110°$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Linear Mapping
Each degree of visual angle covers an equal slice of pixels (since the lens maps linear distance to angular position at the focal plane):

$$
PPD = \dfrac{R_x}{FOV_x}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Values

$$
PPD = \dfrac{2160}{110}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation

$$
PPD = 19.6363... \approx 19.64 \; px/^\circ
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The human eye can resolve ~ 60 PPD. At ~ 20 PPD, the user still perceives the **screen-door effect** faintly. Modern headsets target > 25 PPD to be considered "retina-crisp."

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stereoscopic Separation (Parallax)

**Problem:** Two virtual objects, one at $z_1 = 1.0$ m and another at $z_2 = 3.0$ m in front of the eye, are rendered for a headset. Compute the **stereoscopic disparity** $\Delta x$ on the display plane. Assume IPD = 64 mm and focal length $f = 39$ mm.

### Step 1 — Vergence Angle Formula
For an object at depth $z$, the convergence angle of each eye is:

$$
\theta(z) = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{IPD/2}{z}\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Disparity Definition
The disparity is the **difference in convergence angle** multiplied by the focal length (since the lens maps angle to display position):

$$
\Delta x = f \cdot \left[ \theta(z_1) - \theta(z_2) \right]
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $z_1 = 1.0$ m, $z_2 = 3.0$ m

$$
\theta(z_1) = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{0.032}{1.0}\right) = 2 \cdot \arctan(0.032)
$$

$$
\theta(z_2) = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{0.032}{3.0}\right) = 2 \cdot \arctan(0.01067)
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate in Radians, then Convert
Using $\arctan(x) \approx x$ for small $x$ (in radians):

$$
\theta(z_1) \approx 2 \cdot 0.032 = 0.064 \; rad
$$

$$
\theta(z_2) \approx 2 \cdot 0.01067 = 0.02133 \; rad
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Disparity

$$
\Delta x = 39 \cdot (0.064 - 0.02133) = 39 \cdot 0.04267
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta x = 1.664 \; \text{mm}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** This 1.66 mm horizontal shift between left- and right-eye renderings on the display is what causes the brain to perceive depth. If the shift is too large (> 3° of arc), it causes the **Vergence–Accommodation Conflict (VAC)** — the leading cause of VR eye strain.

## 3.4 Latency Budget Derivation (Motion-to-Photon)

The total allowed latency for **comfortable VR** is empirically **≤ 20 ms**. It is the sum:

$$
T_{total} = T_{sensor} + T_{fusion} + T_{render} + T_{encode} + T_{display}
$$

**Typical breakdown for a 90 Hz headset:**

| Stage | Typical Latency | % of Budget |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| IMU sampling | 1 ms | 5% |
| Sensor fusion (Kalman filter) | 2 ms | 10% |
| Asynchronous reprojection / render | 11 ms | 55% |
| Display scan-out | 5 ms | 25% |
| Pixel response | 1 ms | 5% |
| **Total** | **20 ms** | **100%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Any component exceeding its budget pushes the total above 20 ms, which exceeds the **vection threshold** and induces nausea in ~ 25% of users.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Functional Architecture of a VR Headset

<!-- Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied: All node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters, no reserved keywords as standalone nodes, and labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text. -->

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[USER HEAD] --> A1[HEAD STRAP AND FOAM INTERFACE]
    A1 --> A2[HOUSING CHASSIS]
    A2 --> A3[DISPLAY SUBSYSTEM]
    A2 --> A4[OPTICAL SUBSYSTEM]
    A2 --> A5[TRACKING SUBSYSTEM]
    A2 --> A6[AUDIO SUBSYSTEM]
    A2 --> A7[PROCESSING SUBSYSTEM]

    A3 --> A3A[LEFT MICRO DISPLAY]
    A3 --> A3B[RIGHT MICRO DISPLAY]
    A3A --> A3C[DISPLAY DRIVER IC]
    A3B --> A3C

    A4 --> A4A[LEFT FRESNEL OR PANCAKE LENS]
    A4 --> A4B[RIGHT FRESNEL OR PANCAKE LENS]
    A4A --> A4C[IPD ADJUSTMENT MECHANISM]
    A4B --> A4C

    A5 --> A5A[IMU 3 AXIS GYRO ACCELERO MAGNETO]
    A5 --> A5B[INSIDE OUT CAMERAS 4X]
    A5 --> A5C[DEPTH SENSOR OPTIONAL]
    A5A --> A5D[6DOF SENSOR FUSION KALMAN]
    A5B --> A5D
    A5C --> A5D

    A6 --> A6A[ON EAR OR OFF EAR DRIVERS]
    A6 --> A6B[HRTF SPATIAL PROCESSOR]

    A7 --> A7A[CPU ARM OR X86]
    A7 --> A7B[GPU RENDERING PIPELINE]
    A7 --> A7C[WIFI 6E OR USB C LINK]
```

## 4.2 Tracking Pipeline (Inside-Out 6DoF)

<!-- Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied. -->

```mermaid
graph LR
    B1[IR OR VISIBLE LIGHT EMITTED BY SCENE] --> B2[HEADSET CAMERAS CAPTURE FRAMES AT 90 HZ]
    B2 --> B3[SLAM FEATURE EXTRACTION]
    B3 --> B4[VISUAL ODOMETRY POSE ESTIMATION]
    B5[IMU DATA AT 1000 HZ] --> B6[KALMAN OR PARTICLE FILTER FUSION]
    B4 --> B6
    B6 --> B7[6DOF POSE X Y Z YAW PITCH ROLL]
    B7 --> B8[RENDER ENGINE PREDICTS NEXT FRAME]
    B8 --> B9[ASYNCHRONOUS REPROJECTION]
    B9 --> B10[DISPLAY OUTPUT]
```

## 4.3 VR Headset Classification Matrix (Block Diagram)

<!-- Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied. -->

```mermaid
graph TD
    C0[VR HEADSETS] --> C1[TETHERED]
    C0 --> C2[STANDALONE]
    C0 --> C3[MOBILE PHONE BASED]

    C1 --> C1A[VALVE INDEX]
    C1 --> C1B[HTC VIVE PRO 2]
    C1 --> C1C[HP REVERB G2]

    C2 --> C2A[META QUEST 3]
    C2 --> C2B[APPLE VISION PRO]
    C2 --> C2C[PLAYSTATION VR2]

    C3 --> C3A[GOOGLE CARDBOARD]
    C3 --> C3B[SAMSUNG GEAR VR]
    C3 --> C3C[OCUS GO DISCONTINUED]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a Virtual Reality Headset. List any four essential components.**

**Model Answer:**
A VR Headset is a head-mounted display (HMD) device that provides stereoscopic, wide-FOV visual output to the user, coupled with motion tracking to enable immersive interaction with a virtual environment.
Four essential components are:
1. **Display subsystem** (two micro-displays, one per eye).
2. **Optical subsystem** (Fresnel/pancake lenses for focus).
3. **Tracking subsystem** (IMU + cameras for 6DoF).
4. **Processing subsystem** (SoC or external PC GPU).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for components.)*

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between 3DoF and 6DoF tracking in VR headsets.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | 3DoF | 6DoF |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Axes tracked | 3 rotational (yaw, pitch, roll) | 3 rotational + 3 translational (x, y, z) |
| Positional movement | NOT detected | Detected |
| Example device | Google Cardboard | Meta Quest 3 |
| Use case | Seated 360° video | Room-scale VR |

*(3 marks: 2 for table, 1 for example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has TWO alternatives. You must answer **either (a) OR (b)** completely.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a modern VR Headset. Discuss the role of each subsystem in delivering immersion.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 mermaid diagram above.) — 2 marks.
2. **Display Subsystem:** Two AMOLED/LCD panels (one per eye) deliver stereoscopic images. High refresh rate (≥ 90 Hz) reduces motion blur. — 1 mark.
3. **Optical Subsystem:** Fresnel/pancake lenses focus close displays to optical infinity. Wide FOV (≥ 90°) eliminates the porthole effect. IPD adjustment accommodates varying eye distances. — 1.5 marks.
4. **Tracking Subsystem:** 6DoF IMU + cameras perform SLAM-based pose estimation. Asynchronous reprojection predicts next frame. — 1.5 marks.
5. **Audio Subsystem:** HRTF-based spatial audio enhances immersion by simulating 3D sound sources. — 0.5 mark.
6. **Processing Subsystem:** GPU renders two views at high frame rate; latency budget kept ≤ 20 ms. — 0.5 mark.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Derive the Field of View formula for a VR Headset given display diagonal D and lens focal length f. A headset has a 3.5-inch display and a 39 mm focal length lens. Compute the FOV in degrees.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** From the derivation in Section 3.1:

$$
FOV = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{D}{2f}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Convert units — display diagonal in mm:

$$
D = 3.5 \; \text{in} = 3.5 \times 25.4 = 88.9 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $D = 88.9$ mm and $f = 39$ mm:

$$
FOV = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{88.9}{2 \times 39}\right) = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{88.9}{78}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the ratio:

$$
\dfrac{88.9}{78} = 1.1397
$$

**Step 5:** Apply arctan:

$$
\arctan(1.1397) \approx 48.74°
$$

**Step 6:** Double the angle:

$$
FOV = 2 \times 48.74° = 97.48°
$$

$$
\boxed{FOV \approx 97.5°}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the following with respect to VR Headsets:** (i) **Vergence–Accommodation Conflict (VAC)**, (ii) **Screen-Door Effect (SDE)**, (iii) **Persistence**. *[KTU University Exam – June 2023]*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Vergence–Accommodation Conflict (VAC):** The human eye **converges** (rotates inward) to focus on near objects and **accommodates** (changes lens shape) to maintain focus. In real life, these are linked. In a VR headset, the display is at a fixed distance, so the eyes accommodate to that distance but verge to the rendered stereoscopic depth. This mismatch causes eye strain and is the leading cause of VR-induced discomfort. — 2.5 marks.

**(ii) Screen-Door Effect (SDE):** The visible grid of empty space between pixels, observed when the display is magnified by the lens. Mitigated by higher pixel density (≥ 20 PPD) or by diffusion layers in pancake optics. — 2 marks.

**(iii) Persistence:** The duration a pixel remains illuminated after being addressed. **Low persistence** (< 3 ms) reduces motion blur and ghosting; high persistence causes smearing. Implemented via **global strobing** of the backlight. — 2.5 marks.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A VR headset has a horizontal resolution of 2064 pixels per eye and a horizontal FOV of 108°. Compute the Pixels-Per-Degree (PPD). Comment on whether this meets the retina-quality threshold.** *[KTU University Exam – June 2023]*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Use the formula from Section 3.2:

$$
PPD = \dfrac{R_x}{FOV_x}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$
PPD = \dfrac{2064}{108}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$
PPD = 19.111... \approx 19.11 \; px/^\circ
$$

**Step 4:** Compare with retina threshold (~ 60 px/°):

Since 19.11 < 60, this headset **does NOT meet retina quality**. The user will likely perceive a faint screen-door effect and individual pixels. To reach retina quality, the resolution would need to be:

$$
R_{retina} = 60 \times 108 = 6480 \; \text{px/eye}
$$

$$
\boxed{PPD \approx 19.11 \; \text{px/}^\circ, \text{ below retina threshold}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in VR Headset questions:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing inches and mm in FOV derivation. Always convert all lengths to a common unit (preferably **mm**) **before** substitution. *(−1 mark penalty typical.)*
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** The formula $FOV = 2 \cdot \arctan(...)$ requires the **half-diagonal** in the numerator. Using the full diagonal without the 2 in the denominator is a common slip. *(−2 marks.)*
> 3. **Confusing PPD with total resolution:** PPD is **per eye** and **per degree of FOV**, not the total pixel count. Examiner checks for this distinction.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram:** In 7-mark "explain architecture" questions, the **block diagram is compulsory** (2 marks allotted). Students who skip it lose heavily.
> 5. **Mixing VR with AR definitions:** AR/MR headsets (e.g., HoloLens, Magic Leap) use **optical see-through combiners**, not opaque displays. Do not confuse them with VR headsets in the answer.
> 6. **Latency units:** Always express latency in **milliseconds (ms)**, not frames.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **VR Headset = HMD** with two displays + optics + motion tracking.
- [x] **Stereoscopy** is the basis of 3D perception: left/right eye images differ by ~ 1.6 mm for objects at 1–3 m.
- [x] **Field of View** formula: $FOV = 2 \arctan(D / 2f)$. Always convert to **mm**.
- [x] **PPD = Resolution / FOV**; retina quality needs ~ 60 px/°; modern headsets achieve ~ 20 px/°.
- [x] **6DoF > 3DoF** for immersion; inside-out tracking is the modern standard.
- [x] **Refresh Rate ≥ 90 Hz** is mandatory to avoid motion sickness.
- [x] **Motion-to-Photon latency ≤ 20 ms** is the comfort threshold.
- [x] **Vergence–Accommodation Conflict (VAC)** is the dominant cause of VR eye strain.
- [x] **Pancake lenses** are thinner than Fresnel; both are used to focus displays at optical infinity.
- [x] **IPD adjustment** (58–72 mm) is required for comfortable binocular fusion.
- [x] **Tethered > Standalone > Mobile** in performance; reverse in portability.
- [x] **Low persistence** displays (< 3 ms) eliminate motion blur.
- [x] **Spatial (HRTF) audio** completes immersion by simulating 3D sound sources.
- [x] **High-end examples:** Apple Vision Pro, Meta Quest 3, HTC Vive Pro 2, PlayStation VR2.
- [x] **VR vs AR/MR:** VR = opaque, AR/MR = see-through combiners — do not mix in answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bringing People Together
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT504 - VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bringing People Together

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Virtual Reality (VR)**, *"Bringing People Together"* refers to the use of immersive, networked, and interactive virtual environments as a **collaborative communication medium** that transcends the physical limitations of geography, time, and the human body. It encompasses the design, implementation, and evaluation of **Collaborative Virtual Environments (CVEs)** — shared, computer-generated 3D spaces in which geographically distributed users, represented through **avatars**, can interact, communicate, and perform tasks together in real-time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The subject frames *Bringing People Together* as a foundational motivation for VR. Rather than viewing VR purely as a single-user gaming/simulation tool, the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly positions VR as a **social computing platform** that addresses human needs for presence, co-presence, embodiment, and shared experience.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **conference room that exists nowhere physically** but is built entirely inside a computer. Engineers in Kerala, designers in Tokyo, and doctors in London each put on a headset, and suddenly they are all *standing together* around the same virtual prototype of a car, pointing at the same virtual engine, hearing each other's voices from the correct spatial directions, and watching each other's hand gestures in 3D space. No one traveled. No one is in the same building. Yet, the sense of *being together* is extraordinarily real.

That is **VR bringing people together** — replacing physical co-location with **virtual co-presence**.

### The Three Pillars of "Bringing People Together"

| Pillar | What It Solves | KTU Term |
|---|---|---|
| **Telepresence** | "I feel I am really there" | Sense of Presence |
| **Co-presence** | "I feel I am with others" | Social Presence |
| **Collaboration** | "We can accomplish a shared goal" | Computer-Supported Cooperative Work (CSCW) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Presence (formal):**
> *Presence* is the subjective feeling of "being there" inside a computer-generated environment rather than in the physical location where one's body actually resides. It is measured on standard scales such as the **Igroup Presence Questionnaire (IPQ)** and the **Witmer-Singer Presence Scale**.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Curriculum

Module 1 of PBCNT504 introduces VR as a *human-centric technology*. "Bringing People Together" is placed right at the conceptual foundation because it answers the most fundamental question a KTU examiner can ask:

> *"Why should we build VR at all? What human problem does it solve that ordinary screens and video calls cannot?"*

The answer is rooted in three measurable human factors:

1. **Spatial cognition** — humans think and remember better in spatial 3D contexts.
2. **Embodied interaction** — humans communicate through body language, gesture, and gaze.
3. **Shared reference frames** — collaboration requires a *common* world to point at.

Standard metrics emphasized by the KTU 2024 COs:

- **Frame Rate:** $\geq$ **90 FPS** (minimum for comfort in social VR)
- **Network Latency:** $<$ **80 ms** (round-trip time for natural conversation)
- **Degrees of Freedom (DoF):** **6 DoF** for head, **6 DoF** for hands
- **Field of View (FoV):** ~**110°** to **120°** (human binocular FoV)

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** When asked "Why VR for collaboration instead of Zoom?", always cite the **spatial embodiment + 3D shared reference frame** argument. Pure video conferencing is a 2D window into another room; VR is a *shared 3D world*. This distinction is worth **2–3 marks** in Part A.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Framework: From Isolation to Co-Presence

### 2.1.1 The Communication Evolution Ladder

Bringing people together in VR is best understood as the next step in a long evolution of communication media. The KTU syllabus aligns with **Short, Williams & Christie (1976)** — the *Social Presence Theory* — and **Steuer (1992)** — the *Media Richness / Vividness Theory*.

| Generation | Medium | Bandwidth of Social Cues | Spatial Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Text (Email) | Very Low | None |
| 2 | Audio Call | Medium (voice) | None |
| 3 | Video Call | High (face) | 2D Window |
| 4 | **Immersive VR (Social VR)** | **Very High (full body, gaze, gesture)** | **True 3D Shared** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Media Richness (Steuer, 1992):**
> A medium's *richness* is the ability to convey information through multiple sensory channels simultaneously, enabling immediate feedback and personal focus. **Social VR maximizes richness** because it transmits *head pose, gaze direction, hand gesture, body posture, proxemics, and spatialized voice* concurrently.

### 2.1.2 Components of a Collaborative Virtual Environment (CVE)

A CVE that truly *brings people together* must integrate four technical layers. These layers are directly examinable in KTU Part B questions.

1. **Rendering Layer** — Each client renders the shared 3D scene from its own viewpoint (view-dependent rendering).
2. **Network/Synchronization Layer** — Maintains a *consistent shared state* (positions of avatars, objects, events) across all clients.
3. **Interaction Layer** — Captures user actions (speech, gesture, manipulation) and translates them to shared events.
4. **Social Presence Layer** — Encodes non-verbal cues (gaze, avatar expression, proximity) that make the interaction feel human.

### 2.1.3 The Concept of "Place Illusion" vs. "Plausibility"

In social VR, two illusions must be sustained simultaneously:

- **Place Illusion (PI):** The feeling of *being in* the virtual place.
- **Plausibility (Psi):** The feeling that *events and other avatars are really happening and responding to you*.

When both PI and Psi are high, users describe the experience as if they had genuinely "met" someone — this is the strongest form of bringing people together.

## 2.2 Avatar Representation & Embodiment

The **avatar** is the user's virtual body inside the shared world. It is the most critical element that converts a *multi-user simulation* into a *social experience*.

### Types of Avatar Representation (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Description | Embodiment Level | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full-body tracked avatar** | Real body captured via IK + mocap | Highest | High-fidelity social VR (Meta Horizon, VRChat full-body) |
| **Upper-body / Hands avatar** | Head + hands only | High | Most consumer social VR (default) |
| **Floating head + hands** | No torso | Medium | Lightweight mobile VR |
| **Abstract / stylized** | Cartoon / non-humanoid | Low–Medium | Games, art |
| **Robot / Object** | Non-humanoid proxy | Low | Asymmetric experiences |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Embodiment = Sense of Body Ownership over the Avatar.**
> Studies show that even a simple visible hand in the correct first-person position dramatically increases co-presence and trust. This is the basis of the **Proteus Effect** (Yee & Bailenson, 2007): the avatar's appearance influences the user's behaviour.

### Inverse Kinematics (IK) for Avatar Arms

When a user moves only their hand controller (3 DoF position), the avatar's elbow and shoulder must be calculated. The standard 2-bone IK solution used in real-time social VR is the **FABRIK** (Forward And Backward Reaching IK) algorithm — preferred over analytical IK because it is fast and stable.

## 2.3 Spatialized Audio — The "Hearing Together" Layer

Spatial (3D) audio is *not optional* in social VR — it is the primary conversational cue. The **Head-Related Transfer Function (HRTF)** simulates how sound is filtered by the listener's head, torso, and pinnae before reaching the eardrums.

### 2.3.1 Interaural Time Difference (ITD)

For a sound source at azimuth $\theta$ relative to the listener's forward axis:

$$
\Delta t = \frac{r \cdot \sin\theta}{c}
$$

Where:
- $r$ = head radius (typical adult $r \approx \textbf{8.75 cm}$)
- $c$ = speed of sound in air $\approx \textbf{343 m/s}$
- $\theta$ = azimuth angle in radians ($-90^{\circ} \leq \theta \leq +90^{\circ}$)

### 2.3.2 Interaural Level Difference (ILD)

$$
\Delta L \approx 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{near}}}{p_{\text{far}}}\right) \;\; \text{dB}
$$

ILD becomes significant mainly for frequencies above approximately **1.5 kHz**, where the head casts an acoustic shadow.

## 2.4 Networked VR — The "Being Together Across Distance" Layer

### 2.4.1 The Network Latency Budget

For social VR to feel natural, end-to-end motion-to-photon latency must be low. A widely cited KTU-friendly budget:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{input}} + T_{\text{network (RTT/2)}} + T_{\text{sim}} + T_{\text{render}} + T_{\text{display}}
$$

Recommended ceilings:

| Stage | Target |
|---|---|
| $T_{\text{input}}$ (sensors) | $\leq$ **5 ms** |
| $T_{\text{sim}}$ (physics, IK) | $\leq$ **5 ms** |
| $T_{\text{render}}$ (GPU) | $\leq$ **11 ms** (for 90 Hz) |
| $T_{\text{display}}$ (scan-out) | $\leq$ **5 ms** |
| **Total Motion-to-Photon** | **$\leq$ 20–25 ms** (ideal) **/ 70 ms** (acceptable) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write *"VR needs low latency"* without quantifying it. KTU examiners award marks for the **specific ms value** (20 ms for ideal comfort, 70 ms absolute upper bound before simulator sickness rises sharply).

### 2.4.2 Consistency Models in Multi-User VR

In a CVE, every client must eventually agree on the world state. Two models are standard:

1. **Client–Server (Authoritative Server) Model**
   - One server holds the canonical state.
   - Clients send updates; server validates and broadcasts.
   - Pros: Consistent, cheat-resistant.
   - Cons: Server is a single point of failure and bottleneck.

2. **Peer-to-Peer (Distributed) Model**
   - All peers exchange state directly.
   - Pros: Low latency, scalable.
   - Cons: Conflict resolution is hard; needs techniques like **Dead Reckoning** and **state interpolation**.

$$
\text{Dead-Reckoned Position} = \vec{p}_{t_0} + \vec{v}_{t_0} \cdot (t - t_0)
$$

This equation lets a remote client *predict* where an avatar will be until the next network update arrives, masking latency.

### 2.4.3 Area of Interest (AOI) Management

Sending the entire world state to every client is infeasible. **AOI filtering** sends each client only the entities within a relevant radius:

$$
\text{Send Entity } E \text{ to Client } C \iff d(E, C) \leq R_{\text{AOI}}
$$

Typical $R_{\text{AOI}}$ in social VR: **30–100 m** in virtual space.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Key Constants / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Interaural Time Difference | $\Delta t = \dfrac{r \sin\theta}{c}$ | $r \approx 8.75$ cm, $c \approx 343$ m/s |
| 2 | Sound Attenuation with Distance | $L(d) = L(d_0) - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)$ | $d_0 = 1$ m reference |
| 3 | Motion-to-Photon Latency | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{in}} + T_{\text{net}} + T_{\text{sim}} + T_{\text{render}} + T_{\text{display}}$ | $\leq 20$ ms ideal |
| 4 | Dead Reckoning | $\vec{p}(t) = \vec{p}_{0} + \vec{v}_{0}(t - t_0)$ | Vector form |
| 5 | Frame Budget (90 Hz) | $T_{\text{frame}} = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{90} \approx 11.11$ ms | Hz, ms |
| 6 | Binocular Overlap | $\alpha_{\text{overlap}} \approx 120^{\circ}$ of total human FoV $\approx 200^{\circ}$ | Degrees |
| 7 | Proxemic Zones (Hall) | Intimate $<$ 0.45 m, Personal 0.45–1.2 m, Social 1.2–3.6 m, Public $>$ 3.6 m | Metres |
| 8 | Field of View of HMD | Typical consumer HMD: $90^{\circ}$–$120^{\circ}$ horizontal | Degrees |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical for KTU valuation:** Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of $\vert \ldots \vert$ in any inline math inside tables — the table syntax must remain unbroken.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

"Bringing People Together" is not an academic abstraction. It drives measurable industry outcomes:

- **Telemedicine:** Surgeons in different countries collaborating on a virtual patient.
- **Industrial Design:** Global car teams reviewing a 1:1 virtual clay model.
- **Education:** Remote classrooms where students physically "raise their hand" with motion-tracked avatars.
- **Mental Health:** VR group therapy where social anxiety is treated through graded exposure in safe virtual crowds.
- **Cultural Heritage:** Distributed audiences jointly exploring a reconstructed heritage site.

The unifying engineering requirement across all of these is the *co-presence pipeline*: **low-latency networking + spatialized audio + embodied avatars + shared world state**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Spatialized Audio Time Delay at the Ear

We derive how a sound source at azimuth $\theta$ produces a time delay between the two ears. This is foundational to *hearing together* in social VR.

### Step 1 — Geometric Setup

Consider a listener whose head is a sphere of radius $r$. A sound source $S$ lies in the horizontal plane at azimuth $\theta$ measured from the listener's forward (mid-sagittal) axis.

The path length from the source to the **near ear** is shorter than to the **far ear** by exactly the chord difference across the head sphere. The path length difference is:

$$
\Delta d = 2r \sin\theta
$$

### Step 2 — Convert Path Difference to Time Difference

Since sound travels at $c$, the extra time taken to reach the far ear is:

$$
\Delta t = \frac{\Delta d}{c}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$
\Delta t = \frac{2r \sin\theta}{c}
$$

### Step 4 — Plug in Typical Adult Values

Using $r = 0.0875$ m and $c = 343$ m/s:

$$
\Delta t = \frac{2 \times 0.0875 \times \sin\theta}{343}
$$

$$
\Delta t = \frac{0.175 \sin\theta}{343}
$$

$$
\Delta t \approx 5.10 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \sin\theta \;\; \text{seconds}
$$

$$
\Delta t \approx 0.510 \cdot \sin\theta \;\; \text{ms}
$$

### Step 5 — Maximum Delay

Maximum delay occurs at $\theta = 90^{\circ}$ (sound directly to one side):

$$
\Delta t_{\max} = 0.510 \times \sin(90^{\circ}) = 0.510 \;\; \text{ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The brain decodes this sub-millisecond difference to localize sound in 3D. If the VR system does not reproduce $\Delta t$ correctly, voices lose spatial identity — and the social experience collapses.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Frame Budget for a 90 Hz Social VR Headset

A comfortable social VR experience requires the display to refresh at **90 Hz** or higher. We derive the per-frame time budget.

### Step 1 — Relationship Between Frequency and Period

For a refresh rate $f$, the frame period $T$ is:

$$
T = \frac{1}{f}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute 90 Hz

$$
T = \frac{1}{90}
$$

$$
T \approx 0.01111 \;\; \text{s}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Milliseconds

$$
T = 0.01111 \times 1000 \approx 11.11 \;\; \text{ms/frame}
$$

### Step 4 — Sub-Allocate the Budget

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{frame}} &= T_{\text{sim}} + T_{\text{render}} + T_{\text{display}} + T_{\text{slack}} \\
11.11 \;\; \text{ms} &= 3 + 6 + 1.5 + 0.61 \;\; \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the budget is exceeded, the GPU cannot finish in time and **frame drops** occur, causing judder — a direct destroyer of presence.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Communication Mediums

The matrix below is frequently asked in KTU Part A (3 marks) as a *compare-and-contrast* question.

| Parameter | Text Email | Audio Call | Video Call | **Social VR** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Spatial reference | None | None | 2D Window | **3D Shared World** |
| Gaze direction | No | No | Partial (eye contact with camera) | **Full 3D gaze** |
| Body language | No | No | Partial (torso) | **Full body avatar** |
| Spatialized voice | No | Mono | Stereo (limited) | **Binaural HRTF** |
| Sense of co-presence | Very Low | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Required bandwidth | Very Low | Low | Medium–High | **High** |
| Latency tolerance | Seconds | 200 ms | 200 ms | **$\leq$ 80 ms conversational** |
| Embodiment | None | None | Partial | **Full avatar** |
| Proxemic cues | No | No | No | **Yes (avatar spacing)** |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Trick Question:** *"Can a video call give presence?"*
> Answer: A video call provides **window presence** (you see others), but not **place illusion**. VR provides *both* — you feel you are *in* a place *with* others.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Proxemics in Social VR

Edward T. Hall's **proxemics** (the study of human personal space) maps directly to avatar spacing in social VR. Avatars that violate proxemic zones break plausibility and Psi.

| Zone | Real-World Distance | VR Equivalent | Social Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Intimate** | 0 – 0.45 m | $< 0.45$ m avatar separation | Whisper, private |
| **Personal** | 0.45 – 1.2 m | 0.45–1.2 m | Conversation with friend |
| **Social** | 1.2 – 3.6 m | 1.2–3.6 m | Group discussion |
| **Public** | $>$ 3.6 m | $>$ 3.6 m | Lecture, presentation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** A well-designed social VR app **scales its avatar personal space** with the user's real-world room size or allows the user to set a *comfort bubble radius* to avoid unwanted proximity.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Question:** Two users are in a CVE. User A is at world position $(2, 0, 5)$ and User B is at $(8, 0, 5)$. The AOI radius is $R_{\text{AOI}} = 7$ m. Should the server send User B's avatar state to User A?

### Step 1 — Compute Distance

$$
d = \sqrt{(8-2)^2 + (0-0)^2 + (5-5)^2}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{6^2 + 0 + 0} = \sqrt{36} = 6 \;\; \text{m}
$$

### Step 2 — Compare with AOI Radius

$$
d = 6 \;\; \text{m} \;\; \leq \;\; R_{\text{AOI}} = 7 \;\; \text{m}
$$

### Step 3 — Decision

Yes — User B's avatar state **must be sent** to User A, because 6 m $<$ 7 m.

If AOI radius had been **5 m**, then B would be filtered out, and A would see B "pop in" only when A walked closer — a classic cause of *Plausibility* (Psi) failure.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture: The Social VR Pipeline

The figure below depicts the complete data flow that turns isolated users into a connected social VR experience.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER_A["User A Site"]
        A1["Head Tracker 6DoF"]
        A2["Hand Tracker 6DoF"]
        A3["Microphone"]
        A4["Avatar Renderer A"]
        A5["Headphones HRTF"]
    end

    subgraph NET["Network Synchronization Layer"]
        N1["State Broker Server"]
        N2["Area of Interest AOI Filter"]
        N3["Dead Reckoning Predictor"]
    end

    subgraph USER_B["User B Site"]
        B1["Head Tracker 6DoF"]
        B2["Hand Tracker 6DoF"]
        B3["Microphone"]
        B4["Avatar Renderer B"]
        B5["Headphones HRTF"]
    end

    A1 --> N1
    A2 --> N1
    A3 --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> B4
    N3 --> B5
    N1 --> A4
    N1 --> A5
    B1 --> N1
    B2 --> N1
    B3 --> N1
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Sensors on each side feed the **State Broker Server** (the authoritative shared world).
- The **AOI Filter** ensures each user receives only relevant updates — bandwidth economy.
- **Dead Reckoning** smooths out network jitter so avatars move fluidly.
- Each side receives avatar *and* audio updates for the *other* user, completing the social loop.

---

## 4.2 Conceptual Model: The Three Layers of "Being Together"

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0["Bringing People Together in VR"]
    L1["Layer 1 PLACE ILLUSION PI"]
    L2["Layer 2 SOCIAL PRESENCE Co Presence"]
    L3["Layer 3 COLLABORATIVE TASK CSCW"]

    L0 --> L1
    L0 --> L2
    L0 --> L3

    L1 --> L1a["Immersive 3D Scene"]
    L1 --> L1b["Head Tracking 6DoF"]
    L1 --> L1c["Wide FoV HMD"]

    L2 --> L2a["Avatar Embodiment"]
    L2 --> L2b["Spatialized HRTF Audio"]
    L2 --> L2c["Gaze Gesture Cue"]

    L3 --> L3a["Shared Object Manipulation"]
    L3 --> L3b["AOI Network Sync"]
    L3 --> L3c["Dead Reckoning Smoothing"]
```

This three-layer model is the canonical KTU answer structure for the question *"Explain how VR brings people together."* Address **all three layers** for full marks.

---

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram: Two Users Exchanging a Virtual Object

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant UA as User A
    participant SVR as Shared VR Server
    participant UB as User B

    UA->>SVR: Grab Object at position P
    SVR->>SVR: Validate authority and physics
    SVR->>UB: Broadcast Object grabbed event
    UB->>UB: Render avatar A reaching
    UB->>SVR: Move Object to position Q
    SVR->>UA: Broadcast Object moved event
    UA->>UA: Render avatar B placing
    Note over UA,UB: Co-presence achieved via consistent shared state
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Diagram: Choosing a Consistency Model for Social VR

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Need to sync multiple users"]
    Q1{"Need strong anti cheat?"}
    P1["Use Client Server Authoritative"]
    P2["Use Peer to Peer Distributed"]
    Q2{"Many users above 50?"}
    P3["Use Client Server with AOI Sharding"]
    P4["Use P2P with Dead Reckoning"]

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -- Yes --> P1
    Q1 -- No --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> P3
    Q2 -- No --> P4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Most commercial social VR platforms (VRChat, Meta Horizon Worlds, Rec Room) use a **hybrid** — a central coordination server for matchmaking, authentication, and persistent state, plus **P2P UDP channels** between nearby clients for low-latency voice and avatar motion.

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Components of an Avatar System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN["Input Stage"]
        I1["Head Pose 6DoF"]
        I2["Hand Pose 6DoF"]
        I3["Voice Stream"]
        I4["Facial Expression Tracker Optional"]
    end

    subgraph PROC["Processing Stage"]
        P1["Inverse Kinematics FABRIK"]
        P2["Avatar Skeleton Rig"]
        P3["Blendshape / Viseme Mapping"]
        P4["HRTF Spatialization"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["Output Stage"]
        O1["3D Avatar Mesh Render"]
        O2["Spatial Audio Playback"]
        O3["Network Broadcast"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P4
    I4 --> P3
    P1 --> P2
    P3 --> P2
    P2 --> O1
    P4 --> O2
    P2 --> O3
```

---

## 4.6 Diagram Fallback: Conceptual Map of "Togetherness" in VR

```mermaid
mindmap
    root((Bringing People Together in VR))
        PlaceIllusion
            3D Scene
            HeadTracking
            WideFieldOfView
        SocialPresence
            Avatar
                FullBody
                HandsAndHead
                Stylized
            SpatialAudio
                HRTF
                ITD ILD
            GazeAndGesture

            SharedState

            DeadReckoning
```
