```mermaid
            CulturalHeritage
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define *Place Illusion* and *Plausibility* as introduced by Mel Slater. Why are both essential for "bringing people together" in VR?

**Model Answer:**

- **Place Illusion (PI):** The strong sense of *being there* in the virtual environment, even though your body is physically elsewhere. It is achieved primarily through immersive display, wide field of view, accurate head tracking (6DoF), and consistent spatial audio.
- **Plausibility (Psi):** The sense that *the events happening inside the virtual environment are really occurring and are responsive to the user*. For example, another avatar turning to look at you when you speak creates plausibility.

**Why both are essential for togetherness:**

- PI alone gives you a beautiful empty world — no social meaning.
- Psi alone gives you believable events without a sense of place — you feel manipulated, not present.
- Together, PI + Psi let a user feel *"I am really in this place, and these other people are really here with me."* This co-presence is the defining feature of social VR. Without both, the system degrades to either an empty simulation (only PI) or a video-game-like narrative (only Psi).

**(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` What is the Interaural Time Difference (ITD)? Compute the ITD for a sound source at azimuth $\theta = 60^{\circ}$ for a typical adult head.

**Model Answer:**

ITD is the difference in arrival time of a sound wave at the two ears due to the path-length difference around the head.

$$
\Delta t = \frac{2r \sin\theta}{c}
$$

For $r = 0.0875$ m, $c = 343$ m/s, and $\theta = 60^{\circ}$:

$$
\Delta t = \frac{2 \times 0.0875 \times \sin 60^{\circ}}{343}
$$

$$
\Delta t = \frac{0.175 \times 0.866}{343}
$$

$$
\Delta t \approx \frac{0.1516}{343} \approx 4.42 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta t \approx 0.44 \;\; \text{ms}}
$$

This sub-millisecond delay is what the human auditory system decodes to localize sound in 3D. In social VR, the HRTF filter synthesizes this ITD for each audio source so that voices appear to come from the correct avatar mouth.

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer ONE full question out of the two alternatives offered from the module.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A. (a)** Explain the concept of **Collaborative Virtual Environments (CVEs)**. With a neat block diagram, describe the four functional layers of a CVE that enable people in different physical locations to feel "together."

**Model Answer:**

A **Collaborative Virtual Environment (CVE)** is a computer-generated, persistent 3D space in which multiple geographically distributed users, represented as avatars, can interact in real time for a shared purpose — learning, designing, training, or socializing.

**Four Functional Layers of a CVE:**

1. **Rendering Layer** — Each client GPU renders the shared 3D scene from its own viewpoint. Uses view-dependent rendering and stereo (left/right eye) projection for HMDs.
2. **Network/Synchronization Layer** — Maintains the *consistent shared state* (positions, rotations, object states, events) using either client-server or P2P models, with AOI filtering and dead reckoning.
3. **Interaction Layer** — Captures user inputs (6DoF head, 6DoF hands, voice, gesture) and translates them into shared events via input dispatching and Inverse Kinematics (IK).
4. **Social Presence Layer** — Encodes and renders non-verbal cues: avatar facial expression, gaze direction, body posture, proxemic spacing, and spatialized (HRTF) audio. This is what makes interaction *feel human*.

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 — Social VR Pipeline):** The diagram shows User A's sensors → State Broker → AOI Filter → Dead Reckoning → User B's renderer and audio, with bidirectional flow.

**Why these layers together produce "togetherness":**

- Rendering alone gives each user only their own world — no sharing.
- The network layer provides a *common world state* — necessary but invisible to users.
- The interaction layer turns isolated inputs into shared actions.
- The social presence layer translates data into the *feeling* of being with others.

**(7 marks)**

**A. (b)** Discuss the role of **avatars** in social VR. Compare at least three avatar representation styles and explain how the **Proteus Effect** influences collaborative behaviour.

**Model Answer:**

An **avatar** is the user's virtual embodiment inside the CVE. It is the conduit through which presence, identity, gesture, and emotion are conveyed to other users. Without avatars, the CVE is an empty 3D scene — users cannot identify, locate, or socially respond to one another.

**Three Avatar Representation Styles (compared):**

| Style | Embodiment | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full-body tracked | Highest | Real gesture, gaze, posture | Expensive hardware, bandwidth heavy |
| Hands + head | High | Affordable, expressive enough for most social cues | No leg/body language |
| Abstract / stylized | Low–Medium | Creative, identity-flexible | Reduced co-presence, weak Psi |

**Proteus Effect (Yee & Bailenson, 2007):**

The Proteus Effect states that a user's behaviour is influenced by the *appearance* of their avatar. Examples:

- Users given **tall avatars** negotiate more confidently than those given short avatars in the same CVE.
- Users with **attractive avatars** maintain greater interpersonal distance from strangers, mirroring real-world proxemic norms.
- Users embodying **doctor avatars** in VR medical training exhibit more confident clinical decisions than those with patient avatars.

**Implications for Bringing People Together:**

- Avatars are not just cosmetic — they are *behavioural levers*. A well-designed CVE lets users choose avatars that support the intended collaborative tone.
- Identity mismatches (e.g., cartoon avatar in a serious telemedicine session) can erode plausibility and trust.

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**B. (a)** With suitable diagrams, explain the **client-server** and **peer-to-peer** consistency models used in multi-user VR. Which model is preferred for large-scale social VR, and why?

**Model Answer:**

In a multi-user VR system, every client must eventually agree on the *shared state* — the list of avatars, objects, and events. Two principal models exist.

**1. Client–Server (Authoritative Server) Model:**

- A central server holds the **canonical** state of the world.
- Each client sends its local updates (head pose, hand pose, voice, object manipulations) to the server.
- The server validates, integrates, and broadcasts the updated state back to all clients.

**Diagram (from Section 4.4)**: Three clients connected bidirectionally to a single authoritative server.

**Advantages:** Consistency, easier cheat detection, simpler conflict resolution.
**Disadvantages:** Single point of failure, server bandwidth bottleneck, higher worst-case latency.

**2. Peer-to-Peer (Distributed) Model:**

- Every client exchanges state directly with every other client (or with nearby peers).
- No central authority; each client maintains its own world state.

**Diagram (from Section 4.4)**: Clients forming a mesh with peer-to-peer links.

**Advantages:** Lowest latency for nearby users, naturally scalable.
**Disadvantages:** Conflict resolution is hard, requires Dead Reckoning and state interpolation, vulnerable to cheating.

**Preferred Model for Large-Scale Social VR:**

A **hybrid** model — a central server handles matchmaking, authentication, and persistent storage; **P2P UDP channels** between nearby clients carry low-latency voice and avatar motion. This is the architecture used by VRChat, Meta Horizon Worlds, and Rec Room. The server provides *eventual consistency and persistence*, while P2P provides *real-time smoothness*.

**(7 marks)**

**B. (b)** Define **motion-to-photon latency**. Derive the frame budget for a 90 Hz HMD and discuss why latency is critical to social VR.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Motion-to-photon latency is the elapsed time between a user moving their head and the HMD displaying the updated image corresponding to that movement. It is the single most important performance metric for VR comfort.

**Pipeline stages:**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{input}} + T_{\text{network}} + T_{\text{sim}} + T_{\text{render}} + T_{\text{display}}
$$

**Frame Budget Derivation for 90 Hz:**

The frame period is:

$$
T_{\text{frame}} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{90} \approx 11.11 \;\; \text{ms}
$$

Sub-allocated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{sim}} &\leq 3 \;\; \text{ms} \\
T_{\text{render}} &\leq 6 \;\; \text{ms} \\
T_{\text{display}} &\leq 1.5 \;\; \text{ms} \\
T_{\text{slack}} &\leq 0.61 \;\; \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the budget is exceeded, **frame drops** occur, the head-pose-to-image feedback loop desynchronizes, and the user experiences **simulator sickness** (nausea, eye strain).

**Why latency is critical to social VR specifically:**

1. **Conversational turn-taking** depends on hearing a reply within ~200 ms. Network round-trip above ~80 ms makes conversations feel awkward.
2. **Avatar mirroring** — when User A waves and User B sees the wave late, A feels *not seen* and co-presence collapses.
3. **Spatial audio direction** — at high latency, the perceived source of a voice drifts relative to the avatar's mouth.
4. **Hand-object contact** — manipulating a shared virtual tool feels rubbery if latency exceeds ~50 ms.

Hence the KTU-emphasized ceilings:

- **$\leq$ 20 ms** motion-to-photon (ideal comfort)
- **$\leq$ 70 ms** (acceptable upper bound)
- **$\leq$ 80 ms** conversational RTT for natural dialogue

**(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks on "Bringing People Together":**
>
> 1. **Conflating "presence" and "co-presence"** — they are not synonyms. *Presence* is being in the world; *co-presence* is being with others. Many students use only "presence" and lose **2 marks**.
> 2. **Omitting HRTF / spatial audio** — examiners specifically look for HRTF, ITD, and ILD when discussing why social VR feels real. Writing only "good graphics" = **0 marks** for the audio sub-part.
> 3. **No numerical latency value** — saying "low latency is important" without giving **20 ms / 70 ms / 80 ms** loses the quantitative marks.
> 4. **Forgetting AOI and Dead Reckoning** in the network architecture — these are explicitly in the KTU Module 1 syllabus and frequently tested.
> 5. **Missing the Proteus Effect** when discussing avatars — it is the most cited social-VR theory and a guaranteed 2-mark item.
> 6. **Confusing PI and Psi** — Mel Slater's distinction is examinable. Always define both.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **"Bringing People Together"** = using VR as a social/collaborative medium that creates **co-presence** across distance.
- **Three pillars:** Telepresence, Co-presence, Collaboration (CSCW).
- **Two illusions:** **Place Illusion (PI)** + **Plausibility (Psi)** — both required.
- **Avatar** = user's virtual body; embodiment level directly determines social presence.
- **Proteus Effect:** avatar appearance shapes user behaviour.
- **HRTF** enables spatialized audio; key cues are **ITD** ($\Delta t = 2r\sin\theta / c$) and **ILD** (above ~1.5 kHz).
- **Motion-to-photon latency budget:** $\leq$ **20 ms** ideal, $\leq$ **70 ms** acceptable.
- **Conversational RTT:** $\leq$ **80 ms** for natural turn-taking.
- **Network models:** Client–Server (consistent, central) vs P2P (low-latency, distributed); modern systems use a **hybrid**.
- **AOI filtering:** each client receives only nearby entities within radius $R_{\text{AOI}}$ (typically 30–100 m).
- **Dead Reckoning:** $\vec{p}(t) = \vec{p}_0 + \vec{v}_0(t - t_0)$ masks latency between updates.
- **Frame budget at 90 Hz:** ~**11.11 ms/frame**, sub-allocated across simulation, render, and display.
- **Proxemic zones (Hall):** Intimate $<$ 0.45 m, Personal 0.45–1.2 m, Social 1.2–3.6 m, Public $>$ 3.6 m — apply to avatar spacing.
- **Standard metrics to memorize:** $r \approx 8.75$ cm, $c \approx 343$ m/s, HMD FoV $90^{\circ}$–$120^{\circ}$, refresh $\geq 90$ Hz, **6 DoF** head and hands.
- **Applications:** Telemedicine, remote industrial design, virtual classrooms, mental-health group therapy, cultural heritage.
- **Engineering rule:** the *co-presence pipeline* = **embodied avatar + spatialized audio + low-latency network + shared world state** — all four are necessary, none alone is sufficient.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtual Reality Systems - Hardware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT504 - VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Virtual Reality Systems - Hardware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Virtual Reality (VR) Hardware** refers to the physical, electro-mechanical, and opto-electronic components that collectively capture user input, compute the virtual environment, and stimulate the human sensory channels (primarily visual, auditory, and haptic) to produce a convincing, immersive, and interactive synthetic experience. The hardware stack is responsible for enforcing the three pillars of VR: **Immersion**, **Presence**, and **Interactivity**.

In the KTU 2024 framework, VR hardware is classified into four functional sub-systems:

1. **Sensor Sub-System (Input)** — Captures user pose, gesture, gaze, biometrics, and motion.
2. **Processor / Rendering Sub-System (Compute)** — Generates the virtual scene at real-time frame budgets.
3. **Display Sub-System (Output – Visual)** — Presents stereoscopic imagery close to the eye via HMDs, CAVEs, or wall projectors.
4. **Interaction Sub-System (Output – Haptic/Auditory)** — Delivers force feedback, vibration, spatial audio, and locomotion interfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Terminology:** The KTU syllabus explicitly uses the term **"VR System Hardware"** to denote Head-Mounted Displays (HMDs), Tracking Systems, Input Devices, and Graphics Rendering Pipelines. A 14-mark question may directly ask students to *"Explain the architecture of a VR system with a block diagram"* — always draw the **Input → Compute → Output** flow.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **scuba diver descending into the ocean**. The diver is fully surrounded by water, fish, and currents. The diver is *not* looking at a TV screen showing the ocean — the diver is *inside* the ocean.

A **VR headset does the same thing to your eyes and ears**:

- The **lenses** act like a **scuba mask**, placing screens extremely close to your eyes.
- The **headphones** act like the water — they fill your ears with directional sound.
- The **motion trackers** act like the diver's wetsuit sensors — they tell the system "the diver just turned left," so the virtual ocean rotates right.
- The **GPU** acts like the **ocean itself** — it constantly generates new water, fish, and bubbles at 90 times per second.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simplified Plain-English Definition:** VR hardware is anything you wear, hold, or sit in front of that fools your brain into thinking you are somewhere else.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Industry Standards)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Field of View | FOV | 90° – 210° | degrees |
| Refresh Rate | $f$ | 60 – 120 | Hz |
| Interpupillary Distance | IPD | 54 – 74 | mm |
| Pixel Density | PPD | ≥ 25 (Retina) | pixels/degree |
| Motion-to-Photon Latency | $L$ | ≤ 20 | ms |
| Positional Tracking Accuracy | $\sigma_p$ | ≤ 1 | mm |
| Rotational Tracking Drift | $\sigma_r$ | ≤ 0.1 | °/min |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold Constants to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Threshold for VR Comfort:** Motion-to-Photon Latency $\le$ **20 ms**
> - **Minimum VR Refresh Rate:** **90 Hz**
> - **Recommended FOV:** **110°** (Human binocular vision)
> - **Standard IPD:** **63.5 mm** (average adult)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Component-Wise Functional Breakdown

#### A. Head-Mounted Display (HMD) — The Visual Engine
- **Two micro-displays** (LCD/OLED/MicroLED) placed ~**3–5 cm** from each eye.
- **Fresnel or Pancake lenses** focus the screen image onto the retina, making it appear distant.
- **Binocular disparity** is achieved by rendering two offset images (Left Eye / Right Eye) with parallax matching the IPD.
- **IPD Adjustment Mechanism** physically slides the lens array to match the user's pupil spacing.

#### B. Tracking Sub-System
- **Outside-In Tracking:** External cameras (e.g., SteamVR Lighthouse base stations) emit IR lasers; HMD photodiodes triangulate 6-DoF pose.
- **Inside-Out Tracking:** Cameras mounted on the HMD observe the room's features using SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping).
- **Degrees of Freedom (DoF):**
  - **3-DoF:** Rotation only (yaw, pitch, roll) — *Google Cardboard*
  - **6-DoF:** Rotation + Translation (X, Y, Z) — *Meta Quest, HTC Vive*

#### C. Input / Interaction Devices
- **Hand Controllers** (capacitive touch, IMU, IR LEDs).
- **Data Gloves** (flex sensors, IMU per finger, haptic actuators).
- **3D Pointers / Wands**.
- **Haptic Suits / Gloves** (vibrotactile, force-feedback exoskeleton).

#### D. Graphics Rendering Pipeline (Compute Sub-System)
The render path must satisfy a strict **20 ms motion-to-photon budget**:

| Pipeline Stage | Time Budget |
|---|---|
| Tracking + Pose Update | 1 ms |
| Scene Graph Update | 2 ms |
| CPU Game Logic / Physics | 4 ms |
| GPU Rendering (2 views × reprojection) | 10 ms |
| Display Scan-out | 3 ms |
| **Total** | **20 ms** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 20 ms?** The human vestibular-ocular reflex (VOR) tolerates latency below **20 ms** without inducing motion sickness. Above 20 ms, the brain detects the mismatch between visual motion and inner-ear motion, triggering nausea.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Binocular Field of View | $FOV_{total} = FOV_{mono,L} + FOV_{mono,R} - \theta_{overlap}$ | $\theta$ in degrees |
| 2 | Stereo Overlap | $\theta_{overlap} = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{IPD/2}{d_{lens}}\right)$ | IPD, $d_{lens}$ in mm |
| 3 | Required Frame Rate | $f_{min} = \dfrac{1000}{L_{budget}}$ | $f$ in Hz, $L$ in ms |
| 4 | Pixels-Per-Degree (PPD) | $PPD = \dfrac{N_{horizontal}}{FOV_{horizontal}}$ | pixels / degree |
| 5 | Angular Resolution | $\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{p}{d_{eye}}\right)$ | $\alpha$ in degrees, $p$ = pixel pitch (mm) |
| 6 | Vergence–Accommodation Conflict | $\Delta_{VAC} = \vert d_{virtual} - d_{focus} \vert$ | $d$ in meters |
| 7 | Tracking Pose Update (6-DoF) | $P_t = \bigl[T_t \;\vert\; R_t\bigr] \in SE(3)$ | $T$ = translation, $R$ = rotation |
| 8 | Lighthouse Sweep Angle | $\theta(t) = \omega \cdot t$ | $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 9 | Latency Budget Allocation | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i \le L_{total}$ | All $L$ in ms |
| 10 | VRAM Throughput | $BW = W \cdot H \cdot bpp \cdot f \cdot 2$ | $BW$ in MB/s; factor 2 = stereo |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formula #10 is used in KTU numerical problems. For a 4K-per-eye HMD at 90 Hz with 24-bit color, you can calculate the minimum VRAM bandwidth required by the GPU.

### 2.3 Why Each Metric Matters (Engineering Context)

- **FOV** determines *immersion* — narrow FOV (like 60°) feels like looking through binoculars, breaking presence.
- **Refresh Rate** determines *smoothness* — below 90 Hz, the brain perceives flicker, causing eye-strain.
- **Latency** determines *comfort* — high latency causes VR sickness (cybersickness).
- **PPD** determines *clarity* — the "Retina threshold" is **~60 PPD** (Apple Vision Pro target).
- **6-DoF Tracking** determines *realism* — 3-DoF feels "swivel-chair" limited.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Motion-to-Photon Latency Budget

The total motion-to-photon latency $L_{total}$ is the sum of all sub-system delays from physical user motion to a photon leaving the display panel.

$$
L_{total} = L_{sensor} + L_{compute} + L_{transmit} + L_{scanout}
$$

**Step 1 — Sensor Polling Delay ($L_{sensor}$):**
The IMU samples at a fixed frequency $f_s$. The worst-case wait before the next sample is one full period.

$$
L_{sensor} = \frac{1}{2 f_s}
$$

*Explanation:* A 1000 Hz IMU yields $L_{sensor} = 0.5$ ms (we average the half-period to account for asynchronous polling).

**Step 2 — Compute Delay ($L_{compute}$):**
Spent in game logic + GPU rasterization.

$$
L_{compute} = T_{CPU} + T_{GPU}
$$

**Step 3 — Transmission Delay ($L_{transmit}$):**
HDMI 2.1 / DisplayPort transport over the HMD cable.

$$
L_{transmit} = \frac{N_{bytes}}{R_{link}}
$$

where $R_{link}$ is link bandwidth (e.g., 48 Gbps for HDMI 2.1).

**Step 4 — Scan-out Delay ($L_{scanout}$):**
The display panel writes pixels line-by-line from top to bottom.

$$
L_{scanout} = \frac{1}{f_{display}} = \frac{1}{90\,\text{Hz}} \approx 11.1\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Combining the Budget:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= L_{sensor} + L_{compute} + L_{transmit} + L_{scanout} \\
&= 0.5 + 6 + 1.5 + 11.1 \\
&= 19.1\,\text{ms} \;\le\; 20\,\text{ms} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked *"What is the minimum frame rate to keep latency below 20 ms?"* — immediately write $f_{min} = 1000 / 20 = 50$ Hz minimum at the scan-out level. But **90 Hz** is the industry standard because the GPU needs at least 10 ms to render, leaving a sub-20 ms total.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Required GPU VRAM Bandwidth (Exhaustive)

**Problem:** A VR headset has a resolution of $2160 \times 2160$ per eye, 24-bit color depth, and runs at 90 Hz. Compute the minimum VRAM bandwidth.

**Given Data:**
- $W = 2160$ pixels
- $H = 2160$ pixels
- $bpp = 24$ bits = 3 bytes
- $f = 90$ Hz
- Stereo factor = 2 (two eyes)

**Step 1 — Bytes per single frame for one eye:**

$$
B_{frame} = W \times H \times bpp = 2160 \times 2160 \times 3
$$

$$
B_{frame} = 4,665,600 \times 3 = 13{,}996{,}800\,\text{bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to megabytes:**

$$
B_{frame} = 13{,}996{,}800 \div (1024 \times 1024) \approx 13.35\,\text{MB}
$$

**Step 3 — Per second (90 Hz):**

$$
B_{sec} = 13.35 \times 90 \approx 1201.5\,\text{MB/s per eye}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply stereo factor of 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW_{total} &= 2 \times B_{sec} \\
&= 2 \times 1201.5 \\
&= 2403\,\text{MB/s} \\
&\approx 2.4\,\text{GB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Gbps for engineering reporting:**

$$
BW_{total} = 2403 \times 8 = 19{,}224\,\text{Mbps} \approx 19.2\,\text{Gbps}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** This VR system requires a minimum VRAM bandwidth of **~2.4 GB/s** (≈ 19.2 Gbps). Most modern GDDR6X GPUs (e.g., RTX 4070) provide > 500 GB/s, so this is easily met.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Lighthouse Tracking Angle

The SteamVR Lighthouse uses a rotating IR laser on a base station. The angle of the laser at time $t$ is:

$$
\theta(t) = \omega t
$$

For a 60 Hz rotation at 1800 RPM:

$$
\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 60 = 376.99\,\text{rad/s}
$$

**Time for a single sweep:**

$$
T_{sweep} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{60} = 16.67\,\text{ms}
$$

**Angular resolution** (sensitivity of photodiode) when the laser crosses one photodiode in 100 $\mu$s:

$$
\Delta\theta = \omega \cdot \Delta t = 376.99 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0377\,\text{rad} \approx 2.16^{\circ}
$$

This is the angular subtense the photodiode can resolve.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 VR System Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete flow of a VR hardware system from physical user input to visual stimulation of the retina.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[USER PHYSICAL MOTION] --> B[SENSOR SUBSYSTEM]
    B1[IMU 9 Axis] --> B
    B2[IR Photodiodes] --> B
    B3[Camera SLAM] --> B
    B --> C[POSE ESTIMATION MODULE]
    C --> D[CPU GAME LOGIC AND PHYSICS]
    D --> E[GPU RENDER PIPELINE]
    E --> E1[Left View Pass]
    E --> E2[Right View Pass]
    E1 --> F[FRAME COMPOSITOR]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[DISPLAY TRANSPORT HDMI DP]
    G --> H[HMD MICRODISPLAYS]
    H --> I[OPTICAL LENSES]
    I --> J[USER RETINA]
    K[CONTROLLER INPUT] --> D
    L[HAPTIC ACTUATORS] --> A
    M[SPATIAL AUDIO DSP] --> N[HEADPHONES]
```

### 4.2 HMD Internal Optical Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Microdisplay Left] --> L1[Pancake Lens L]
    S2[Microdisplay Right] --> L2[Pancake Lens R]
    L1 --> E1[Left Eye Pupil]
    L2 --> E2[Right Eye Pupil]
    E1 --> R1[Left Retina]
    E2 --> R2[Right Retina]
    H[IPD Slider Mechanism] --> L1
    H --> L2
    T[Tracking Rigid Body IR LEDs] --> TS[External Base Station]
    TS --> T
```

### 4.3 Tracking System Comparison Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TS[TRACKING TECHNOLOGIES]
        A1[Outside In IR] --> A2[SteamVR Lighthouse]
        A1 --> A3[Constellation Cameras]
        B1[Inside Out Visual SLAM] --> B2[Meta Quest 3]
        B1 --> B3[Apple Vision Pro]
        C1[Magnetic] --> C2[Polhemus]
        C1 --> C3[STEM]
    end
```

### 4.4 Latency Budget Allocation (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[User Motion] -->|0.5 ms| S[IMU Sensor]
    S -->|2 ms| CPU[CPU Logic]
    CPU -->|6 ms| GPU[GPU Render]
    GPU -->|1.5 ms| TX[Display Transport]
    TX -->|10 ms| SC[Display Scanout]
    SC --> RET[Photon to Retina]
    RET -.->|Total 20 ms| P
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Hint:** For a *"With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a VR system"* question, draw **Diagram 4.1** as the primary answer. Add **Diagram 4.4** as a sub-illustration if the question is specifically about latency.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three tracking technologies used in VR systems and state the principle of operation of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Mechanical Tracking** (1 mark) — Uses joint encoders and mechanical linkages (e.g., BOOM display) to directly measure the joint angles, from which the head pose is computed via forward kinematics.

2. **Electromagnetic Tracking** (1 mark) — A transmitter coil generates a known magnetic field; receiver coils on the HMD sense the field strength in three orthogonal axes, which is used to compute 6-DoF pose via the *Aurora* algorithm.

3. **Optical Tracking (Outside-In)** (1 mark) — IR LEDs on the HMD are observed by multiple fixed cameras; the 2D pixel positions in each camera are triangulated to recover the 3D pose (e.g., Vicon, OptiTrack).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*What is meant by Interpupillary Distance (IPD) in an HMD, and why is IPD adjustment important?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** IPD is the horizontal distance between the centers of the pupils of the two eyes, typically **54–74 mm** in adults (average **63.5 mm**).
- **Importance – Optical (1 mark):** The lens centers must align with the pupil centers; misalignment causes eye-strain, blur, and headaches.
- **Importance – Stereo (1 mark):** Correct IPD ensures the rendered stereoscopic disparity matches the user's actual eye separation, preserving correct depth perception. Mismatched IPD is the leading cause of the **Vergence–Accommodation Conflict (VAC)**.

---

### Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical Virtual Reality system. Describe the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A VR system consists of four inter-connected sub-systems that form a closed real-time loop:

**Block 1 — Sensor / Input Subsystem (2 marks):**
Captures the user's physical motion and commands. Includes:
- *Inertial Measurement Units (IMU):* gyroscope, accelerometer, magnetometer — high-frequency 1000 Hz sampling for rotation.
- *Optical Sensors:* IR photodiodes (Lighthouse) or cameras (inside-out SLAM) for 6-DoF pose.
- *Active Input Devices:* hand controllers, data gloves, voice mics.

The output is a 6-DoF pose vector $P_t = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$ in the world frame.

**Block 2 — Compute / Processing Subsystem (2 marks):**
Receives the pose vector and runs:
- *Pose Filtering* (e.g., Madgwick or Kalman filter) to fuse IMU + optical data.
- *CPU Stage:* game logic, AI, collision detection, physics — typically 4–6 ms.
- *GPU Stage:* renders two views (stereo) with lens-distortion pre-correction — typically 6–10 ms.
- *Asynchronous Reprojection / Timewarp* is applied to minimize perceived latency.

**Block 3 — Display / Output Subsystem (1.5 marks):**
- *Micro-displays:* LCD / OLED / MicroLED panels, typically 2K × 2K per eye.
- *Optical Lenses:* Fresnel or Pancake, providing FOV of 90° – 120°.
- *Scanout:* the rendered frame is transmitted via HDMI 2.1 / DisplayPort and written to the panel.
- The *Image Signal Processor (ISP)* may apply barrel distortion correction so the lens produces a geometrically correct final image.

**Block 4 — Interaction / Haptic Subsystem (1.5 marks):**
- *Haptic Actuators:* LRA / ERM motors in controllers for vibrotactile feedback.
- *Force-Feedback Gloves / Suits:* provide resistance.
- *Spatial Audio:* HRTF-based 3D audio delivered via headphones or bone-conduction transducers.
- *Locomotion Interfaces:* treadmills, omnidirectional treadmills for natural walking in VR.

The diagram drawn is the one in **Section 4.1** above.

**(b)** Explain the *Motion-to-Photon Latency* pipeline in a VR system. Compute the total latency if sensor delay = 0.5 ms, CPU = 4 ms, GPU = 6 ms, transmission = 1.5 ms, and scanout = 11.1 ms. Is the system within the safe VR comfort budget? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**
Motion-to-Photon Latency is the time elapsed between the *physical motion* of the user's head and the *photon emission* from the display that reflects the new pose. It traverses the following chain:

$$
L_{total} = L_{sensor} + L_{CPU} + L_{GPU} + L_{TX} + L_{scanout}
$$

- $L_{sensor}$: IMU polling delay; depends on sampling rate.
- $L_{CPU}$: game-logic and physics step.
- $L_{GPU}$: rasterization of two stereo views.
- $L_{TX}$: encode + transport over cable.
- $L_{scanout}$: pixel line-by-line write to display.

**Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= 0.5 + 4 + 6 + 1.5 + 11.1 \\
&= 23.1\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with safe budget:**

- Safe threshold = **20 ms**
- Computed $L_{total}$ = **23.1 ms**
- Conclusion: The system **EXCEEDS** the safe budget by **3.1 ms**, which would likely induce VR sickness.

**Remediation (carry-forward for full marks):**
- Enable *Asynchronous Spacewarp (ASW)* to reproject the previous frame.
- Use a higher refresh-rate display (120 Hz → scanout = 8.3 ms, saving ~2.8 ms).
- Optimize GPU shaders or reduce poly count.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **Head-Mounted Display (HMD)**. With a neat diagram, describe the optical path from the microdisplay to the retina. What is the role of the IPD adjustment mechanism? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working of HMD (3 marks):**
An HMD is a wearable display that places one or two micro-displays directly in front of the user's eyes. It consists of:
- A *housing/strap* for mounting on the head.
- *Two micro-displays* (one per eye, or one shared display split by optics).
- *Lens assemblies* (Fresnel or Pancake) that focus the close-up image to appear at optical infinity.
- *IPD adjustment slider* to align lens centers with user pupils.
- *Integrated IMU* (gyro + accelerometer) for rotation tracking.
- *Stereoscopic rendering*: GPU outputs two slightly offset views (corresponding to the eye separation), and the brain fuses them into a 3D percept.

**Optical Path (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 diagram)*
- Light from the *microdisplay* (typically 2 inches diagonal, placed 3–5 cm from the eye) passes through the *collimating lens* (Fresnel/Pancake).
- The lens bends divergent rays into near-parallel rays, so the eye perceives the image as *optically distant* (e.g., 2 meters away), even though the source is 3 cm away.
- The rays enter the *cornea → pupil → lens → retina*, where the *fovea* forms a sharp focus.
- *Distortion correction* is applied in software to counteract the pincushion distortion introduced by the lens, so the final retinal image is rectilinear.

**Role of IPD Adjustment (2 marks):**
- The IPD slider physically moves the lens + display assembly laterally to match the user's pupil spacing.
- *Optical alignment* prevents vignetting, blur, and double-image formation.
- *Stereo alignment* ensures that the rendered disparity matches the actual eye baseline, preserving accurate *binocular depth cues*.
- Incorrect IPD forces the user to *verge* (rotate eyes inward/outward) to a non-physiological angle, causing fatigue and amplifying the *Vergence–Accommodation Conflict*.

**(b)** Differentiate between **3-DoF** and **6-DoF** tracking. Explain *Outside-In* and *Inside-Out* tracking with examples. A lighthouse system has two base stations rotating at 60 Hz. Compute the angular sweep time and the angular subtense per 100 $\mu$s of photodiode exposure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**3-DoF vs 6-DoF (2 marks):**

| Aspect | 3-DoF | 6-DoF |
|---|---|---|
| Tracking | Rotation only (yaw, pitch, roll) | Rotation + Translation (X, Y, Z) |
| Sensor | IMU (gyro/accel) | IMU + optical/camera |
| Translation | Not tracked | Tracked |
| Example | Google Cardboard, Gear VR | Meta Quest 3, HTC Vive Pro |

**Outside-In Tracking (1.5 marks):**
- External sensors (cameras / IR base stations) are placed *in the room*.
- Markers on the HMD are observed by the external sensors; pose is computed via triangulation.
- *Examples:* SteamVR Lighthouse (rotating IR lasers + photodiodes), Vicon (multi-camera IR), OptiTrack.

**Inside-Out Tracking (1.5 marks):**
- Cameras are mounted *on the HMD itself*.
- The cameras observe static features in the environment; SLAM algorithms build a map and estimate the HMD's pose relative to it.
- *Examples:* Meta Quest 3, Apple Vision Pro, HoloLens 2, Magic Leap.

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given: $f = 60$ Hz, photodiode exposure $\Delta t = 100\,\mu s$.

**Sweep time:**

$$
T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{60} = 16.67\,\text{ms per rotation}
$$

**Angular velocity:**

$$
\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 60 = 376.99\,\text{rad/s}
$$

**Angular subtense for 100 $\mu$s:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\theta &= \omega \cdot \Delta t \\
&= 376.99 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 0.0377\,\text{rad} \\
&= 0.0377 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 2.16^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse rotation DOF with positional DOF.** 3-DoF means *only three rotational axes* are tracked; the user cannot lean or walk. Many students write "3-DoF means three sensors" — this is WRONG.
> 2. **In numerical problems, do not forget the factor 2 (stereo)** when computing VRAM bandwidth for an HMD with two displays. Omitting the stereo factor is the #1 cause of lost marks in this topic.
> 3. **In IPD-related answers,** always state the *average value 63.5 mm* explicitly. Writing "around 60 mm" is too vague for KTU valuation.
> 4. **In latency problems,** remember the **20 ms comfort threshold** is from *photon emission* to *retinal response*, but the budget you compute is from *user motion* to *photon emission*. They are different — do not confuse.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ VR Hardware = Sensor + Compute + Display + Haptic/Audio sub-systems forming a **real-time closed loop**.
- ✅ **HMD** = Two micro-displays + Lenses + IPD slider + IMU + Housing.
- ✅ **IPD** average = **63.5 mm**; slider aligns optical + stereo axes.
- ✅ **FOV** target = **≥ 110°** for human-binocular-equivalent immersion.
- ✅ **Refresh Rate** ≥ **90 Hz**; below 60 Hz causes visible flicker.
- ✅ **Motion-to-Photon Latency** must be ≤ **20 ms**; formula = $L_{sensor} + L_{CPU} + L_{GPU} + L_{TX} + L_{scanout}$.
- ✅ **3-DoF** = rotation only; **6-DoF** = rotation + translation.
- ✅ **Outside-In** = external sensors (Lighthouse); **Inside-Out** = on-HMD cameras (Quest).
- ✅ **Vergence–Accommodation Conflict (VAC)** is the leading visual fatigue cause in stereoscopic HMDs.
- ✅ **VRAM Bandwidth** for stereo = $BW = W \cdot H \cdot bpp \cdot f \cdot 2$.
- ✅ **Pancake lenses** are thinner than Fresnel but have lower light transmission.
- ✅ **PPD ≥ 25** is the consumer-grade clarity threshold; **PPD ≥ 60** is the retina-grade target.
- ✅ **Asynchronous Timewarp / Reprojection** is the standard software technique to reduce perceived latency when GPU misses a frame.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT504 - VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software (in the context of Virtual Reality):** The complete stack of programs, libraries, run-time engines, drivers, and toolkits that drive the creation, management, simulation, rendering, and interaction of immersive 3D virtual environments. VR software spans **four canonical layers**:
> 1. **System Software** (Operating Systems, HAL, Device Drivers for HMDs, trackers, haptic gloves)
> 2. **Development Frameworks / Engines** (Unity, Unreal Engine, WebXR, OpenXR)
> 3. **Middleware / Libraries** (OpenGL, Vulkan, DirectX, OpenXR Runtime, SteamVR, ALSA)
> 4. **Application Software** (End-user VR experiences, simulations, training modules, games)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBCNT504 – Module 1), the term **"Software"** specifically refers to the **graphics rendering pipeline, scene-graph architectures, software toolkits, and run-time engines** that allow 3D virtual worlds to be created, displayed, and updated at the high frame rates (typically **90 Hz, 120 Hz**) demanded by VR head-mounted displays.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: VR Software = The "Invisible Director" of a Stage Play**
> 
> Imagine a theatrical performance. The **actors (3D models)** are visible, the **stage (rendered scene)** is visible, but everything you actually *see* and *interact* with is controlled by an **invisible director (the software)** standing behind the curtain.
> 
> This director:
> - Tells the **lighting crew (rendering pipeline)** how to illuminate every polygon.
> - Updates the **stagehands (tracking system drivers)** whenever the lead actor (your head) moves an inch.
> - Coordinates with the **sound team (audio engine)** to spatialize 3D sound.
> - Sends **stage directions (scene graph traversal)** to all background characters.
> 
> Without this director, nothing happens — even if the actors and stage are perfectly built. **VR Software IS that director.**

## 1.3 Key Software Components (Syllabus Mapping)

| Layer | Function | Typical Frame Rate | Latency Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Device Drivers** | Interface with HMD, controllers, trackers | Hardware-limited | < 1 ms |
| **Operating System** | Process scheduling, memory mgmt | – | < 1 ms |
| **Rendering Engine** | Rasterization, lighting, shading | **90 FPS** | < 11 ms / frame |
| **Physics Engine** | Collision, rigid body simulation | 60–90 Hz | < 16 ms |
| **Audio Engine** | 3D spatial audio | 44.1 / 48 kHz | < 20 ms |
| **Tracking Middleware** | Sensor fusion, pose prediction | **1000 Hz** | < 1 ms |
| **Application Layer** | User logic, AI, scripting | Variable | – |

## 1.4 Why Software is Critical in VR (The 3 Pillars)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a VR system to be **"believable"** to the human brain, software must satisfy three simultaneous constraints (often called the **VR Trinity**):
> 1. **High Frame Rate** (≥ 90 FPS) to avoid motion sickness.
> 2. **Low Motion-to-Photon Latency** (≤ 20 ms, ideally < 15 ms) — the delay between user action and visible screen update.
> 3. **Correct Stereoscopic Rendering** — two slightly offset views, one per eye, with proper **IPD (Inter-Pupillary Distance)** support.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 3D Graphics Rendering Pipeline (Software View)

The **rendering pipeline** is the heart of VR software. It converts 3D geometric data (vertices, polygons) into 2D images for each eye. It proceeds in **seven distinct stages**:

### Stage 1 — Application Stage (CPU-side)
- **Scene-graph traversal.**
- **Frustum culling:** Eliminates objects outside the viewing volume.
- **Level-of-Detail (LOD) selection:** Chooses a low-poly model for distant objects.
- **Input handling:** Head pose, controller pose.
- Runs in the **C++ game logic** layer (e.g., Unity MonoBehaviour / UE Actor Tick).

### Stage 2 — Geometry Processing
- **Vertex Shader** (Programmable): Transforms 3D world coordinates → 2D screen coordinates using the projection matrix.
- **Primitive Assembly:** Groups vertices into triangles/lines.
- **Clipping & Culling:** Discards primitives outside the view volume.

### Stage 3 — Rasterization
- Converts triangles into **fragments** (candidate pixels).
- **Interpolates** per-vertex attributes (color, normal, UV) across each fragment.

### Stage 4 — Fragment / Pixel Processing
- **Fragment Shader (Programmable):** Computes the final color of each fragment using lighting, textures, shadows.
- **Per-fragment tests:** Depth test, stencil test, alpha blending.

### Stage 5 — Output Merger
- Combines fragment color with framebuffer.
- Performs **stereoscopic duplication** for VR (renders twice — once per eye — with different view matrices).

### Stage 6 — Post-Processing (VR-Critical)
- **Lens Distortion Correction** (Barrel/Pincushion compensation).
- **Chromatic Aberration Correction.**
- **Timewarp** (Asynchronous/Spacewarp) — re-projects the image using the latest head pose to mask latency.
- **Anti-Aliasing (MSAA, FXAA, TAA).**

### Stage 7 — Display Submission
- Frame is queued to the **HMD display driver.**
- Synchronization via **V-Sync** or **adaptive sync** (FreeSync, G-Sync).

## 2.2 The Scene Graph (Hierarchical Software Data Structure)

> [!NOTE]
> A **Scene Graph** is a tree data structure used by VR engines to organize all entities in a virtual world. Each node (called a **Node** or **GameObject** in Unity, **Actor** in Unreal) holds:
> - A **Transform** (Position, Rotation, Scale) — relative to its parent.
> - Optional **Mesh / Geometry.**
> - Optional **Behavior / Script.**
> - **Children Nodes** (sub-tree).

**Why a tree?** Because real and virtual objects have a natural **parent-child hierarchy**: a *hand* is attached to an *arm*, which is attached to a *body*, which is attached to a *torso*. Moving the parent moves all children automatically — this is the principle of **hierarchical transformation composition.**

## 2.3 Stereoscopic Rendering — The "Two-View" Problem

VR requires **two distinct views** rendered from slightly different camera positions (separated by the **IPD ≈ 64 mm**). Two strategies exist:

| Strategy | Description | Performance | Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Multi-Pass Stereo** | Renders the scene twice (left + right) | Slow (2× cost) | High |
| **Instanced Stereo (Single-Pass Instanced)** | Renders both views in a single draw call using GPU instancing | Fast (~1.1× cost) | Slight artifacts |
| **Multi-View / Single-Pass Stereo (OVR_multiview)** | GPU extension rendering both layers in one pass | Fastest | Excellent on mobile/Quest |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save these formulas — they appear frequently in KTU 14-mark and competitive exam questions.**

$$
\text{Motion-to-Photon Latency} = T_{\text{sensor}} + T_{\text{fusion}} + T_{\text{render}} + T_{\text{post}} + T_{\text{display}}
$$

$$
\text{Frame Budget (ms)} = \frac{1000}{\text{Target FPS}} = \frac{1000}{90} \approx 11.11 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\text{Field of View (Horizontal)} = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{W}{2f}\right)
$$

where $W$ = display width and $f$ = focal length.

$$
\text{Parallax (Disparity)} = \frac{\text{IPD} \cdot Z}{f}
$$

where $Z$ = depth of the virtual object and $f$ = focal length.

$$
\text{Throughput (vertices/sec)} = \frac{\text{Triangles per frame} \times \text{FPS}}{10^6} \quad [\text{MTri/s}]
$$

$$
\text{Asynchronous Timewarp gain} = 1 - \frac{\text{Render time}}{\text{Frame budget}}
$$

$$
\text{IPD}_{\text{typical}} = 64 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(range 54–74 mm)}
$$

$$
\text{Target VR Latency} \leq 20 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{ideal} < 15 \text{ ms})
$$

$$
\text{Stereoscopic Render Cost} = \text{Mono Cost} \times \text{Eye Multiplier} \quad (\text{multi-pass} \approx 2.0,\ \text{instanced} \approx 1.1)
$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Aerospace / Defense:** VR software simulators (e.g., Lockheed Martin Prepar3D, Bohemia Interactive VBS) use the same pipeline for pilot training.
> - **Medical Surgery:** Software like Surgical Theater or FundamentalVR uses stereoscopic rendering for pre-operative planning.
> - **Automotive Design:** Ford, BMW use VR software (Unreal Engine + VR headsets) for design reviews in CAVE-like systems.
> - **Education:** Labster, Google Expeditions rely on Unity/Unreal for immersive chemistry and history lessons.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Frame Budget for 90 FPS VR

We derive the **per-frame time budget** available to the rendering pipeline when the HMD is configured for 90 Hz refresh.

**Given:**
- Target refresh rate $R = 90\ \text{Hz}$
- One second = 1000 ms

**Step 1 — Relate frequency to period.**
The time available per frame (the period $T$) is the reciprocal of the frequency $R$:

$$
T = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{90}\ \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert seconds to milliseconds.**
Multiply by 1000:

$$
T = \frac{1000}{90} = \frac{100}{9}\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Perform the division.**

$$
T = 11.111\ldots\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**
The CPU + GPU combined must complete **all** geometry, rasterization, fragment shading, and post-processing within **11.11 ms**. Otherwise, the GPU will either drop frames (causing judder → motion sickness) or fall back to **Asynchronous Spacewarp (ASW)** which re-projects a previous frame at half the rate.

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{90 Hz}} \approx 11.11\ \text{ms per frame}}
$$

For comparison, the same derivation at 120 Hz:

$$
T_{120} = \frac{1000}{120} = 8.33\ \text{ms}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Field of View from Display Geometry

**Given:** A flat display panel of width $W = 2.0\ \text{inches}$ placed at a focal distance $f = 1.5\ \text{inches}$ from the eye lens.

**Step 1 — Write the geometric relation.**
The horizontal field of view (FoV) of a pinhole-style display is twice the half-angle subtended by the panel at the eye:

$$
\theta_{\text{H}} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{W/2}{f}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $W = 2.0$, $f = 1.5$.**

$$
\theta_{\text{H}} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{1.0}{1.5}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the ratio.**

$$
\frac{1.0}{1.5} = 0.6667
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the arctangent (in degrees).**

$$
\arctan(0.6667) \approx 33.69^\circ
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 2.**

$$
\theta_{\text{H}} \approx 2 \times 33.69^\circ = 67.38^\circ
$$

**Result:** The horizontal FoV of this hypothetical HMD is approximately **67.4°**. (Consumer HMDs like Meta Quest 3 achieve ~110° horizontal; this calculation is for a single lens pair.)

## 3.3 Derivation: Latency Budget Allocation (Motion-to-Photon)

A common KTU question asks to *partition* the **20 ms motion-to-photon budget** among pipeline stages. We assume the following representative allocation used by Oculus/Meta for 90 Hz:

| Stage | Symbol | Typical Time |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor sample | $T_s$ | 1 ms |
| Pose fusion + prediction | $T_f$ | 2 ms |
| Render (CPU+GPU) | $T_r$ | 8.5 ms |
| Timewarp + post-processing | $T_w$ | 1.5 ms |
| Display scan-out | $T_d$ | 7 ms |

**Step 1 — Sum the components.**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_s + T_f + T_r + T_w + T_d
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 1 + 2 + 8.5 + 1.5 + 7
$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 20\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify against target.**

$$
20\ \text{ms} \leq 20\ \text{ms (target)} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Note on display scan-out:** Although display scan-out is 7 ms, **Asynchronous Timewarp (ATW)** masks this by re-projecting the most recent completed frame at the latest known head pose just before the panel scans out. This is why the *worst-case* perceived latency is closer to $T_r + T_w + T_{\text{ATW update}} \approx 11$ ms.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Software Technologies (KTU Expected)

| Software / Library | Category | Role in VR | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unity** | Game Engine | Authoring 3D scenes, scripting in C# | High |
| **Unreal Engine** | Game Engine | High-fidelity photoreal VR, Blueprints/C++ | High |
| **OpenXR** | API Standard | Cross-vendor VR/AR runtime abstraction | High |
| **OpenGL / Vulkan** | Graphics API | Low-level rendering pipeline | Medium |
| **DirectX 11/12** | Graphics API (Windows) | Renders HMD framebuffers (WMR) | Medium |
| **SteamVR** | Runtime | Driver layer for HTC Vive, Valve Index | Medium |
| **Oculus / Meta XR SDK** | Vendor SDK | Quest native, PC tethered | Medium |
| **WebXR** | Web API | Browser-based VR (Chrome, Edge) | Low–Medium |
| **ALSA / FMOD / Wwise** | Audio | 3D spatial audio HRTF | Low |
| **Bullet / PhysX** | Physics | Collision, rigid-body in VR scenes | Low |

## 3.5 Worked Example: Vertex Throughput of a VR Scene

A VR application renders a scene with **1.2 million triangles** at **90 FPS** using single-pass instanced stereo.

**Step 1 — Total triangles per second.**

$$
\text{Tri/s} = 1.2 \times 10^{6} \times 90
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply.**

$$
\text{Tri/s} = 108 \times 10^{6} = 1.08 \times 10^{8}\ \text{triangles/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to M-Tri/s.**

$$
= 108\ \text{MTri/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for instanced stereo (factor ≈ 1.1).**

$$
\text{Effective load} = 108 \times 1.1 \approx 118.8\ \text{MTri/s}
$$

**Interpretation:** A modern mid-range GPU (e.g., RTX 3060) delivers ~10,000+ MTri/s; thus 118 MTri/s is well within capability. However, **fragment shading cost** (per-pixel) usually dominates, not geometry.
