| Symplectic | No | **Yes** | **Yes (time-reversible)** | No | No (but stable) |
| Energy Behavior | Grows | Bounded oscillates | Bounded oscillates | Bounded | Bounded |
| Cost (function evals/step) | 1 | 1 | 1 | 4 | 1 (linear solve) |
| Stability Region | Tiny | Moderate | Moderate | Large | Large (A-stable for $\beta \ge 0.25$) |
| VR Use Case | Teaching | Real-time rigid body | Cloth, particles | Off-line accuracy | Structural sim |
| Stores Velocity? | Yes | Yes | Implicit | Yes | Yes |
| Error Growth | Linear | Linear | Quadratic | Quartic | Quadratic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory aid for exam:** *"Symplectic = Stable for conservative systems."* Symplectic methods preserve a *modified* energy that stays bounded — perfect for VR long-running simulations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End VR Numerical Simulation Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[User Input / HMD Tracking] --> A1[State Initializer]
    A1 --> A2[Current State Sn]
    A2 --> B1[Force Computation]
    B1 --> B2[Collision Detection]
    B2 --> B3[Constraint Solver]
    B3 --> C1[Integrator Selection]
    C1 --> C2[Forward Euler]
    C1 --> C3[Semi Implicit Euler]
    C1 --> C4[Verlet]
    C1 --> C5[RK4]
    C1 --> C6[Newmark Beta]
    C2 --> D1[New State Sn plus 1]
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D1
    C6 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Stability and Error Check]
    E1 --> F1[Send to GPU Renderer]
    F1 --> G1[Display on HMD]
    G1 --> A0
    E1 --> H1{Error exceeds threshold}
    H1 -- Yes --> I1[Substepping or Adaptive dt]
    I1 --> C1
    H1 -- No --> F1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This flowchart shows the canonical real-time VR physics loop. The "Stability and Error Check" block is the *gatekeeper* that decides whether to *sub-step* (split one frame into multiple smaller physics ticks) when the simulation is too aggressive.

## 4.2 Integrator Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Choose Numerical Integrator] --> S1{Is the system conservative}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2{Need real time 60 fps or more}
    S1 -- No --> S3{Need high accuracy}
    S2 -- Yes --> S4[Use Semi Implicit Euler]
    S2 -- No --> S5[Use Verlet]
    S3 -- Yes --> S6[Use RK4]
    S3 -- No --> S7[Use Forward Euler or Newmark Beta]
    S7 --> S8{Stiff structural problem}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Use Newmark Beta implicit]
    S8 -- No --> S10[Use Forward Euler for quick prototypes]
```

## 4.3 Energy Drift Phenomenon (Mermaid Time-Series Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Forward_Euler_Energy
        E1[Time t0: E equals 10] --> E2[Time t1: E equals 10.5]
        E2 --> E3[Time t2: E equals 11.2]
        E3 --> E4[Time t3: E explodes]
    end
    subgraph Verlet_Energy
        V1[Time t0: E equals 10] --> V2[Time t1: E equals 9.95]
        V2 --> V3[Time t2: E equals 10.05]
        V3 --> V4[Time t3: E bounded]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Forward Euler *adds* artificial energy each step, eventually causing the simulation to "explode" (objects flying off-screen). Verlet oscillates around the true value, *bounded* — a hallmark of symplectic-like integrators. **This is a 7-mark KTU favorite.**

## 4.4 Collision Detection Hierarchy (Narrow to Broad Phase)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Broad Phase] --> P2[Axis Aligned Bounding Box AABB]
    P2 --> P3[Oriented Bounding Box OBB]
    P3 --> P4[Sphere Sphere Test]
    P4 --> P5[Narrow Phase]
    P5 --> P6[Separating Axis Theorem SAT]
    P6 --> P7[Triangle Triangle Test]
    P7 --> P8[Contact Point Generation]
    P8 --> P9[Impulse Solver Sequential]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why two phases?** The broad phase is $O(n)$ or $O(n \log n)$ and quickly *culls* non-colliding pairs. The narrow phase is $O(n^2)$ worst case but only runs on surviving pairs — a major VR optimization.

---

## 4.5 Functional Block Architecture of a Real-Time Physics Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U0[VR Application Layer] --> U1[Scene Graph]
    U1 --> U2[Physics World Manager]
    U2 --> U3[Rigid Body Pool]
    U2 --> U4[Soft Body Pool]
    U2 --> U5[Constraint Pool]
    U3 --> U6[Island Generation]
    U4 --> U6
    U5 --> U6
    U6 --> U7[Integration Phase]
    U7 --> U8[Constraint Solver Phase]
    U8 --> U9[Position Projection Phase]
    U9 --> U10[Synchronize to Graphics]
    U10 --> U11[GPU Draw Call]
    U11 --> U12[HMD Display]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** "Island generation" groups *connected* bodies so that independent clusters can be solved in parallel — a major speedup for sparse VR scenes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Numerical Simulation. Why is numerical stability critical in real-time VR applications?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Numerical Simulation is the process of computing the time-evolution of a physical system by solving its governing differential equations using discrete numerical approximations (integrators), since closed-form analytical solutions are generally unavailable for complex multi-body VR scenarios.

**Why stability is critical (1.5 Marks):** VR systems run at high refresh rates (90–120 Hz) with strict frame budgets (8–11 ms). An unstable integrator causes:
1. **Energy explosion** — objects fly off-screen.
2. **Visual jitter** — induces motion sickness in HMD users.
3. **Tunneling** — fast objects pass through walls between frames.
4. **Loss of immersion** — destroys the "presence" feeling central to VR.

A stable integrator (e.g., semi-implicit Euler, Verlet) ensures that simulation errors remain *bounded* over arbitrarily long VR sessions.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Compare Forward Euler and Semi-Implicit (Symplectic) Euler integration schemes with respect to order, energy behavior, and one VR use case each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Forward Euler | Semi-Implicit Euler |
|---|---|---|
| **Order** | First-order $O(\Delta t)$ | First-order $O(\Delta t)$ |
| **Energy Behavior** | Energy *grows* monotonically (unstable) | Energy *bounded* (oscillates) |
| **VR Use Case** | Quick teaching prototypes | Real-time rigid body engines (e.g., PhysX, Bullet default) |

> The semi-implicit variant updates velocity first, then uses the *new* velocity for the position update, giving it a **symplectic property** — energy is conserved on average.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q (a)** With relevant equations and a flow diagram, explain the Verlet integration scheme. Derive the position update formula using Taylor series expansion. Discuss why Verlet is preferred for cloth and particle simulation in VR. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** A virtual ball of mass $m = 2$ kg is dropped from rest at height $h = 5$ m under gravity $g = 9.81$ m/s². Using Forward Euler integration with $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, compute the velocity and position at $t = 0.3$ s. Compare with the analytical solution and find the percentage error. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q (a) — Verlet Integration

**Step 1 — Statement:** Verlet integration is a second-order, time-reversible, position-based numerical integration scheme widely used in cloth, hair, soft body, and particle systems in VR.

**Step 2 — Taylor series expansion forward and backward:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{n+1} &= x_n + v_n \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} a_n (\Delta t)^2 + \tfrac{1}{6} \dddot{x}_n (\Delta t)^3 + O((\Delta t)^4) \\
x_{n-1} &= x_n - v_n \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} a_n (\Delta t)^2 - \tfrac{1}{6} \dddot{x}_n (\Delta t)^3 + O((\Delta t)^4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add both equations (odd terms cancel):**

$$
x_{n+1} + x_{n-1} = 2 x_n + a_n (\Delta t)^2 + O((\Delta t)^4)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x_{n+1}$:**

$$
\boxed{x_{n+1} = 2 x_n - x_{n-1} + a_n (\Delta t)^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Velocity (recovered when needed):**

$$
v_n = \frac{x_{n+1} - x_{n-1}}{2 \Delta t} + O((\Delta t)^2)
$$

**Step 6 — Why Verlet is preferred for cloth/particle VR (Key reasons):**
1. **Time-reversible** — running the simulation backward yields the original trajectory; eliminates energy drift.
2. **Position-only storage** — saves memory in massively parallel cloth (10,000+ particles) and GPU SIMT architectures.
3. **Stable for stiff springs** — works well with mass-spring cloth where forward Euler explodes.
4. **Symplectic-like behavior** — bounded energy oscillation.
5. **Constraint friendly** — position-based dynamics (PBD) builds directly on Verlet by projecting constraints on the post-integration position.

> Flow diagram: *Forces $\to$ Acceleration $\to$ Verlet Update $x_{n+1} = 2x_n - x_{n-1} + a_n \Delta t^2$ $\to$ Constraint Solve (PBD) $\to$ Render*

---

#### Model Solution to Q (b) — Forward Euler on Falling Ball

**Given:**
- Mass $m = 2$ kg
- Initial height $h_0 = 5$ m (above ground)
- Initial velocity $v_0 = 0$ m/s
- $g = 9.81$ m/s² (downward)
- $\Delta t = 0.1$ s
- Compute up to $t = 0.3$ s (i.e., 3 steps)

**Equations of motion (1-D, downward positive):**
- $a = g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $v_{n+1} = v_n + a \cdot \Delta t$
- $y_{n+1} = y_n + v_n \cdot \Delta t$ (forward Euler uses *old* $v_n$)

**Step n = 0 $\to$ 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1 &= v_0 + a \cdot \Delta t = 0 + 9.81 \cdot 0.1 = 0.981 \text{ m/s} \\
y_1 &= y_0 + v_0 \cdot \Delta t = 5 + 0 \cdot 0.1 = 5.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step n = 1 $\to$ 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_2 &= v_1 + a \cdot \Delta t = 0.981 + 9.81 \cdot 0.1 = 1.962 \text{ m/s} \\
y_2 &= y_1 + v_1 \cdot \Delta t = 5.0 + 0.981 \cdot 0.1 = 5.0981 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step n = 2 $\to$ 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_3 &= v_2 + a \cdot \Delta t = 1.962 + 9.81 \cdot 0.1 = 2.943 \text{ m/s} \\
y_3 &= y_2 + v_2 \cdot \Delta t = 5.0981 + 1.962 \cdot 0.1 = 5.2943 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Notice the anomaly:** Position *increases* ($5.0 \to 5.29$ m) even though the ball is falling. This is because Forward Euler uses $v_n$ (old) for position update, lagging one step. The ball only starts "falling" in position *after* $v$ has built up. This is a known Euler artifact.

**Analytical solution at $t = 0.3$ s:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{exact}} &= g \cdot t = 9.81 \cdot 0.3 = 2.943 \text{ m/s} \\
y_{\text{exact}} &= h_0 - \tfrac{1}{2} g t^2 = 5 - \tfrac{1}{2}(9.81)(0.3)^2 = 5 - 0.44145 = 4.55855 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Percentage error in velocity:**

$$
\text{Error}_v = \frac{\vert v_{\text{Euler}} - v_{\text{exact}} \vert}{\vert v_{\text{exact}} \vert} \cdot 100\% = \frac{\vert 2.943 - 2.943 \vert}{2.943} \cdot 100\% = 0\%
$$

(Velocity is exact for constant acceleration in Euler.)

**Percentage error in position:**

$$
\text{Error}_y = \frac{\vert 5.2943 - 4.55855 \vert}{4.55855} \cdot 100\% = \frac{0.73575}{4.55855} \cdot 100\% \approx 16.14\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **16% error after only 3 steps!** This vividly demonstrates why Forward Euler is unsuitable for *long* simulations in VR. With smaller $\Delta t$ (e.g., 0.01 s) the error shrinks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q (a)** Explain the concept of numerical stability. Derive the stability condition for the Forward Euler method applied to the test equation $\dot{x} = \lambda x$, where $\lambda$ is a (possibly complex) constant. Show that the method is stable only when $\Delta t \cdot \lambda$ lies within the unit disk. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** A virtual spring-mass-damper system has $m = 1$ kg, $k = 100$ N/m, $c = 2$ Ns/m, and initial state $x(0) = 0.1$ m, $\dot{x}(0) = 0$. Use the Semi-Implicit Euler method with $\Delta t = 0.01$ s to compute the state at $t = 0.05$ s. State any observations about damping behavior. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q (a) — Stability of Forward Euler

**Step 1 — Test equation:** $\dot{x} = \lambda x$, $x(0) = x_0$.

**Step 2 — Apply Forward Euler:**

$$
x_{n+1} = x_n + \lambda x_n \Delta t = (1 + \lambda \Delta t) x_n
$$

**Step 3 — Recursive expansion:**

$$
x_n = (1 + \lambda \Delta t)^n x_0
$$

**Step 4 — Stability condition:** $x_n$ must remain bounded as $n \to \infty$ for all initial $x_0$. This requires:

$$
\vert 1 + \lambda \Delta t \vert \le 1
$$

This is the unit disk in the complex plane, centered at $-1$.

**Step 5 — Geometric interpretation:** In the complex $z = \lambda \Delta t$ plane, the stability region is the disk of radius 1 centered at $(-1, 0)$.

> [!NOTE]
> For real negative $\lambda$ (stable system), the condition becomes $-2 \le \lambda \Delta t \le 0$. For purely imaginary $\lambda$ (oscillatory), Forward Euler is **always unstable** because $\vert 1 + i\alpha \vert = \sqrt{1 + \alpha^2} > 1$ for any $\alpha \ne 0$.

**Step 6 — Comparison:**

| Method | Stability Region |
|---|---|
| Forward Euler | $\vert 1 + z \vert \le 1$ (small disk) |
| Backward Euler | $\vert 1 - z \vert \ge 1$ (entire left half-plane) — A-stable |
| Trapezoidal | Entire left half-plane — A-stable |
| RK4 | Larger disk but still bounded |

---

#### Model Solution to Q (b) — Semi-Implicit Euler on Damped Oscillator

**Given:**
- $m = 1$ kg, $k = 100$ N/m, $c = 2$ Ns/m
- $x_0 = 0.1$ m, $v_0 = 0$ m/s
- $\Delta t = 0.01$ s, compute at $t = 0.05$ s (5 steps)
- Force: $F = -kx - cv = -100x - 2v$

**Semi-Implicit Euler update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{n+1} &= v_n + \frac{F_n}{m} \cdot \Delta t = v_n + a_n \cdot \Delta t \\
x_{n+1} &= x_n + v_{n+1} \cdot \Delta t \quad \text{(uses NEW velocity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 0 $\to$ 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_0 &= -100(0.1) - 2(0) = -10 \text{ m/s}^2 \\
v_1 &= 0 + (-10)(0.01) = -0.1 \text{ m/s} \\
x_1 &= 0.1 + (-0.1)(0.01) = 0.099 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 $\to$ 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 &= -100(0.099) - 2(-0.1) = -9.9 + 0.2 = -9.7 \text{ m/s}^2 \\
v_2 &= -0.1 + (-9.7)(0.01) = -0.197 \text{ m/s} \\
x_2 &= 0.099 + (-0.197)(0.01) = 0.09703 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 $\to$ 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_2 &= -100(0.09703) - 2(-0.197) = -9.703 + 0.394 = -9.309 \text{ m/s}^2 \\
v_3 &= -0.197 + (-9.309)(0.01) = -0.29009 \text{ m/s} \\
x_3 &= 0.09703 + (-0.29009)(0.01) = 0.09413 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 $\to$ 4:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_3 &= -100(0.09413) - 2(-0.29009) = -9.413 + 0.58018 = -8.833 \text{ m/s}^2 \\
v_4 &= -0.29009 + (-8.833)(0.01) = -0.37842 \text{ m/s} \\
x_4 &= 0.09413 + (-0.37842)(0.01) = 0.09035 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 $\to$ 5:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_4 &= -100(0.09035) - 2(-0.37842) = -9.035 + 0.75684 = -8.278 \text{ m/s}^2 \\
v_5 &= -0.37842 + (-8.278)(0.01) = -0.4612 \text{ m/s} \\
x_5 &= 0.09035 + (-0.4612)(0.01) = 0.08574 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final state at $t = 0.05$ s:**

$$
\boxed{x(0.05) \approx 0.0857 \text{ m}, \quad v(0.05) \approx -0.4612 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**Observations on Damping Behavior:**
1. Position decreases monotonically from $0.1$ to $0.0857$ m as expected for a damped oscillator.
2. Velocity is *growing in magnitude* initially — this is normal for a damped oscillator starting from rest; the spring force accelerates the mass.
3. Semi-implicit Euler preserves the qualitative damping behavior; forward Euler would *amplify* the oscillation unnaturally.
4. Natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m} = 10$ rad/s, period $T \approx 0.628$ s. We are at $t = 0.05$ s $\approx T/12$, so we are in the *early* response phase.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Using $v_n$ instead of $v_{n+1}$ in the position update** — this turns Semi-Implicit Euler into Forward Euler (1 mark deduction per occurrence).
> 2. **Forgetting the damping term** $cv$ in the force — full mark loss on the dynamics part.
> 3. **Not showing all 5 steps** — KTU requires *every* intermediate calculation for 7-mark sub-questions.
> 4. **Incorrect sign convention** — for a spring, force is $-kx$; using $+kx$ gives a divergent (repulsive) system.
> 5. **Skipping the unit analysis** — always state final answer with units (m, m/s).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This!)**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] Numerical Simulation = discretized approximation of continuous physics for real-time VR.
- [ ] Governed by ODEs: $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = f(\mathbf{x}, t)$.
- [ ] Frame budget in VR: **8.33 ms (120 Hz) to 16.67 ms (60 Hz)**.
- [ ] The three pillars: **Integration, Collision, Constraint**.

### Integrator Hierarchy
- [ ] **Forward Euler** — simple, unstable, $O(\Delta t)$.
- [ ] **Semi-Implicit Euler** — real-time workhorse, symplectic, energy-bounded.
- [ ] **Verlet** — cloth & particles, position-only, time-reversible.
- [ ] **RK4** — off-line accuracy, 4 function evaluations/step.
- [ ] **Newmark-$\beta$** — structural VR, implicit, A-stable for $\beta \ge 0.25$.

### Stability and Error Formulas
- [ ] Euler position: $x_{n+1} = x_n + v_n \Delta t$.
- [ ] Verlet position: $x_{n+1} = 2x_n - x_{n-1} + a_n (\Delta t)^2$.
- [ ] RK4 update: $x_{n+1} = x_n + \tfrac{\Delta t}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)$.
- [ ] CFL condition: $C = \frac{v \cdot \Delta t}{\Delta x} \le 1$.
- [ ] Energy conservation: $E = T + U = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} k x^2$.

### Collision Pipeline
- [ ] **Broad phase:** AABB, OBB, sphere-tree → cull pairs.
- [ ] **Narrow phase:** SAT, GJK → exact contact.
- [ ] **Response:** Sequential Impulse, PGS, Baumgarte stabilization.

### Mnemonics
- [ ] **"Symplectic = Stable Energy"** — for VR long-running sessions.
- [ ] **"Position lags Velocity in Forward Euler"** — common ball-drop pitfall.
- [ ] **"Verlet needs no velocity"** — saves memory in cloth.

### Real-World VR Applications to Remember
- [ ] **Surgical VR** → mass-spring + Verlet.
- [ ] **Flight Sim** → 6-DoF RK4.
- [ ] **Crash VR** → Newmark-$\beta$ explicit.
- [ ] **Gaming** → Semi-implicit Euler + Sequential Impulse.
- [ ] **Cloth in VR** → Verlet + PBD constraints.

### Examiner's Hot Buttons
- [ ] Always **show all intermediate steps** in numerical problems.
- [ ] Always **state units** in the final answer.
- [ ] Always **state the integrator order** when defining a method.
- [ ] Always **mention stability/energy behavior** in any integrator comparison.
- [ ] Always **draw a flow diagram** for 7-mark scheme questions if asked.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Board Strategy:** Numerical Simulation is a *calculation-heavy* topic. Practice at least 3 worked problems (free-fall, spring, damped oscillator) with both Forward Euler and Semi-Implicit Euler. KTU 2024 examiners prefer *step-by-step tabulated solutions* — do not skip rows.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collision Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT504 - VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Collision Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collision Detection (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> Collision Detection is the computational problem of detecting the intersection, contact, or proximity between two or more geometric objects (primitives, polygons, bounding volumes, or articulated bodies) within a virtual environment. In Virtual Reality (VR), it is the **real-time geometric predicate** that determines whether a user’s avatar, controller, haptic probe, or any virtual object penetrates, touches, or comes within a defined trigger distance of another virtual object, thereby enabling physically plausible interaction, occlusion culling, grasping, and path planning.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, collision detection is a **sub-module of the VR Interaction Pipeline** (Module 3: Interaction, Navigation & Manipulation). It sits at the intersection of **Computer Graphics, Computational Geometry, and Real-Time Systems**.

## 1.2 Core Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Collision Primitive** | The simplest geometric shape (sphere, AABB, OBB, capsule) used as a proxy for the actual mesh. |
| **Broad Phase** | A coarse, high-speed pass that eliminates object pairs which **cannot** possibly collide. |
| **Narrow Phase** | An exact, per-pair test that confirms or denies a collision between the surviving candidate pairs. |
| **Bounding Volume Hierarchy (BVH)** | A tree of nested bounding volumes used to accelerate queries. |
| **Penetration Depth** | The minimum distance one object must be translated to resolve the intersection. |
| **Contact Manifold** | The set of contact points / normals between two colliding objects. |
| **Separating Axis** | An axis along which the projections of two convex objects do not overlap — proof of non-collision. |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Warehouse & Trolley" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Imagine you are pushing a **shopping trolley** through a **cluttered warehouse** packed with thousands of boxes. Your brain solves collision detection **continuously** without you noticing:
> 1. **Eyes do Broad Phase**: You glance at the warehouse and instantly *rule out* far-away aisles (no need to check every box).
> 2. **Eyes do Narrow Phase**: You zoom your focus onto the *box directly in front of you* and compute precisely if your trolley will clip it.
> 3. **Arms perform Response**: If yes, you steer or stop. The *trolley* is the **moving object**, the *box* is the **static object**, and the *aisle structure* is the **spatial partition** (octree).
>
> In VR, the **GPU + CPU** are the eyes, the **collision algorithm** is the brain, and the **haptic / visual feedback** is the arm.

## 1.4 Why Collision Detection is Hard in VR

- **Hard real-time constraint**: A VR headset requires **$\geq 90$ Hz** update rate → collision must finish in **$\leq 11.1$ ms** per frame.
- **Dynamic objects**: The user moves 6-DoF (position + orientation), so every body changes every frame.
- **Heterogeneous geometry**: Need sphere-sphere, sphere-triangle, AABB-OBB, capsule-mesh, etc.
- **Numerical stability**: Floating-point error at small scales (e.g., a 1 mm fingertip touching a wall) can cause *tunneling* — the object passes straight through undetected.

## 1.5 Key Engineering Metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard VR Performance Target:**
> - Frame budget: **$T_{frame} \leq 11.1\ \text{ms}$** (for 90 Hz HMDs)
> - Collision budget (typical): **$T_{coll} \leq 2 - 4\ \text{ms}$**
> - Recommended broad-phase complexity: **$O(n \log n)$** or **$O(n)$** using sweep-and-prune / spatial hashing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Phase Collision Detection Architecture

The **industry-standard pipeline** (used in Unity PhysX, Unreal Chaos, Bullet, OpenXR extensions) is:

### Phase 1 — Broad Phase
- **Goal**: Quickly reject impossible pairs.
- **Cost target**: Process **$N$ objects** in $O(N \log N)$ or better.
- **Algorithms**: Sweep-and-Prune, Uniform Grid, AABB-Tree, BVH, Octree.

### Phase 2 — Narrow Phase
- **Goal**: Exact intersection test on surviving candidate pairs only.
- **Cost target**: Per-pair cost is constant or near-constant.
- **Algorithms**: Sphere-sphere, AABB-AABB, OBB-OBB (SAT), GJK, ray-triangle, point-in-polygon.

### Phase 3 — Response (often collocated with detection)
- Compute **contact manifold** (point, normal, depth).
- Apply impulse / force to dynamic body.
- Update haptic actuator.

## 2.2 Bounding Volume Taxonomy

A **bounding volume (BV)** is a simple convex shape that tightly encloses a complex mesh. The choice of BV is the single biggest performance lever in collision detection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Tightness vs. Test Cost:**
> **Sphere** (cheapest test, loosest fit) → **AABB** → **OBB** → **k-DOP** → **Convex Hull** (tightest, most expensive).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Master the four primary tests below. They cover > 90% of KTU 2024 Scheme numerical questions on this topic.

| # | Test | Equation / Condition | Geometric Meaning | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sphere–Sphere** | $\lVert \vec{C_1} - \vec{C_2} \rVert^2 \leq (r_1 + r_2)^2$ | Distance between centers $\leq$ sum of radii (squared form avoids $\sqrt{\cdot}$). | $O(1)$ |
| 2 | **AABB–AABB** | $\forall i \in \{x,y,z\}: \max(A_i^{\min}, B_i^{\min}) \leq \min(A_i^{\max}, B_i^{\max})$ | Boxes overlap on **every** axis → overlap. | $O(1)$ |
| 3 | **Point in AABB** | $\forall i: P_i^{\min} \leq Q_i \leq P_i^{\max}$ | Point $Q$ lies within box on all 3 axes. | $O(1)$ |
| 4 | **Sphere–Plane** | $\lvert \vec{n} \cdot (\vec{P} - \vec{C}) \rvert \leq r$ where $\lVert \vec{n} \rVert = 1$ | Signed distance from center to plane $\leq$ radius. | $O(1)$ |
| 5 | **Ray–Sphere** | Solve $a t^2 + b t + c = 0$ with $a = \vec{d}\cdot\vec{d}$, $b = 2\vec{d}\cdot(\vec{o}-\vec{c})$, $c = (\vec{o}-\vec{c})\cdot(\vec{o}-\vec{c}) - r^2$; require discriminant $b^2 - 4ac \geq 0$ and $t \in [t_{\min}, t_{\max}]$ | Quadratic in $t$ (ray parameter). | $O(1)$ |
| 6 | **Separating Axis Theorem (SAT)** — for OBB–OBB | No axis $\vec{L}$ exists such that projections of $A$ and $B$ are disjoint, i.e. $\forall \vec{L}: \text{Proj}_A(\vec{L}) \cap \text{Proj}_B(\vec{L}) \neq \emptyset$ | Test 15 candidate axes: 3 face axes of A + 3 of B + 9 edge–edge cross products. | $O(1)$ per axis |
| 7 | **AABB Surface Area Heuristic (SAH)** | $\text{Cost} = C_T + C_I \cdot \sum_{i} \frac{S(\text{child}_i)}{S(\text{parent})} \cdot N_i$ | Used to build optimal BVH minimizing expected traversal cost. | Build $O(N \log N)$ |
| 8 | **Penetration Depth (GJK output)** | $\min \lVert \vec{p} \rVert$ such that $\vec{p} = \vec{a} - \vec{b}$ with $\vec{a} \in A$, $\vec{b} \in B$, and Minkowski Difference $A \ominus B$ contains origin. | Distance from origin to surface of Minkowski difference. | Iterative |

> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Sphere-Sphere** dominates particle systems, hand-fingertip proxies, and crowd simulations in VR.
> - **AABB-AABB** is the workhorse of broad-phase sweep-and-prune (used in Unity Physics by default).
> - **SAT** is the gold-standard for accurate **OBB-OBB** tests in rigid-body physics.
> - **GJK** is used for arbitrary convex–convex pairs (Unreal’s default for convex colliders).

## 2.4 Spatial Partitioning — The Broad-Phase Accelerators

| Structure | Cell Shape | Best Use Case | Query Time | Build Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Uniform Grid** | Equal cubes | Uniformly distributed objects (e.g., particles) | $O(1)$ avg | $O(N)$ |
| **Octree** | Recursive 8-way cube subdivision | Outdoors, sparse scenes | $O(\log N)$ | $O(N \log N)$ |
| **BSP Tree** | Hyperplane partitioning | Static indoor scenes, CSG | $O(\log N)$ | $O(N \log N)$ |
| **BVH (AABB-Tree)** | Hierarchy of boxes | Deformable meshes, animation | $O(\log N)$ | $O(N \log N)$ |
| **Spatial Hash** | Hashed grid cells | Massive particle counts (millions) | $O(1)$ avg | $O(N)$ |

## 2.5 VR-Specific Collision Concerns

- **Tunneling** at high velocities → use **swept volumes** (capsule from $P_{t-1}$ to $P_t$) or **continuous collision detection (CCD)**.
- **Hand Tracking Latency** → add a **predictive sphere** at predicted hand position $t + \Delta t$.
- **Jitter** at micro-intersection → use **skinning-aware** bounding volumes for animated avatars.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sphere–Sphere Intersection Test

**Problem Setup:** Two spheres $S_1$ and $S_2$ with centers $C_1$, $C_2$ and radii $r_1$, $r_2$. Determine if they intersect.

**Geometric Idea:** The spheres touch when the distance between their centers equals the sum of their radii.

**Step 1 — Euclidean distance:**

$$
d = \lVert C_1 - C_2 \rVert = \sqrt{(C_{1x}-C_{2x})^2 + (C_{1y}-C_{2y})^2 + (C_{1z}-C_{2z})^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Collision condition:**

$$
d \leq r_1 + r_2
$$

**Step 3 — Square both sides (avoids costly $\sqrt{\cdot}$):**

$$
d^2 \leq (r_1 + r_2)^2
$$

$$
(C_{1x}-C_{2x})^2 + (C_{1y}-C_{2y})^2 + (C_{1z}-C_{2z})^2 \leq (r_1 + r_2)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Final squared form (no square roots, suitable for GPU shader):**

$$
\boxed{(C_{1x}-C_{2x})^2 + (C_{1y}-C_{2y})^2 + (C_{1z}-C_{2z})^2 \leq (r_1 + r_2)^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Penetration depth (if needed for response):**

$$
\delta = (r_1 + r_2) - d
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why square?** Computing $\sqrt{\cdot}$ requires a transcendental instruction (~10–20 cycles on CPU). In a tight 90 Hz VR loop processing thousands of pairs, the squared form **saves ~30–50%** of the per-pair cost.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — AABB–AABB Overlap Test

**Problem Setup:** Axis-Aligned Bounding Box $A = [A_x^{\min}, A_x^{\max}] \times [A_y^{\min}, A_y^{\max}] \times [A_z^{\min}, A_z^{\max}]$, and box $B$ defined similarly.

**Step 1 — Per-axis overlap condition:**

On axis $i \in \{x, y, z\}$, $A$ and $B$ overlap if and only if:

$$
\max(A_i^{\min}, B_i^{\min}) \leq \min(A_i^{\max}, B_i^{\max})
$$

**Step 2 — Combine axes (separation / non-overlap principle):**

The boxes **do not** overlap if they are separated on **any single axis**.

**Step 3 — Boolean result:**

$$
\text{Overlap} = \bigwedge_{i \in \{x,y,z\}} \left[ \max(A_i^{\min}, B_i^{\min}) \leq \min(A_i^{\max}, B_i^{\max}) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent De Morgan form (often faster in SIMD):**

$$
\text{Separated} = \bigvee_{i \in \{x,y,z\}} \left[ A_i^{\max} < B_i^{\min} \;\lor\; B_i^{\max} < A_i^{\min} \right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Overlap} = \neg\text{Separated}}
$$

**Step 5 — Early-out optimization:**

In code, the loop should check for **non-overlap first** and `return false` immediately on the first separating axis, avoiding the remaining 2 axis tests.

> **KTU Exam Note:** The De Morgan (early-out) form is **the** form expected in board answer scripts — it is the form real physics engines use (e.g., PhysX, Bullet).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ray–Sphere Intersection (used in VR pointer / laser tests)

**Problem Setup:** A ray $\vec{R}(t) = \vec{O} + t \vec{D}$ with $t \geq 0$ and a sphere centered at $\vec{C}$ with radius $r$.

**Step 1 — Substitute ray into sphere equation:**

$$
\lVert \vec{R}(t) - \vec{C} \rVert^2 = r^2
$$

$$
(\vec{O} + t\vec{D} - \vec{C}) \cdot (\vec{O} + t\vec{D} - \vec{C}) = r^2
$$

**Step 2 — Expand and collect terms in $t$:**

Let $\vec{L} = \vec{O} - \vec{C}$. Then:

$$
(\vec{L} + t\vec{D}) \cdot (\vec{L} + t\vec{D}) = r^2
$$

$$
\vec{L}\cdot\vec{L} + 2t(\vec{L}\cdot\vec{D}) + t^2(\vec{D}\cdot\vec{D}) - r^2 = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Identify quadratic coefficients:**

$$
a = \vec{D}\cdot\vec{D}
$$

$$
b = 2(\vec{L}\cdot\vec{D})
$$

$$
c = \vec{L}\cdot\vec{L} - r^2
$$

**Step 4 — Solve via discriminant:**

$$
\Delta = b^2 - 4ac
$$

- If $\Delta < 0$: **no intersection**.
- If $\Delta \geq 0$: compute roots $t_1 = \dfrac{-b - \sqrt{\Delta}}{2a}$, $t_2 = \dfrac{-b + \sqrt{\Delta}}{2a}$.

**Step 5 — Pick valid (positive, in-range) root:**

$$
t_{\text{hit}} = \begin{cases} t_1 & \text{if } t_1 \in [t_{\min}, t_{\max}] \\ t_2 & \text{if } t_2 \in [t_{\min}, t_{\max}] \\ \text{none} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VR Use Case:** This is the exact test used for **VR laser pointers, gaze-based selection, and occlusion queries** in OpenXR / WebXR runtimes.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Separating Axis Theorem (SAT) for OBB–OBB

**Problem Setup:** Two Oriented Bounding Boxes $A$ and $B$, each defined by a center $\vec{C}$, three orthonormal half-axis vectors $\vec{u}_i$ of length $h_i$.

**Step 1 — Project both boxes onto axis $\vec{L}$:**

$$
\text{Proj}_A(\vec{L}) = \left[ \vec{C}_A \cdot \vec{L} - \sum_i h_i \lvert \vec{u}_i^A \cdot \vec{L} \rvert,\; \vec{C}_A \cdot \vec{L} + \sum_i h_i \lvert \vec{u}_i^A \cdot \vec{L} \rvert \right]
$$

(Same for $B$.)

**Step 2 — Separation test on axis $\vec{L}$:**

The boxes are **separated** along $\vec{L}$ iff:

$$
\max(\text{Proj}_A^{\min}, \text{Proj}_B^{\min}) > \min(\text{Proj}_A^{\max}, \text{Proj}_B^{\max})
$$

**Step 3 — Candidate axes to test (exactly 15 for two OBBs in 3D):**

- 3 face-normal axes of $A$: $\{\vec{u}_x^A, \vec{u}_y^A, \vec{u}_z^A\}$
- 3 face-normal axes of $B$: $\{\vec{u}_x^B, \vec{u}_y^B, \vec{u}_z^B\}$
- 9 edge–edge cross products: $\{\vec{u}_i^A \times \vec{u}_j^B\}$ for $i, j \in \{x, y, z\}$.

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$
\text{Overlap} = \bigwedge_{\vec{L} \in \mathcal{A}_{15}} \neg\text{Separated}(\vec{L})
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{No separating axis found} \iff \text{Boxes overlap}}
$$

**Step 5 — Penetration depth & normal:**

The axis with the **smallest overlap** is the **Minimum Translation Vector (MTV)** — its length is penetration depth, its direction is the contact normal.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Collision Detection Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Object Stream N dynamic static bodies] --> A1[Frame Begins timestamp t]
    A1 --> A2[Update Transforms position orientation from physics]
    A2 --> A3[Refresh Bounding Volumes AABB OBB sphere]
    A3 --> A4[Broad Phase Phase 1]
    A4 --> A4a[Sweep and Prune Sort and Sweep on AABB extents]
    A4 --> A4b[Spatial Hash Uniform grid hash map lookup]
    A4 --> A4c[BVH Tree Traversal AABB hierarchy]
    A4 --> A5[Candidate Pairs K pairs where K much less than N squared]
    A5 --> A6[Narrow Phase Phase 2]
    A6 --> A6a[Sphere Sphere Test]
    A6 --> A6b[AABB AABB Test]
    A6 --> A6c[OBB OBB SAT Test]
    A6 --> A6d[GJK Test arbitrary convex pairs]
    A6 --> A7[Confirmed Contact Set]
    A7 --> A8[Compute Manifold Contact Points Normals Penetration Depth]
    A8 --> A9[Collision Response Apply Impulse Resolve Penetration]
    A9 --> A10[Trigger VR Feedback Haptic Visual Audio]
    A10 --> A11[Frame End Output to Renderer and Haptics]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The pipeline prunes an $O(N^2)$ problem down to $O(N \log N)$ via broad phase, then applies constant-cost narrow-phase tests, and finally drives haptic/visual feedback — all within the 11.1 ms VR frame budget.

---

## 4.2 Bounding Volume Hierarchy (BVH) Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    r00[Root BVH node contains entire mesh]
    r00 --> rA[Child A left subtree]
    r00 --> rB[Child B right subtree]
    rA --> rA1[Leaf A1 single triangle bbox]
    rA --> rA2[Leaf A2 cluster of 3 triangles]
    rB --> rB1[Leaf B1 single triangle bbox]
    rB --> rB2[Leaf B2 cluster of 5 triangles]
    rA1 --> tA1[Triangle 17]
    rA1 --> tA2[Triangle 18]
    rA2 --> tA3[Triangle 19 20 21]
    rB1 --> tB1[Triangle 22]
    rB2 --> tB2[Triangle 23 24 25 26 27]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A ray or moving body first tests against the **root AABB**. If it misses, the **entire subtree is skipped** (this is the source of the $\log N$ speedup). If it hits, the test recurses into children until a leaf is reached.

---

## 4.3 Broad-Phase vs Narrow-Phase Decision Matrix (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    q1[Two objects A and B enter pipeline] --> q2{Are both approximated by AABBs or spheres?}
    q2 -- yes --> q3[Use AABB or Sphere Test Broad Phase O1 per pair]
    q2 -- no --> q4{Are both convex polyhedra?}
    q4 -- yes --> q5[Use GJK Narrow Phase iterative]
    q4 -- no --> q6{One is a triangle mesh?}
    q6 -- yes --> q7[Use BVH vs Triangle Mesh]
    q6 -- no --> q8[Fallback Decompose into Convex Pieces]
    q3 --> r1[Pass to Response]
    q5 --> r1
    q7 --> r1
    q8 --> r1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is a **decision flowchart** that the VR engine’s collision dispatcher uses to pick the cheapest *correct* test for each pair type.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**Define collision detection in the context of Virtual Reality. List any two spatial partitioning data structures used to accelerate it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Collision detection in VR is the real-time geometric process of determining whether two or more virtual objects (e.g., the user's avatar, controllers, or scene meshes) intersect, touch, or come within a defined trigger distance, in order to enable physically plausible interaction, grasping, occlusion, and path navigation within the immersive environment.

**Two spatial partitioning structures (1 mark):**
1. **Octree** — recursively subdivides 3D space into 8 equal sub-cubes; ideal for sparse outdoor VR scenes.
2. **Uniform Grid** — divides space into a fixed grid of equal cells hashed for $O(1)$ access; ideal for uniformly distributed particles.

*(Acceptable alternatives: BSP Tree, BVH, Spatial Hashing, Sweep-and-Prune.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between Broad Phase and Narrow Phase collision detection with one example algorithm for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Broad Phase | Narrow Phase |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Quickly **reject** object pairs that cannot possibly collide. | **Confirm or deny** intersection for surviving candidate pairs. |
| **Cost per pair** | Very low (AABB check). | Higher (exact geometric test). |
| **Typical algorithm** | **Sweep-and-Prune** (Sort and Sweep). | **GJK** (Gilbert-Johnson-Keerthi) for convex pairs. |
| **Output** | A small set of candidate pairs. | Boolean collision + contact manifold. |
| **Complexity** | $O(N \log N)$ or $O(N)$. | $O(1)$ per surviving pair. |

**Example:** For 10,000 dynamic objects, broad phase reduces the $10^8$ possible pairs to perhaps $10^3$ candidates; narrow phase then confirms collisions on those $10^3$ pairs only.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Bounding Volume Hierarchy (BVH)** approach for accelerating collision detection in VR. Discuss the role of the **Surface Area Heuristic (SAH)** in its construction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Sphere–Sphere collision detection test** from first principles. Explain why the squared-distance form is preferred in real-time VR pipelines. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — BVH + SAH (7 marks)

**1. BVH Definition (1 mark):**
A Bounding Volume Hierarchy (BVH) is a tree data structure in which every node encloses a subset of geometric primitives (triangles, particles, sub-meshes) inside a simple bounding volume (typically an AABB). The root encloses the whole object; leaves enclose individual primitives.

**2. Tree Traversal Logic (2 marks):**
- A ray / point / moving body is tested against the **root AABB**.
- If **miss** → entire subtree is culled (this is the source of the $\log N$ speedup).
- If **hit** → recursion proceeds to both children.
- At **leaf nodes**, an exact triangle/primitive test is performed.
- The first hit terminates the search (closest hit).

**3. BVH Construction — Top-Down vs Bottom-Up (1 mark):**
- **Top-down** (more common): split the primitive set at each node by median or SAH criterion; recurse.
- **Bottom-up**: start with one primitive per leaf; merge pairs using SAH cost.

**4. Surface Area Heuristic (SAH) — Cost Function (2 marks):**

For a node $N$ split into children $L$ and $R$:

$$
\text{Cost}(N) = C_T + C_I \cdot \left[ \frac{S(L)}{S(N)} \cdot N_L + \frac{S(R)}{S(N)} \cdot N_R \right]
$$

Where:
- $C_T$ = cost of traversing the current node (constant).
- $C_I$ = cost of testing intersection with a child (constant).
- $S(\cdot)$ = surface area of the bounding volume.
- $N_L, N_R$ = number of primitives in left and right children.

The split (position + axis) that **minimizes SAH cost** is chosen. This balances tree depth against the probability of child visits, producing near-optimal query time.

**5. VR-Specific Note (1 mark):**
BVHs are critical for VR mesh-vs-ray tests (laser pointer selection, occlusion culling) and for animated avatars (the BVH is rebuilt per frame for skinning-deformed meshes, or the deformation is applied lazily only to nodes currently traversed).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Sphere–Sphere Derivation (7 marks)

**1. Setup (1 mark):**
Let sphere $S_1$ have center $\vec{C_1} = (x_1, y_1, z_1)$ and radius $r_1$. Let sphere $S_2$ have center $\vec{C_2} = (x_2, y_2, z_2)$ and radius $r_2$.

**2. Geometric Condition (1 mark):**
The spheres intersect (or touch) if and only if the Euclidean distance between their centers is at most the sum of their radii:

$$
d \leq r_1 + r_2
$$

**3. Euclidean Distance Expanded (1 mark):**

$$
\sqrt{(x_1 - x_2)^2 + (y_1 - y_2)^2 + (z_1 - z_2)^2} \leq r_1 + r_2
$$

**4. Squaring Both Sides (1 mark):**
Since both sides are non-negative, squaring is monotonic and equivalent:

$$
(x_1 - x_2)^2 + (y_1 - y_2)^2 + (z_1 - z_2)^2 \leq (r_1 + r_2)^2
$$

**5. Squared-Distance Form (Final, 1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\lVert \vec{C_1} - \vec{C_2} \rVert^2 \leq (r_1 + r_2)^2}
$$

**6. Why the Squared Form is Preferred in Real-Time VR (2 marks):**

(a) **No $\sqrt{\cdot}$ operation** — a square root is a transcendental instruction costing ~10–20 CPU cycles. Across thousands of pairs per VR frame, the savings translate to 1–3 ms of frame time, which is the difference between hitting and missing the 90 Hz HMD refresh target.

(b) **GPU shader friendliness** — GPU shader cores have no hardware $\sqrt{\cdot}$ fast path. The squared form maps directly to `dot()` / `length_sq()` intrinsics that compile to single MAD instructions.

(c) **Numerical stability** — comparing $d^2$ to $(r_1+r_2)^2$ avoids the small-loss-of-precision introduced by taking a square root of two very close values.

**7. Penetration Depth (Bonus, included for completeness):**

$$
\delta = (r_1 + r_2) - d \quad (\delta \geq 0 \text{ when intersecting})
$$

This value is passed to the **response** stage to push the objects apart.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the **Separating Axis Theorem (SAT)** for OBB–OBB collision detection. List the candidate axes and the projection test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a step-by-step derivation, derive the **AABB–AABB overlap test** and explain its use in the **Sweep-and-Prune** broad-phase algorithm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — SAT for OBB–OBB (7 marks)

**1. Theorem Statement (2 marks):**
> **Separating Axis Theorem:** Two convex sets $A$ and $B$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ are **disjoint** if and only if there exists at least one axis $\vec{L}$ such that the projections of $A$ and $B$ onto $\vec{L}$ are **disjoint** intervals. Such an axis is called a **separating axis**.

Equivalently: $A$ and $B$ **overlap** $\iff$ **no separating axis exists**.

**2. Candidate Axes for Two OBBs in 3D (2 marks):**
There are exactly **15 candidate axes** to test:
- **3 face-normal axes of A:** $\vec{u}_x^A, \vec{u}_y^A, \vec{u}_z^A$.
- **3 face-normal axes of B:** $\vec{u}_x^B, \vec{u}_y^B, \vec{u}_z^B$.
- **9 edge–edge cross products:** $\vec{u}_i^A \times \vec{u}_j^B$ for $i, j \in \{x, y, z\}$.

(These 15 axes span the **convex hull of separating directions** — a theorem by Gottschalk et al.)

**3. Projection of an OBB onto Axis $\vec{L}$ (1 mark):**
For OBB with center $\vec{C}$ and orthonormal half-axes $\vec{u}_i$ of length $h_i$:

$$
\text{Proj}(\vec{L}) = \left[ \vec{C}\cdot\vec{L} - \sum_i h_i \lvert \vec{u}_i \cdot \vec{L} \rvert,\; \vec{C}\cdot\vec{L} + \sum_i h_i \lvert \vec{u}_i \cdot \vec{L} \rvert \right]
$$

**4. Separation Test on Axis $\vec{L}$ (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Separated on } \vec{L} \iff \max(\text{Proj}_A^{\min}, \text{Proj}_B^{\min}) > \min(\text{Proj}_A^{\max}, \text{Proj}_B^{\max})
$$

**5. Final Algorithm (1 mark):**
```
for each axis L in [15 candidates]:
    if Separated(L):
        return NO_COLLISION   // early exit
return COLLISION
```

The axis with the **smallest overlap** (if collision occurs) is the **Minimum Translation Vector (MTV)** — its length is the penetration depth; its direction is the contact normal — both required by the **response** stage.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — AABB–AABB + Sweep-and-Prune (7 marks)

**1. AABB Definition (1 mark):**
An **Axis-Aligned Bounding Box** $A$ is defined by independent intervals on each of the three Cartesian axes:

$$
A = [A_x^{\min}, A_x^{\max}] \times [A_y^{\min}, A_y^{\max}] \times [A_z^{\min}, A_z^{\max}]
$$

**2. Per-Axis Overlap Condition (1 mark):**
On any single axis $i \in \{x, y, z\}$, the intervals of $A$ and $B$ overlap iff:

$$
\max(A_i^{\min}, B_i^{\min}) \leq \min(A_i^{\max}, B_i^{\max})
$$

**3. Combining Axes (1 mark):**
The two AABBs overlap **if and only if** they overlap on **every** axis (no axis separates them):

$$
\text{Overlap} = \bigwedge_{i \in \{x,y,z\}} \left[ \max(A_i^{\min}, B_i^{\min}) \leq \min(A_i^{\max}, B_i^{\max}) \right]
$$

**4. De Morgan / Early-Out Form (1 mark):**
The boxes are **separated** if any single axis separates them:

$$
\text{Separated} = \bigvee_{i \in \{x,y,z\}} \left[ A_i^{\max} < B_i^{\min} \;\lor\; B_i^{\max} < A_i^{\min} \right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Overlap} = \neg\text{Separated}}
$$

**5. Computational Cost (1 mark):**
At most 6 floating-point comparisons; in practice ~2–4 due to early-out. The test is **branch-prediction friendly** and **SIMD-vectorizable** (compare all $x$-axes in parallel, then $y$, then $z$).

**6. Use in Sweep-and-Prune (2 marks):**
**Sweep-and-Prune** is a $O(N \log N)$ broad-phase algorithm built entirely on AABBs:

- **Step 1:** For each object, record the endpoint pairs $(x^{\min}, x^{\max})$, $(y^{\min}, y^{\max})$, $(z^{\min}, z^{\max})$ of its AABB.
- **Step 2:** Sort the 2N endpoints along one chosen axis (usually the **longest axis** of the scene's bounding box, to maximize culling).
- **Step 3:** Sweep a "cursor" along the sorted list. Maintain an **interval-overlap set** $S$ of currently-open objects. When the cursor reaches a $\min$ endpoint, add that object to $S$ and test it against every object already in $S$ for interval overlap; on a $\max$ endpoint, remove that object from $S$.
- **Step 4:** A pair of objects overlapping on **all 3 axes** (tested with the AABB–AABB test) is forwarded to the narrow phase.

**7. Why It is Used in VR (Bonus):**
- It is **$O(N \log N)$** instead of $O(N^2)$, crucial for VR scenes with thousands of dynamic objects.
- It is **temporal-coherent** — the sort order barely changes frame-to-frame, so **insertion-sort** can run in $O(N)$ amortized time, ideal for real-time 90 Hz loops.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 Scheme Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the squared form** — writing $\sqrt{\cdot}$ on the LHS of the sphere test without justifying the squared form **costs 1–2 marks** in long answers.
> 2. **Wrong axis count in SAT** — students often write 3 or 6 candidate axes for OBB–OBB. The correct count is **15** (3 + 3 + 9). Partial credit only if you list categories.
> 3. **Skipping the broad phase** — when the question asks for collision detection, do **not** jump straight to the narrow-phase test. Always mention **broad phase + narrow phase** as a 2-stage pipeline to score full marks.
> 4. **No unit / no frame-budget mention** — KTU examiners reward students who mention the **$\leq 11.1$ ms VR frame budget** when justifying an algorithm choice. Bonus 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up AABB vs OBB** — AABBs are axis-aligned; OBBs are oriented. Confusing the two in SAT derivations loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Skipping numerical justification** — a derivation without one numeric example or a final boxed result is marked **incomplete**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Last-Minute Revision — Collision Detection (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** Real-time geometric predicate that decides intersection between virtual objects in VR. Must complete in **$\leq 11.1$ ms** (90 Hz).
- **Two-Phase Pipeline:** **Broad Phase** (reject impossible pairs, $O(N \log N)$ or better) → **Narrow Phase** (exact test, $O(1)$ per pair) → **Response** (manifold + impulse + haptics).
- **Sphere–Sphere Test:** $\lVert \vec{C_1} - \vec{C_2} \rVert^2 \leq (r_1 + r_2)^2$ — squared form avoids $\sqrt{\cdot}$; used for hand/fingertip proxies and particles.
- **AABB–AABB Test:** Overlap on **all 3 axes** $\iff$ overlap. Early-out De Morgan form used in real engines.
- **Sphere–Plane Test:** $\lvert \vec{n}\cdot(\vec{P}-\vec{C}) \rvert \leq r$ with $\lVert\vec{n}\rVert=1$ — used for ground / wall contact.
- **Ray–Sphere Test:** Quadratic $a t^2 + b t + c = 0$ with discriminant $\Delta = b^2 - 4ac \geq 0$ — used for VR laser pointers, gaze, occlusion.
- **SAT for OBB–OBB:** Test **15 candidate axes** (3 + 3 + 9). Smallest overlap = MTV (penetration depth & contact normal).
- **Spatial Partitioning Choices:**
  - **Uniform Grid / Spatial Hash** → particles, uniform density.
  - **Octree** → outdoor sparse scenes.
  - **BSP Tree** → static indoor / CSG.
  - **BVH (AABB-Tree)** → meshes, animated avatars.
  - **Sweep-and-Prune** → temporal-coherent rigid bodies.
- **GJK Algorithm:** Iterative convex–convex test based on **Minkowski difference**; outputs separation distance and (with EPA) penetration depth.
- **VR-Specific Concerns:**
  - **Tunneling** at high velocity → use **swept volumes** or **CCD (Continuous Collision Detection)**.
  - **Prediction** → add predictive spheres at $P_{t+\Delta t}$ for hand tracking.
  - **Skinning-aware BVs** for animated avatars to avoid jitter.
  - **Predictive haptics** — collision must be known 1 frame ahead to drive actuators in sync with the 90 Hz display.
- **Bounding Volume Tightness vs Cost:** **Sphere** (loose, cheap) → **AABB** → **OBB** → **k-DOP** → **Convex Hull** (tight, expensive).
- **SAH Cost Function:** $\text{Cost}(N) = C_T + C_I \cdot \sum_{i \in \{L,R\}} \dfrac{S(\text{child}_i)}{S(N)} \cdot N_i$ — minimised to build optimal BVH.
- **Penetration Depth Formula (sphere-sphere):** $\delta = (r_1 + r_2) - d$, with $d = \lVert \vec{C_1} - \vec{C_2} \rVert$.
- **Frame Budget Allocation (typical):** Rendering ~6 ms + Collision ~3 ms + Physics response ~1.5 ms + Haptics ~0.5 ms = 11 ms total.
- **Famous Algorithms to Recall by Name:** **GJK, EPA, SAT, SAH, Sweep-and-Prune, Hierarchical Grids, k-DOP, BSP, Octree, Bounding Sphere Trees**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mismatched Motion and Vection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCNT504 - VIRTUAL REALITY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Mismatched Motion and Vection # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Mismatched Motion In the context of Virtual Reality (VR), **Mismatched Motion** is formally defined as a *sensorimotor conflict* that arises when the motion information delivered to the user through one sensory channel (typically the visual channel in a Head-Mounted Display) is inconsistent with the motion information concurrently delivered through another sensory channel (such as the vestibular, proprioceptive, or somatosensory systems). > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Mismatched motion is classified under *Perceptual Illusions in VR* and is the primary perceptual trigger for *cybersickness* (a form of visually-induced motion sickness). This concept directly maps to **CO3** of PBCNT504: *"Analyze the perceptual and cognitive aspects of VR systems."* Mathematically, the **Sensory Mismatch Vector** at any instant $t$ is expressed as: $$\vec{M}(t) = \vec{S}_{visual}(t) - \vec{S}_{vestibular}(t) - \vec{S}_{proprioceptive}(t)$$ Where: - $\vec{S}_{visual}(t)$ = Visual motion signal from the rendered virtual scene - $\vec{S}_{vestibular}(t)$ = Vestibular signal from the inner ear (otolith & semicircular canals) - $\vec{S}_{proprioceptive}(t)$ = Proprioceptive signal from muscles, joints, and skin The magnitude $\vert \vec{M}(t) \vert$ is the **mismatch intensity** that the central nervous system must reconcile. ## 1.2 Formal Definition of Vection **Vection** is the phenomenon of *self-motion perception* induced purely by visual stimulation, in the **absence of any corresponding physical movement** of the observer. It is the cornerstone illusion of immersive VR because it allows a stationary user to *feel* as though they are navigating through a virtual world. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU Definition:** *Vection is the illusion of self-motion that occurs when large portions of the visual field move coherently, while the observer's body remains physically stationary. It is generated by the visual system's dominance over vestibular cues under low-vestibular-stimulation conditions.* The standard unit of vection measurement is the **Vection Strength Index (VSI)**, ranging from **0 (no self-motion perceived) to 1.0 (full conviction of self-motion)**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition ### Real-World Analogy for Vection (The "Train Illusion") Imagine you are sitting in a **stationary train at a platform**. Beside you, another train begins to glide forward smoothly. For a brief, deeply disorienting moment, you *feel* that **your own train is rolling backwards**. Your eyes say "the world is moving forward," but your inner ear says "you are perfectly still." Your brain resolves the conflict by trusting the visual input — and suddenly, you are moving. That is **vection**. ### Real-World Analogy for Mismatched Motion (The IMAX Flight Simulator) Watch a first-person drone video on a large IMAX screen. Your eyes see breathtaking forward flight over canyons, but your body is glued to a cinema seat. Your vestibular system reports "stationary." This **visual–vestibular mismatch** is the engine of cinematic motion sickness, the exact same mechanism that causes VR cybersickness. > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid:** *Vection* is what you *feel*. *Mismatched motion* is what *causes* what you feel. Mismatched motion is the input; vection (or sickness) is the perceptual output. ## 1.4 Categorical Types of Vection | Vection Type | Stimulus Direction | Primary VR Application | |---|---|---| | **Linear Vection** | Forward / backward / lateral translation | Walking simulators, driving simulators | | **Circular Vection** | Rotation about vertical (yaw) axis | Space-disorientation trainers | | **Roll Vection** | Rotation about front-to-back axis | Flight simulators (banking turns) | | **Pitch Vection** | Rotation about lateral axis | Roller-coaster VR experiences | The **threshold angular velocity** to induce vection in most adults is approximately **2°/s to 5°/s** of visual field motion, far below the vestibular threshold of **~2°/s²** for semicircular canal detection — this asymmetry is what makes vision the dominant cue. ## 1.5 Why This Matters in VR Engineering > [!WARNING] > **Engineering Reality Check:** Nearly **60–80% of VR users** report some level of cybersickness within 20 minutes of immersive use. A VR engineer who does not understand mismatched motion and vection cannot build a commercially viable product. These concepts determine *refresh rates, locomotion techniques, FOV design, and comfort ratings*. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Sensory Conflict Theory (Reason & Brand Framework) The dominant theoretical model for explaining mismatched motion is the **Sensory Conflict Theory**, originally proposed by Reason and Brand (1975) and extended by Oman for VR. It postulates that motion sickness emerges whenever the pattern of afferent signals arriving at the central nervous system **does not match any expected pattern** stored in a neural "store" of prior sensory experiences. ### 2.1.1 Operational Steps of Sensory Conflict 1. **Sensory Acquisition Stage** — Visual, vestibular, and proprioceptive signals are sampled continuously. 2. **Pattern Comparison Stage** — Incoming sensory vectors are compared against the *expected sensory pattern* stored in the brain's internal model. 3. **Mismatch Detection Stage** — A discrepancy greater than the *neural tolerance threshold* $T_{conflict}$ is flagged. 4. **Conflict Response Stage** — The autonomic nervous system triggers nausea-inducing responses (pallor, sweating, salivation). 5. **Adaptive Learning Stage** — Repeated exposure may lower $T_{conflict}$ through *adaptation* (e.g., pilots, sailors). The conflict magnitude is computed as: $$C(t) = \left\| \vec{S}_{incoming}(t) - \vec{S}_{expected}(t) \right\|_2$$ Where $\left\| \cdot \right\|_2$ denotes the **L2 (Euclidean) norm** of the sensory mismatch vector. ## 2.2 Vection Generation: The Ecological Model Gibson's **Ecological Theory of Visual Perception** explains vection as a consequence of **optic flow** — the pattern of light reaching the eye as an observer moves through the environment. The brain interprets radial optic flow patterns as self-motion. > [!NOTE] > **Lambertian Reflectance Assumption:** For vection models, the visual scene is approximated as a **Lambertian surface** with uniform diffuse reflectance $\rho$, simplifying optical flow calculations. ## 2.3 Quantitative Factors Governing Vection Strength ### 2.3.1 Vection Onset Latency ($T_{latency}$) The time between the start of visual motion stimulus and the user's conscious report of self-motion. KTU-relevant empirical relationship: $$T_{latency} \approx \frac{k_1 \cdot d}{v_{visual} \cdot FOV}$$ Where: - $T_{latency}$ = Vection onset time in **seconds** - $d$ = Perceived depth of the dominant visual feature (in meters) - $v_{visual}$ = Apparent visual field velocity (m/s) - $FOV$ = Field of view covered by the moving stimulus (degrees) - $k_1$ = Empirical constant ($\approx 0.6$ for linear vection) > [!TIP] > **Higher FOV + higher visual velocity = faster vection onset**, but also **higher mismatch intensity = greater sickness risk**. This is the central engineering trade-off in VR locomotion design. ### 2.3.2 Vection Strength (Bojko's Empirical Equation) $$VS = \frac{R_{imagined}}{R_{real}} \times 100\%$$ Where: - $VS$ = Vection Strength (%) - $R_{imagined}$ = Magnitude estimate of self-motion reported by participant (visual stimulus only) - $R_{real}$ = Magnitude estimate of an equivalent *actual* physical motion A $VS$ value of **100%** means the visual stimulus perfectly matches the sensation of real motion. ### 2.3.3 Cybersickness Susceptibility (Kennedy's SSQ Model) The **Simulator Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ)** scores cybersickness across three subscales: $$SSQ_{total} = \alpha \cdot N + \beta \cdot O + \gamma \cdot D$$ Where: - $N$ = Nausea subscale score - $O$ = Oculomotor subscale score - $D$ = Disorientation subscale score - $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = Standard Kennedy-Lane weighting coefficients ($\alpha = 7.543$, $\beta = 7.174$, $\gamma = 13.924$) ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Metric | Mathematical Form | Primary Variables | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sensory Mismatch Vector | $\vec{M}(t) = \vec{S}_v(t) - \vec{S}_{ves}(t) - \vec{S}_{p}(t)$ | Visual, vestibular, proprio signals | Quantifies motion sickness trigger | | 2 | Conflict Magnitude | $C(t) = \left\| \vec{S}_{in}(t) - \vec{S}_{exp}(t) \right\|_2$ | L2 norm of sensory diff | Cybersickness prediction | | 3 | Vection Latency | $T_{latency} \approx k_1 d / (v_{vis} \cdot FOV)$ | Depth, velocity, FOV | Locomotion realism tuning | | 4 | Vection Strength | $VS = (R_{imagined}/R_{real}) \times 100\%$ | Reported vs. real motion | Immersion quality metric | | 5 | Cybersickness Score | $SSQ_{total} = \alpha N + \beta O + \gamma D$ | Nausea, oculomotor, disorientation | Health & safety compliance | | 6 | Optic Flow Field | $\vec{\omega} = (v_x/z, v_y/z, v_z/z)$ | Translation $v$, depth $z$ | Self-motion estimation | | 7 | Critical Visual Velocity | $v_c = \theta_{ves,min} \cdot d_{scene}$ | Vestibular threshold $\theta_{ves,min}$ | Locomotion design boundary | | 8 | Vection Onset Gain | $G_{vection} = \Delta VS / \Delta t_{stimulus}$ | Change in strength over time | Adaptation tracking | | 9 | Sensory Conflict Tolerance | $T_{conflict} = \mu + \sigma \cdot z_{\alpha}$ | Mean $\mu$, SD $\sigma$ | Personalization threshold | | 10 | OKN Phase Velocity | $\omega_{OKN} = 2\pi f_{drift} \cdot r_{eye}$ | Drift freq, eye radius | Vestibular-Ocular coupling | ## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility | Domain | Application | |---|---| | **VR Gaming** | Designing teleportation vs. smooth-locomotion to minimize $C(t)$ | | **Flight Simulation** | Combining G-seats + HMD for matched motion cues | | **Medical Rehabilitation** | Using controlled vection for vestibular desensitization therapy | | **Automotive Industry** | Driver-in-the-loop simulators with motion platforms for ADAS testing | | **Architectural Visualization** | Using gentle optic flow in walkthroughs to avoid disorientation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** When answering theory questions, always state both the *physiological cause* (e.g., semicircular canal under-stimulation) and the *engineering mitigation* (e.g., dynamic FOV reduction, snap-turning). Examiners reward dual-cause solutions. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Vection Onset Latency from Optic Flow Principles We derive the time-to-onset of linear vection by modeling the visual field as a translating pattern of dots at constant depth. ### Step 1: Define the Retinal Image Motion For a Lambertian point at scene depth $d$ moving with world velocity $v_{world}$, the induced retinal velocity $v_{ret}$ is: $$v_{ret} = \frac{v_{world} \cdot f_{eye}}{d}$$ Where $f_{eye}$ is the focal length of the human eye (≈ 17 mm). ### Step 2: Compute the Required Retinal Displacement for Vection Empirical work by Brandt et al. establishes that linear vection requires a minimum cumulative retinal displacement $\delta_{min}$ of approximately **2–3 degrees of arc**. $$\delta_{min} = 2.5^{\circ} \text{ (empirical threshold)}$$ ### Step 3: Calculate the Time to Reach Threshold The time to accumulate this displacement: $$T_{latency} = \frac{\delta_{min}}{v_{ret}} = \frac{\delta_{min} \cdot d}{v_{world} \cdot f_{eye}}$$ ### Step 4: Express in Practical VR Engineering Units Convert $\delta_{min}$ from degrees to radians ($\delta_{min,rad} = \delta_{min} \cdot \pi / 180$) and substitute: $$\begin{aligned} T_{latency} &= \frac{(\delta_{min,rad}) \cdot d}{v_{world} \cdot f_{eye}} \\ &= \frac{(2.5 \cdot \pi / 180) \cdot d}{v_{world} \cdot 0.017} \\ &\approx \frac{0.0436 \cdot d}{v_{world} \cdot 0.017} \\ &\approx 2.56 \cdot \frac{d}{v_{world}} \text{ seconds} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5: Verification with Empirical Data For a tunnel scene with $d = 5$ m and $v_{world} = 2$ m/s: $$T_{latency} \approx 2.56 \cdot \frac{5}{2} = 6.4 \text{ seconds}$$ This aligns with experimental values of **5–8 seconds** for tunnel-style vection, validating the model. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Vection latency is **directly proportional to scene depth** and **inversely proportional to world velocity**. This is why close-up rapid motion (e.g., first-person shooter sprinting) induces vection almost instantly, often before the user can adapt. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sensory Mismatch Magnitude for Circular Motion When a user in a VR headset rotates their head with angular velocity $\omega_{head}$, the visual scene rotates at $\omega_{visual} = -\omega_{head}$ (opposite direction to maintain world stability). The vestibular system detects $\omega_{ves} = \omega_{head}$. ### Step 1: Define the Mismatch Vector for Yaw Rotation $$\vec{M}_{yaw} = \omega_{visual} \cdot \hat{y} - \omega_{ves} \cdot \hat{y}$$ ### Step 2: Substitute the Expected Relationship Under *matched* conditions, the brain expects: $$\omega_{visual}^{expected} = -\omega_{ves}$$ ### Step 3: Compute the Mismatch $$\begin{aligned} \vec{M}_{yaw} &= \omega_{visual} - \omega_{ves} \\ &= \omega_{visual} - (-\omega_{visual}^{expected}) \\ &= \omega_{visual} + \omega_{visual}^{expected} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4: Quantify the Latency in VR Rendering If the VR rendering pipeline introduces a delay $\Delta t_{render}$, the displayed visual rotation lags by: $$\omega_{visual}^{delayed} = \omega_{visual} - \dot{\omega}_{visual} \cdot \Delta t_{render}$$ ### Step 5: Final Mismatch with Rendering Delay $$\vec{M}_{yaw} = -\dot{\omega}_{visual} \cdot \Delta t_{render}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Implication:** The mismatch magnitude scales *linearly* with rendering delay. A **20 ms** end-to-end latency at $1000^{\circ}/s^2$ head angular acceleration produces a mismatch of **20°/s** — well above the human detection threshold of **~2°/s** and a guaranteed sickness trigger. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mismatched Motion Scenarios in VR | Scenario | Visual Cue | Vestibular Cue | Proprioceptive Cue | Mismatch $\vert \vec{M} \vert$ | User Experience | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Stationary VR view** | None | Still | Still | **0** | Neutral, no vection | | **Smooth walking in VR** | Forward optic flow | Still (no real motion) | Leg-movement feedback | **Medium** | Moderate vection, low sickness | | **VR driving (no motion platform)** | Forward acceleration | Still | Wheel/stick feedback | **High** | Strong vection, possible sickness | | **VR with motion platform (matched)** | Forward acceleration | Forward acceleration | Seat pressure | **~0** | Realistic, minimal sickness | | **Snap-turn (teleport)** | Sudden yaw rotation | Still | Still | **High but brief** | Disorientation spike | | **Roller-coaster VR** | Rapid pitch + roll | Still | Seat back pressure | **Very High** | Strong sickness risk | | **Ikea-style room-scale VR** | Multi-directional flow | Natural head movement | Natural walking | **Low** | Best comfort profile | | **Gaming: artificial sliding** | Continuous slide | Still | Still (feet on floor) | **Very High** | High drop-out rate | ## 3.4 Derivation 3: Cybersickness Risk Index (CSRI) We derive a unified cybersickness risk index combining mismatch magnitude, exposure duration, and user susceptibility. ### Step 1: Define Exposure Accumulation The total accumulated mismatch over an exposure session of duration $T$: $$E_{total} = \int_{0}^{T} \left\| \vec{M}(t) \right\|_2 \cdot dt$$ ### Step 2: Apply the Susceptibility Coefficient Each user has a personal susceptibility factor $k_{user}$ (range 0.5 to 2.0): $$CSRI = k_{user} \cdot E_{total} = k_{user} \cdot \int_{0}^{T} \left\| \vec{M}(t) \right\|_2 \cdot dt$$ ### Step 3: Decision Threshold A cybersickness drop-out is likely when: $$CSRI > CSRI_{threshold} \approx 1500 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$ ### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example For a 15-minute roller-coaster VR experience with average mismatch of 0.3 rad/s and $k_{user} = 1.5$: $$\begin{aligned} CSRI &= 1.5 \cdot 0.3 \cdot (15 \times 60) \\ &= 1.5 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 900 \\ &= 405 \end{aligned}$$ Result: **CSRI = 405** (below threshold; tolerable for most users). For a 30-minute session, the same content gives **CSRI = 810** (approaching risk zone). > [!TIP] > **Practical Engineering Takeaway:** Halving the mismatch magnitude (e.g., via 90 Hz refresh + motion prediction) has the same effect as halving the session length. **This is the single most cost-effective VR optimization.** ## 3.5 Summary of Derived Quantities | Derived Quantity | Symbol | Final Form | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Vection Onset Latency | $T_{latency}$ | $2.56 \cdot d/v_{world}$ | seconds | | Yaw Mismatch (with delay) | $\vec{M}_{yaw}$ | $-\dot{\omega}_{visual} \cdot \Delta t_{render}$ | rad/s | | Cybersickness Risk Index | $CSRI$ | $k_{user} \cdot \int \vert \vec{M}(t) \vert dt$ | dimensionless | | Retinal Velocity | $v_{ret}$ | $v_{world} f_{eye} / d$ | m/s | | Mismatch Threshold (Human) | $M_{detect}$ | $\approx 2^{\circ}/s \approx 0.035$ rad/s | rad/s | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Sensory Mismatch Processing Pipeline in VR ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Virtual Scene Renderer
HMD Display] --> B[Visual Sensory Input
Optic Flow Field] C[Vestibular System
Inner Ear Otoliths and Canals] --> D[Vestibular Signal] E[Proprioceptive System
Muscles and Joints] --> F[Proprioceptive Signal] B --> G[Central Comparator
Neural Internal Model] D --> G F --> G G --> H{Mismatch Detected} H -->|No| I[Stable Perception
No Vection Induced] H -->|Yes| J[Mismatch Vector M of t] J --> K[Vection Self Motion Illusion] J --> L[Cybersickness Response] K --> M[Perceived Immersion Gain] L --> N[User Dropout Risk] M --> O[VR Experience Quality] N --> O ``` ## 4.2 Diagram: Vection Induction Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Visual Stimulus Onset] --> Q[Compute Retinal Velocity vret] Q --> R{vret exceeds
Threshold 2 deg per s} R -->|No| S[No Vection
Scene interpreted as object motion] R -->|Yes| T[Optic Flow Pattern Recognized] T --> U[Pattern Type Check] U -->|Radial Expansion| V[Forward Self Motion Vection] U -->|Radial Contraction| W[Backward Self Motion Vection] U -->|Lambertian Translation| X[Lateral Linear Vection] U -->|Rotational Drift| Y[Circular Vection] V --> Z[Compute Vection Strength VS] W --> Z X --> Z Y --> Z Z --> AA{VS exceeds 0.5} AA -->|Yes| AB[Full Vection Achieved] AA -->|No| AC[Weak Vection Phase] AB --> AD[Monitor Mismatch Magnitude] AC --> AD AD --> AE{Mismatch below
Comfort Threshold} AE -->|Yes| AF[Stable Immersive State] AE -->|No| AG[Trigger Cybersickness
Mitigation Protocols] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Cybersickness Mitigation Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Head Tracking Input] --> B[Pose Prediction Filter
Kalman or Asynchronous Spacewarp] B --> C[Scene Rendering Pipeline] C --> D[HMD Display Output] D --> E[User Visual System] E --> F[Real Time Mismatch Estimator] F --> G{Mismatch above
Critical Threshold} G -->|No| H[Normal Operation Mode] G -->|Yes| I[Activate Comfort Modes] I --> J1[Dynamic FOV Reduction] I --> J2[Reduce Visual Velocity] I --> J3[Add Visual Anchor Points] I --> J4[Trigger Teleport Locomotion] J1 --> K[User Comfort Preserved] J2 --> K J3 --> K J4 --> K H --> K K --> L[Session Continuation] ``` ## 4.4 Diagram: Vection Types and Their Stimulus Geometry ```mermaid graph TD V[Vection Phenomena] --> V1[Linear Vection] V --> V2[Circular Vection] V --> V3[Roll Vection] V --> V4[Pitch Vection] V1 --> V1a[Forward Optic Flow] V1 --> V1b[Lateral Optic Flow] V1 --> V1c[Vertical Optic Flow] V2 --> V2a[Yaw Rotation Stimulus] V3 --> V3a[Banked Turn Stimulus] V4 --> V4a[Climb or Dive Stimulus] V1a --> APP1[VR Walking Simulators] V1b --> APP2[VR Driving Simulators] V1c --> APP3[VR Climbing Simulators] V2a --> APP4[VR Space Disorientation Trainers] V3a --> APP5[VR Flight Simulators] V4a --> APP6[VR Roller Coaster Experiences] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "explain the architecture of vection processing," sketch a 3-block chain: *(1) Visual Input → (2) Neural Comparator → (3) Perceptual Output (Vection OR Sickness)*. Marks are awarded for correctly identifying the comparator's role as a *prediction-error detector*. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Vection. List its four principal types. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Vection is the illusion of self-motion experienced by an observer who is physically stationary, induced by a coherent large-field visual motion stimulus that the brain interprets as ego-motion. **Four Principal Types (2 Marks):** 1. **Linear Vection** — perception of translational self-motion (forward, backward, lateral, vertical). 2. **Circular Vection** — perception of rotational self-motion about the vertical (yaw) axis. 3. **Roll Vection** — perception of rotation about the front-to-back (longitudinal) axis. 4. **Pitch Vection** — perception of rotation about the lateral (ear-to-ear) axis. --- ### Question 2: What is meant by "Mismatched Motion" in a VR context? State the Sensory Conflict Equation. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model] **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Meaning (1.5 Marks):** Mismatched motion refers to a *sensorimotor conflict* in which the motion information delivered through the visual channel of a VR system is inconsistent with the motion signals from the vestibular, proprioceptive, or somatosensory systems. This conflict is the principal physiological trigger for VR-induced cybersickness. **Sensory Conflict Equation (1.5 Marks):** $$\vec{M}(t) = \vec{S}_{visual}(t) - \vec{S}_{vestibular}(t) - \vec{S}_{proprioceptive}(t)$$ A non-zero $\vec{M}(t)$ whose magnitude exceeds the human detection threshold (≈ 2°/s) produces vection and, if sustained, cybersickness. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A: Explain Mismatched Motion and its role in inducing Cybersickness in VR. Discuss the Sensory Conflict Theory with the necessary mathematical model, and enumerate at least four engineering mitigation techniques. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted] **Model Answer:** #### (a) Definition and Physiological Basis of Mismatched Motion [7 Marks] **Definition (2 Marks):** Mismatched motion in VR is the *discrepancy between the visual motion signal rendered to the user via the HMD and the actual motion (or absence of motion) detected by the vestibular and proprioceptive systems*. The brain, expecting these signals to agree, registers a sensory conflict. **Physiological Basis (3 Marks):** - The **vestibular apparatus** (semicircular canals + otolith organs) detects rotational and linear accelerations. Its threshold is ≈ **2°/s²** for rotation. - The **visual system** detects motion through optic flow and has a lower threshold (≈ **2°/s** for visual field motion). - When a user is visually moving in VR but physically stationary, **only the visual system activates**, and the vestibular remains silent — creating the mismatch. - The **central nervous system** interprets this conflict as *neural poison*, triggering autonomic responses (pallor, cold sweat, nausea, vomiting). **Mathematical Model (2 Marks):** $$\vec{M}(t) = \vec{S}_{visual}(t) - \vec{S}_{vestibular}(t) - \vec{S}_{proprioceptive}(t)$$ The cumulative exposure: $$CSRI = k_{user} \cdot \int_{0}^{T} \left\| \vec{M}(t) \right\|_2 \cdot dt$$ When $CSRI > 1500$, cybersickness drop-out is statistically likely. #### (b) Sensory Conflict Theory & Engineering Mitigation Techniques [7 Marks] **Sensory Conflict Theory (3 Marks):** Proposed by Reason and Brand (1975), the theory states that motion sickness arises when the *incoming pattern* of sensory signals does not match any *expected pattern* stored in the brain's internal model. The brain treats this mismatch as a sensory error and initiates an emetic (vomiting) response — an evolutionary defense against neurotoxin ingestion (Poison Theory of Motion Sickness). **Four Engineering Mitigation Techniques (4 Marks — 1 Each):** 1. **Teleportation-Based Locomotion:** The user "teleports" short distances; no continuous visual motion is presented, so mismatch $\vec{M}(t) \approx 0$. 2. **Dynamic Field-of-View (FOV) Reduction:** When the user's head rotates rapidly, the rendered FOV is automatically narrowed. This reduces retinal velocity and lowers $\vert \vec{M}(t) \vert$. 3. **Asynchronous Spacewarp / Motion Prediction:** Predictive rendering reduces end-to-end motion-to-photon latency from ~20 ms to <11 ms, cutting mismatch magnitude by nearly 50%. 4. **Snap-Turn (Discrete Rotation):** Instead of continuous yaw, the user rotates in 30° discrete jumps with brief blackouts, eliminating the sustained visual-vestibular yaw conflict. --- ### Question B: Define Vection. Derive the expression for Vection Onset Latency from optic flow principles. Discuss the role of FOV, visual velocity, and scene depth in determining vection strength, with reference to its engineering implications in VR locomotion design. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted] **Model Answer:** #### (a) Vection Definition and Derivation of Onset Latency [7 Marks] **Definition (2 Marks):** Vection is the illusion of self-motion perceived when a stationary observer is exposed to a coherent large-field visual motion stimulus. It is the foundational perceptual mechanism underlying immersive VR locomotion. **Derivation (5 Marks):** For a Lambertian point at depth $d$ translating with world velocity $v_{world}$, the retinal image velocity is: $$v_{ret} = \frac{v_{world} \cdot f_{eye}}{d}$$ Empirically, vection requires a minimum cumulative retinal displacement $\delta_{min} = 2.5^{\circ}$. Converting to radians: $$\delta_{min,rad} = \frac{2.5 \pi}{180} \approx 0.0436 \text{ rad}$$ The vection onset latency is the time to accumulate this displacement: $$T_{latency} = \frac{\delta_{min,rad}}{v_{ret}} = \frac{\delta_{min,rad} \cdot d}{v_{world} \cdot f_{eye}}$$ Substituting $\delta_{min,rad} \approx 0.0436$ and $f_{eye} = 0.017$ m: $$\begin{aligned} T_{latency} &\approx \frac{0.0436 \cdot d}{v_{world} \cdot 0.017} \\ &\approx 2.56 \cdot \frac{d}{v_{world}} \text{ seconds} \end{aligned}$$ **Verification:** For a tunnel scene with $d = 5$ m, $v_{world} = 2$ m/s: $$T_{latency} \approx 2.56 \cdot \frac{5}{2} = 6.4 \text{ s}$$ This matches empirical values of 5–8 seconds. ✓ #### (b) Role of FOV, Velocity, and Depth in VR Locomotion Design [7 Marks] 1. **Field of View (FOV) Impact (2 Marks):** A larger FOV covers more of the visual field, producing greater optic flow coverage and faster vection onset. Reducing FOV (e.g., from 110° to 60°) significantly *delays* vection and reduces mismatch, which is why "comfort modes" dynamically narrow the FOV during rapid motion. 2. **Visual Velocity Impact (2 Marks):** Vection strength $VS$ is directly proportional to retinal image velocity $v_{ret}$, which scales linearly with $v_{world}$. Doubling the apparent walking speed halves $T_{latency}$ but doubles the mismatch magnitude — a direct engineering trade-off between immersion and comfort. 3. **Scene Depth Impact (2 Marks):** Closer objects (small $d$) produce much higher retinal velocity, accelerating vection onset. This is why VR scenes with nearby walls or props induce faster, more intense vection than scenes set in open landscapes. 4. **VR Locomotion Design Synthesis (1 Mark):** Optimal locomotion systems use a *blend* of techniques — for example, a slow visual velocity combined with subtle haptic footstep feedback, achieving high vection strength with low $CSRI$. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):** > 1. **Confusing Vection with Cybersickness** — Vection is the *illusion of self-motion* (desired in VR); cybersickness is the *adverse physiological response*. They are *related but distinct*. Examiners WILL deduct marks for using them as synonyms. > 2. **Forgetting the Vestibular Threshold** — When stating mismatch causes, students often forget to mention the **~2°/s² vestibular threshold**. Always state both the visual and vestibular thresholds in any "explain mismatch" answer. > 3. **Missing the Mitigation Counterpart** — A 14-mark question on cybersickness **always** expects 2–3 marks of mitigation content. Describing only the cause and skipping the engineering solution is a guaranteed 3–4 mark loss. > 4. **Wrong Formula Substitution** — In derivation questions, the final numerical verification step is worth 1 mark.
