Students who skip the verification step (e.g., computing $T_{latency} = 6.4$ s for $d=5$, $v=2$) lose this easy mark. > 5. **Misdirection on "Vection Strength"** — $VS$ is a *percentage* (0–100%), not a unitless number on a 0–10 scale. Use the **Bojko formula** explicitly. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mismatched Motion & Vection (Module 3)** > > ✅ **Vection** = *illusion of self-motion with no real movement*; the cornerstone VR perception. > > ✅ **Mismatched motion** = *sensory conflict* between visual, vestibular, and proprioceptive channels. > > ✅ Four vection types: **Linear, Circular, Roll, Pitch** — memorize all four. > > ✅ The **Sensory Conflict Equation** is $\vec{M}(t) = \vec{S}_{visual} - \vec{S}_{vestibular} - \vec{S}_{proprioceptive}$. > > ✅ Visual motion threshold ≈ **2°/s**; vestibular threshold ≈ **2°/s²** — vision is the *dominant* cue. > > ✅ **Vection Latency** $T_{latency} \approx 2.56 \cdot d / v_{world}$ — directly proportional to depth, inversely to velocity. > > ✅ **Vection Strength (Bojko)**: $VS = (R_{imagined}/R_{real}) \times 100\%$. > > ✅ **Cybersickness Risk Index**: $CSRI = k_{user} \cdot \int \vert \vec{M}(t) \vert \, dt$, with threshold ≈ 1500. > > ✅ **SSQ (Kennedy)**: $SSQ_{total} = 7.543N + 7.174O + 13.924D$ — three subscales: Nausea, Oculomotor, Disorientation. > > ✅ Mitigation techniques: **Teleportation, Snap-Turn, Dynamic FOV, Asynchronous Spacewarp, Comfort Vignette, Visual Anchors**. > > ✅ The **Sensory Conflict Theory (Reason & Brand 1975)** and the **Poison Theory of Motion Sickness (Oman)** are the two most cited theoretical models — mention both. > > ✅ Engineering rule of thumb: **Doubling mismatch magnitude has the same effect as doubling session length.** > > ✅ Always cite **both cause and mitigation** in 14-mark answers.

topics.title: Visual Perception - Perception of Depth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT504 - VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Visual Perception - Perception of Depth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Depth Perception** is the visual ability to perceive the relative distance of three-dimensional (3D) objects in the environment and to judge their spatial relationship to the observer. In the context of **Virtual Reality (VR)**, depth perception refers to the engineering of sensory stimuli (visual, vestibular, proprioceptive) that convincingly reproduce these psychophysical cues so that the user's brain interprets the rendered 2D screen content as a true 3D space.

The human visual system (HVS) accomplishes this remarkable task by combining **physiological signals** generated inside the eye, **neural processing** in the visual cortex, and **learned assumptions** about the structure of the physical world. These signals are formally known as **Depth Cues**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **reaching out to pick up a coffee mug on your desk** while simultaneously answering a video call. You do not consciously calculate anything, yet your hand lands precisely on the handle. Why?

- Your brain triangulates the mug using the **two slightly different views** from your left and right eyes (binocular cue).
- It checks how bent the lens of your eye has to become to focus on the mug versus the laptop screen (oculomotor cue).
- It recognizes that the laptop partially hides the wall behind it (pictorial cue).
- It watches how the mug shifts relative to the window as you tilt your head (motion cue).

All of this happens **in parallel, within ~150 milliseconds**, producing a single unified percept of "depth." VR engineers must reconstruct each of these cues artificially — a failure to do so causes cybersickness, mis-scaled worlds, or the famous "flat-looking" 3D TV effect.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

The following constants and metrics are **high-yield** for KTU examinations:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value |
|--------|----------|----------------|
| **IPD** | Inter-Pupillary Distance | **63 mm** (range 50–75 mm) |
| **f_eye** | Focal length of human eye lens | **~17 mm** |
| **d_near** | Near point of distinct vision | **25 cm** |
| **d_far** | Far point of distinct vision | **∞ (theoretically)** |
| **FOV_h** | Horizontal binocular field of view | **~200°** (with eye motion) |
| **FOV_s** | Static horizontal field of view | **~120°** (stereoscopic overlap) |
| **α_res** | Visual acuity / angular resolution | **~1 arc-minute (1/60°)** |
| **f_critical** | Critical flicker fusion threshold | **~60 Hz** (peripheral), **~90 Hz** (foveal) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in VR:** The values of **IPD**, **f_eye**, and **d_near** directly determine how a Head-Mounted Display (HMD) must render stereoscopic images. If the HMD's focal plane and convergence distance are mismatched, the user experiences the **vergence–accommodation conflict**, the single largest source of VR-induced eyestrain.

### 1.4 Classification of Depth Cues (High-Level Map)

```
Visual Depth Cues
├── Physiological (Oculomotor)
│   ├── Accommodation (lens focus)
│   └── Convergence (eye rotation inward)
├── Binocular (Two-Eye)
│   ├── Retinal / Binocular Disparity
│   └── Stereopsis
└── Pictorial / Monocular (Single-Eye)
    ├── Occlusion (interposition)
    ├── Relative Size
    ├── Linear Perspective
    ├── Texture Gradient
    ├── Aerial (Atmospheric) Perspective
    ├── Shading & Shadows
    ├── Height in Visual Field
    └── Motion Parallax
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Oculomotor Cues (Physiological)

These cues originate from the **muscles inside the eye itself** and therefore provide feedback even when the head and the world are completely still.

#### 2.1.1 Accommodation
- **What it is:** The ciliary muscles change the curvature (and therefore the focal length) of the eye's lens to bring objects at different distances into sharp focus on the retina.
- **How it works:** A near object requires a **thicker, more convex** lens (high refractive power). A far object requires a **flatter** lens. The brain monitors the ciliary muscle tension and uses it as a coarse depth signal — accurate up to roughly **2 meters**.
- **Why it matters in VR:** A standard HMD displays its pixels on a flat panel at a fixed optical distance (typically 1.5–2 m from the eye). The eye therefore accommodates to a constant distance even when the user converges on a near virtual object — a fundamental cue conflict.

#### 2.1.2 Convergence
- **What it is:** Both eyes rotate inward (toward the nose) to fixate on a single near point. The greater the inward rotation, the nearer the object.
- **How it works:** The brain senses the angle between the two optical axes using proprioceptors in the extraocular muscles. Convergence is highly accurate for objects within **~6 meters**; beyond that, the angle becomes too small to discriminate.
- **Why it matters in VR:** Stereoscopic rendering in an HMD requires the eyes to converge on a near virtual object while accommodating to the physically distant screen → this is the **vergence–accommodation conflict**.

### 2.2 Binocular Cues (Two-Eye)

#### 2.2.1 Retinal (Binocular) Disparity
- **What it is:** Because the two eyes are separated by the **IPD (~63 mm)**, each eye receives a slightly different 2D projection of the 3D world. The pixel-level difference between the left-eye image and the right-eye image is called the **disparity**.
- **How it works:** The brain fuses the two disparate images in the **visual cortex (V1/V2)** and uses the magnitude of disparity to compute stereoscopic depth.
- **Why it matters in VR:** This is the single most exploited depth cue in HMDs. Rendering the scene twice — once per eye — with a small horizontal offset produces a vivid sense of depth within the **Panum's fusional area** (~10 arc-minutes of disparity).

#### 2.2.2 Stereopsis
- **What it is:** The perceptual phenomenon that **arises from** retinal disparity. It is the conscious experience of solidity and 3D shape that the brain constructs.
- **How it works:** Disparities are classified as:
  - **Zero disparity** → object at the **horopter** (the fixation plane).
  - **Crossed disparity** → object in **front of** the horopter (nearer than fixation).
  - **Uncrossed disparity** → object **behind** the horopter (farther than fixation).

### 2.3 Pictorial / Monocular Cues (Single-Eye)

These are the cues an artist uses on a flat canvas to create the illusion of depth.

| Cue | Mechanism | Strongest Range |
|-----|-----------|-----------------|
| **Occlusion (Interposition)** | A near object blocks part of a far object | All distances |
| **Relative Size** | Farther object projects a smaller retinal image | All distances |
| **Linear Perspective** | Parallel lines converge to a vanishing point | Outdoor / large scale |
| **Texture Gradient** | Texture elements become denser and smaller with distance | Mid-range |
| **Aerial Perspective** | Distant objects lose contrast and shift toward atmospheric hue | >100 m |
| **Shading & Shadows** | Light gradients and cast shadows imply surface orientation | All distances |
| **Height in Visual Field** | Objects higher in the image are perceived as farther | Ground plane only |
| **Motion Parallax** | Closer objects move faster across the retina than farther ones | All distances (when moving) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pictorial cues are essential in 2D screens, movies, and CAVE walls** (monoscopic VR). Even without stereo, the brain can construct a compelling depth ordering using just shading, occlusion, and perspective. Most 3D rendering algorithms (Phong, ray tracing) exist primarily to deliver high-quality pictorial cues.

### 2.4 Motion Parallax (Bridge Between Pictorial and Oculomotor)

- **What it is:** As the observer translates sideways, near objects sweep across the retina faster than far objects. The brain interprets this velocity gradient as depth.
- **How it works:** This is the body's **biological LIDAR**: retinal image velocity is inversely proportional to depth.
- **Why it matters in VR:** Modern HMDs include **6-DoF (six degrees-of-freedom) head tracking** precisely to deliver high-fidelity motion parallax. The famous "VR is more immersive than 3D movies" claim is largely because 3D movies lack motion parallax — the head is locked to the seat.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the formulas below — these appear frequently in KTU Part A and Part B questions on this topic.

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\theta = \dfrac{d}{D}$ (radians) | Visual angle subtended by an object of size $d$ at distance $D$ |
| 2 | $\delta = \dfrac{\text{IPD} \cdot (Z_f - Z_x)}{Z_f \cdot Z_x}$ | Binocular disparity $\delta$ for a point at depth $Z_x$ fixing on a plane at $Z_f$ |
| 3 | $v_{\text{retinal}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{head}}}{Z}$ | Retinal image velocity during translation (motion parallax) |
| 4 | $\Delta C = \dfrac{1}{Z_{\text{near}}} - \dfrac{1}{Z_{\text{far}}}$ | Diopter change between two accommodative targets |
| 5 | $S_{\text{stereo}} \approx \dfrac{\text{IPD}^2 \cdot Z}{f_{\text{eye}} \cdot (Z - \text{IPD})^2}$ | Stereo sensitivity (parallax per unit depth) |
| 6 | $\text{Panum's limit} \approx \pm 10$ arc-min | Range within which fusion succeeds without diplopia |
| 7 | $D_{\text{depth}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{eye}}^2}{Z^2} \cdot \Delta Z$ | Depth of field on the retina for an axial slice $\Delta Z$ |
| 8 | $f_{\text{refresh}} \geq 2 \cdot f_{\text{critical}}$ | Sampling theorem applied to flicker fusion |
| 9 | $t_{\text{photon}} = 100$ ms | Persistence-of-vision window for motion perception |
| 10 | $\alpha_{\text{cone}} = \dfrac{p}{Z}$ | Angular size of a pixel of size $p$ viewed from distance $Z$ |

> **NOTE ON NOTATION:** Where $Z$ denotes the depth of an object from the eye (positive going into the scene), IPD is the inter-pupillary distance in meters, and $f_{\text{eye}}$ is the focal length of the eye in meters.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility in VR

| VR Subsystem | Depth Cue Served |
|--------------|------------------|
| Dual-display HMD optics | Retinal disparity / stereopsis |
| Per-eye viewport calculation using IPD | Convergence & stereopsis |
| Physics-based rendering (PBR) shaders | Pictorial cues: shading & shadows |
| 6-DoF head tracking (IMU + cameras) | Motion parallax |
| Eye-tracking with varifocal / multifocal displays | Accommodation |
| Foveated rendering | Reduces compute while preserving pictorial cues in fovea |
| Anti-aliasing (MSAA, TAA) | Pictorial cue: smooth gradients |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Binocular Disparity Formula

**Problem Setup (Classic KTU Question):**
> A person with an Inter-Pupillary Distance of **IPD = 6.3 cm** fixates on a point F at depth $Z_f$ from the cyclopean eye. A second point X lies at depth $Z_x$ on the median plane. Derive the expression for the binocular disparity $\delta$ of point X.

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.**
Place the cyclopean eye at the origin. The left and right eyes are at $x = -\text{IPD}/2$ and $x = +\text{IPD}/2$ respectively, on the horizontal axis.

**Step 2 — Compute the angle subtended at each eye.**
The horizontal angle from the optical axis of the **left** eye to the point X at depth $Z_x$ on the midline ($x=0$) is:

$$
\alpha_L = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\text{IPD}/2}{Z_x}\right)
$$

Similarly, the angle at the **right** eye is:

$$
\alpha_R = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\text{IPD}/2}{Z_x}\right) = \alpha_L
$$

By symmetry, the angles are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign.

**Step 3 — Compute the corresponding angles to the fixation point F.**
Repeating the same construction for the fixation point at depth $Z_f$:

$$
\beta_L = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\text{IPD}/2}{Z_f}\right)
$$

$$
\beta_R = -\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\text{IPD}/2}{Z_f}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Define disparity.**
Disparity is the **difference in angular position** of point X relative to point F, as seen by the two eyes:

$$
\delta = (\alpha_R - \beta_R) - (\alpha_L - \beta_L)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify.**
Since $\alpha_L = -\alpha_R$ and $\beta_L = -\beta_R$ (midline points), this collapses to:

$$
\delta = 2\left(\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\text{IPD}/2}{Z_x}\right) - \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\text{IPD}/2}{Z_f}\right)\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the small-angle approximation** (valid because $\text{IPD} \ll Z$ in typical viewing):

$$
\delta \approx \dfrac{\text{IPD}}{Z_x} - \dfrac{\text{IPD}}{Z_f}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\delta \approx \text{IPD} \left(\dfrac{1}{Z_x} - \dfrac{1}{Z_f}\right) = \dfrac{\text{IPD}\,(Z_f - Z_x)}{Z_x \, Z_f}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** If $Z_x < Z_f$ (X is closer than the fixation plane), $\delta > 0$ → **crossed disparity**. If $Z_x > Z_f$, $\delta < 0$ → **uncrossed disparity**. When $Z_x = Z_f$, disparity vanishes — X lies on the **horopter**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Motion Parallax Retinal Velocity

**Step 1 — Set up the moving observer.**
An observer translates horizontally with velocity $V_{\text{head}}$ and fixates on a point at depth $Z$.

**Step 2 — The retinal image of the point moves.**
The retinal image velocity $v_{\text{retinal}}$ is the angular velocity of the line of sight to the point:

$$
v_{\text{retinal}} = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{x(t)}{Z}\right)\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate, with $Z$ held constant and $x(t) = V_{\text{head}} t$:**

$$
v_{\text{retinal}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (x/Z)^2} \cdot \dfrac{V_{\text{head}}}{Z}
$$

**Step 4 — Small-angle approximation** ($x \ll Z$, so the denominator $\approx 1$):

$$
\boxed{\;v_{\text{retinal}} \approx \dfrac{V_{\text{head}}}{Z}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** Retinal velocity is **inversely proportional to depth**. A near point at 1 m moves across the retina 10× faster than a point at 10 m. The brain's visual system extracts depth as:

$$
Z \approx \dfrac{V_{\text{head}}}{v_{\text{retinal}}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Dioptric Demand of Accommodation

**Step 1 — Define the diopter.**
The optical power $P$ of the eye (in diopters, D) is the reciprocal of the focal length in meters:

$$
P = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{eye}}}
$$

**Step 2 — For a normal eye focusing on a target at distance $Z$ (in meters):**

$$
P(Z) = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{eye}}} + \dfrac{1}{Z}
$$

The first term is the relaxed power of the cornea + lens; the second term is the additional power supplied by accommodation.

**Step 3 — The diopter change between two targets** at $Z_{\text{near}}$ and $Z_{\text{far}}$ is therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta P = \dfrac{1}{Z_{\text{near}}} - \dfrac{1}{Z_{\text{far}}}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example for VR:**
A user looks at a virtual object 0.5 m away, but the HMD screen is optically 2.0 m away. The accommodation conflict is:

$$
\Delta P_{\text{conflict}} = \dfrac{1}{0.5} - \dfrac{1}{2.0} = 2.0 - 0.5 = 1.5\ \text{D}
$$

A 1.5 D mismatch is roughly the accommodation demand of switching from a distant scene to a book held at arm's length — a substantial and tiring demand, explaining VR eye fatigue.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Strength of Depth Cues

| Depth Cue | Near / Far | Static / Dynamic | VR Delivery | Strength |
|-----------|------------|------------------|-------------|----------|
| Convergence | < 6 m | Static | Stereo render | ★★★★★ |
| Accommodation | < 2 m | Static | Fixed / varifocal optics | ★★★ |
| Retinal Disparity | < 100 m | Static | Stereo render | ★★★★★ |
| Occlusion | All | Both | Z-buffer | ★★★★★ |
| Linear Perspective | All | Static | Camera FOV | ★★★★ |
| Texture Gradient | Mid | Static | Mipmapping | ★★★ |
| Aerial Perspective | Far | Static | Fog / atmospheric shaders | ★★ |
| Shadows | All | Both | Real-time shadow maps | ★★★★ |
| Motion Parallax | All | Dynamic | 6-DoF tracking | ★★★★★ |
| Height in Field | Ground | Static | Camera pitch | ★★★ |

### 3.5 Worked Example (KTU-style Numerical)

> **Q:** A user with an IPD of 6.4 cm fixates on a virtual object 2.0 m away. A second object appears on the median plane at 0.8 m. Compute (a) the binocular disparity in radians, (b) whether the disparity is crossed or uncrossed, and (c) the required diopter change if the user then shifts gaze to an object at 5.0 m.

**Solution:**

**(a) Disparity:**

$$
\delta = \dfrac{\text{IPD}\,(Z_f - Z_x)}{Z_x \, Z_f} = \dfrac{0.064 \times (2.0 - 0.8)}{0.8 \times 2.0}
$$

$$
\delta = \dfrac{0.064 \times 1.2}{1.6} = \dfrac{0.0768}{1.6} = 0.048\ \text{rad}
$$

Converting to arc-minutes (1 rad = 3438 arc-min):

$$
\delta = 0.048 \times 3438 \approx 165\ \text{arc-min}
$$

This exceeds Panum's limit (~10 arc-min), so this would cause **diplopia** in real life; in VR it would simply feel "too far off" to be fused.

**(b) Sign:** $Z_x < Z_f$, so $\delta > 0$ → **crossed disparity** (object appears in front of fixation).

**(c) Diopter change** from 0.8 m to 5.0 m:

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{1}{0.8} - \dfrac{1}{5.0} = 1.25 - 0.20 = 1.05\ \text{D}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Visual Depth Perception]:::root --> B[Oculomotor Cues]:::branch
    A --> C[Binocular Cues]:::branch
    A --> D[Pictorial Monocular Cues]:::branch
    A --> E[Motion Based Cues]:::branch

    B --> B1[Accommodation - Lens Focus]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Convergence - Eye Rotation]:::leaf

    C --> C1[Retinal Disparity]:::leaf
    C --> C2[Stereopsis]:::leaf

    D --> D1[Occlusion]:::leaf
    D --> D2[Relative Size]:::leaf
    D --> D3[Linear Perspective]:::leaf
    D --> D4[Texture Gradient]:::leaf
    D --> D5[Aerial Perspective]:::leaf
    D --> D6[Shading and Shadows]:::leaf
    D --> D7[Height in Visual Field]:::leaf

    E --> E1[Motion Parallax]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0b1d33,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef branch fill:#2e7d8a,stroke:#1b4f5c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#dfeef0,stroke:#1b4f5c,stroke-width:1px,color:#0b1d33
```

### 4.2 VR Rendering Pipeline Tied to Depth Cues (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Scene Input]
        S1[3D Geometry Mesh]:::src
        S2[Textures and Materials]:::src
        S3[Light Sources]:::src
        S4[Head Pose 6DoF]:::src
    end

    subgraph PROC[Perceptual Rendering Engine]
        P1[View Frustum Culling]:::proc
        P2[Stereo Viewport Split IPD]:::proc
        P3[Projective Transform]:::proc
        P4[Z Buffer Occlusion]:::proc
        P5[Shadow Map Generation]:::proc
        P6[Mipmap Texture Filtering]:::proc
        P7[Atmospheric Fog Pass]:::proc
        P8[Motion Parallax Update]:::proc
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Stage]
        O1[Left Eye Framebuffer]:::out
        O2[Right Eye Framebuffer]:::out
        O3[HMD Display]:::out
        O4[User Brain Fuses Cues]:::out
    end

    S1 --> P1
    S2 --> P6
    S3 --> P5
    S4 --> P1
    S4 --> P2
    S4 --> P8
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P3 --> P6
    P3 --> P7
    P5 --> P3
    P6 --> P3
    P7 --> P3
    P8 --> P3
    P3 --> O1
    P3 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> O4

    classDef src fill:#f4d35e,stroke:#7a5a00,stroke-width:1px,color:#0b1d33
    classDef proc fill:#5e9bcf,stroke:#1b4f5c,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#9bc474,stroke:#3a5a1c,stroke-width:1px,color:#0b1d33
```

### 4.3 Vergence–Accommodation Conflict (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N[Natural Viewing]:::nat --> N1[Eye converges AND accommodates to SAME distance]:::nat
    H[Standard HMD Viewing]:::hmd --> H1[Eye converges to NEAR virtual object]:::hmd
    H1 --> H2[Eye accommodates to FAR screen plane]:::hmd
    H2 --> H3[Conflict: 0.5 to 2.0 diopter mismatch]:::hmd
    H3 --> H4[Cybersickness and Eyestrain]:::hmd

    V[Varifocal HMD Fix]:::fix --> V1[Eye tracking measures gaze depth]:::fix
    V1 --> V2[Optics physically shift focal plane]:::fix
    V2 --> V3[Convergence and Accommodation REALIGN]:::fix

    classDef nat fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#3a5a1c,stroke-width:1px,color:#0b1d33
    classDef hmd fill:#f7b9b9,stroke:#7a1f1f,stroke-width:1px,color:#0b1d33
    classDef fix fill:#bbd6f0,stroke:#1b4f5c,stroke-width:1px,color:#0b1d33
```

### 4.4 Panum's Fusional Area (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Point at exact fixation distance Zf]:::hor --> A1[Zero disparity ON the horopter]:::hor

    B[Point slightly in front Zx less than Zf]:::cross --> B1[Crossed disparity fused into 3D]:::cross

    C[Point slightly behind Zx greater than Zf]:::uncross --> C1[Uncrossed disparity fused into 3D]:::uncross

    D[Point far in front or behind]:::out --> D1[Diplopia double vision]:::out

    classDef hor fill:#dfeef0,stroke:#1b4f5c,color:#0b1d33
    classDef cross fill:#fde2c4,stroke:#7a4a00,color:#0b1d33
    classDef uncross fill:#cde7f5,stroke:#1b4f5c,color:#0b1d33
    classDef out fill:#f7b9b9,stroke:#7a1f1f,color:#0b1d33
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> Differentiate between **accommodation** and **convergence** as depth cues. State the working range of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Accommodation | Convergence |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| Source | Ciliary muscle action (lens curvature) | Extraocular muscle action (eye rotation) |
| Signal | Change in lens focal length | Angle between optical axes |
| Working range | **0.25 m to ~2 m** (effective) | **0.25 m to ~6 m** (effective) |
| Failure mode | Presbyopia (lens stiffens with age) | Strabismus (misaligned eyes) |

Both cues degrade rapidly with distance and become useless beyond 6–10 m. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series A]**
> What is the **vergence–accommodation conflict** in VR? Why does it cause discomfort?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **vergence–accommodation conflict** is the mismatch between two depth cues that the visual system normally experiences in **synchrony**:

1. **Vergence** — the eyes converge (rotate inward) to fixate on a near virtual object, signalling that the object is close.
2. **Accommodation** — the eye's lens focuses on the **physical HMD screen** which is at a fixed distance (~1.5–2 m).

Because (1) and (2) demand **different optical states simultaneously**, the brain receives contradictory information, leading to:
- Eyestrain and headaches
- Nausea and cybersickness
- Long-term vision issues in heavy users

Solutions include **varifocal**, **multifocal**, and **light-field** HMDs. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (KTU 2024 ESE):** Answer **any one** full question from the choice given. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. Use neat diagrams wherever specified.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Coverage of Depth Cues

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Classify the various **visual depth cues** perceived by the human visual system. Explain any **three pictorial depth cues** with neat diagrams. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for **binocular disparity** for a point on the median plane in terms of IPD and the depths of the fixation and target points. A person with an IPD of 6.4 cm fixates on an object 1.5 m away. Find the disparity (in arc-minutes) of a point at 2.5 m on the median plane. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of depth cues (3 marks for classification, 4 marks for explanation of three pictorial cues):**

**Classification (already shown in SECTION 1):**

1. **Oculomotor cues:** accommodation, convergence.
2. **Binocular cues:** retinal disparity, stereopsis.
3. **Pictorial (monocular) cues:** occlusion, relative size, linear perspective, texture gradient, aerial perspective, shading, shadows, height in visual field.
4. **Motion-based cues:** motion parallax.

**Three pictorial cues explained:**

**1. Occlusion (Interposition):** When a near object partially covers a far object, the brain instantly perceives the covering object as closer. It is the **strongest** pictorial cue and works at all distances. Eg: A tree in front of a mountain.

**2. Linear Perspective:** Parallel lines in 3D (railway tracks, road edges) appear to converge to a single **vanishing point** in the 2D image. The brain interprets converging lines as receding into the distance. Strong in architectural and outdoor scenes.

**3. Texture Gradient:** A uniformly textured surface (e.g., a tiled floor) projects texture elements that become **smaller and denser** as the surface recedes from the viewer. The gradient of this density change conveys depth. Useful for mid-range ground planes.

**Diagrams:** Refer to SECTION 4 (Mermaid block diagrams) for a graphical classification. In written exams, draw:
- Two rectangles, one overlapping the other → **Occlusion**.
- Two converging lines meeting at a vanishing point → **Linear Perspective**.
- A tiled floor with progressively smaller tiles → **Texture Gradient**.

**(b) Derivation and numerical (7 marks):**

**Derivation (5 marks)** — Follow the steps in SECTION 3.1 exactly. Final result:

$$
\delta = \dfrac{\text{IPD}\,(Z_f - Z_x)}{Z_x \, Z_f}
$$

**Numerical (2 marks):** Given IPD = 0.064 m, $Z_f = 1.5$ m, $Z_x = 2.5$ m:

$$
\delta = \dfrac{0.064 \times (1.5 - 2.5)}{2.5 \times 1.5} = \dfrac{0.064 \times (-1.0)}{3.75}
$$

$$
\delta = -0.01707\ \text{rad}
$$

Converting to arc-minutes (1 rad ≈ 3438 arc-min):

$$
\delta = -0.01707 \times 3438 \approx -58.7\ \text{arc-min}
$$

The negative sign indicates **uncrossed disparity** — the point at 2.5 m appears behind the fixation plane at 1.5 m. **(7 marks total)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice (Depth Cues and VR)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, Module 4 Alternate]**

> **(a)** With suitable diagrams, explain the concepts of **retinal disparity, stereopsis, and Panum's fusional area**. Discuss how these cues are implemented in a modern HMD. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the depth cue **motion parallax** with the underlying mathematics. Discuss why head tracking in a 6-DoF VR system is essential for delivering this cue. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Retinal disparity, stereopsis, Panum's fusional area (7 marks):**

**Retinal disparity (2 marks):** Because the left and right eyes are separated by the **IPD (~6.3 cm)**, the 2D images projected onto the two retinas are slightly different. The horizontal pixel difference between corresponding points in the two images is the **disparity**. The brain measures this disparity in **V1** of the visual cortex.

**Stereopsis (2 marks):** The brain fuses the two disparate images into a single percept with vivid 3D depth. Disparities are classified as:
- **Zero disparity** → object lies on the **horopter** (the plane of fixation).
- **Crossed disparity** → object in **front of** the horopter (nearer than fixation).
- **Uncrossed disparity** → object **behind** the horopter.

**Panum's fusional area (2 marks):** The narrow region of 3D space around the horopter within which the brain can successfully fuse disparate images into a single 3D percept. Outside this region, **diplopia (double vision)** occurs. The width of Panum's area is approximately **±10 arc-min** of disparity near the fovea and **±30 arc-min** in the periphery.

**Implementation in HMDs (1 mark):** A modern HMD contains two micro-displays (one per eye). The rendering engine:
1. Computes two viewpoints offset by the user's IPD.
2. Projects the 3D scene onto each viewport with a small horizontal parallax.
3. Presents the left-eye image to the left display and the right-eye image to the right display.
4. The user's brain fuses them, producing stereopsis.

**Diagrams:** Refer to SECTION 4.4 for the Panum's area concept.

**(b) Motion parallax and 6-DoF tracking (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** Motion parallax is the depth cue generated by **observer translation**. As the viewer moves sideways, near objects sweep across the retina faster than far objects, creating a velocity gradient that the brain interprets as depth.

**Mathematical derivation (3 marks)** — Follow the steps in SECTION 3.2 exactly. Final result:

$$
v_{\text{retinal}} \approx \dfrac{V_{\text{head}}}{Z} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad Z \approx \dfrac{V_{\text{head}}}{v_{\text{retinal}}}
$$

**Example:** If the head translates at $V_{\text{head}} = 0.5$ m/s and a point produces a retinal velocity of 0.1 rad/s, the point's depth is:

$$
Z = \dfrac{0.5}{0.1} = 5\ \text{m}
$$

**6-DoF VR tracking (2 marks):** A 6-DoF VR system tracks **three rotational axes (roll, pitch, yaw)** and **three translational axes (X, Y, Z)** of the user's head in real-time. This requires:
- **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** for rotational tracking.
- **External cameras (e.g., SteamVR Lighthouses) or inside-out cameras** for positional tracking.

Without 6-DoF tracking, the virtual world does not shift correctly with head movement, and the user experiences a frozen, flat 3D world — the **simulation sickness** that plagued early 3D cinema and 3-DoF mobile VR (e.g., Google Cardboard). Modern systems like Meta Quest 3, Apple Vision Pro, and HTC Vive use 6-DoF tracking precisely to deliver convincing motion parallax. **(7 marks total)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Depth Perception Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the units** in the disparity formula — examiners deduct **1 full mark** if radians/arc-minutes are not stated.
> 2. **Confusing crossed vs. uncrossed disparity** — always state the sign convention explicitly: positive = crossed (nearer), negative = uncrossed (farther).
> 3. **Skipping the small-angle approximation justification** — in derivations, write "since IPD $\ll Z$, $\arctan(\theta) \approx \theta$" to earn the approximation mark.
> 4. **Ignoring IPD personalization** — a sophisticated answer mentions that modern HMDs allow IPD adjustment (hardware slider or software IPD) to match the user's anatomy.
> 5. **Not distinguishing accommodation from convergence** — students routinely write one for the other; the **lens curvature** vs. **eye rotation** distinction is mandatory.
> 6. **Omitting units of diopters (D)** when discussing accommodation — 1 D = 1 m⁻¹.
> 7. **Not drawing diagrams** — KTU examiners explicitly reward diagram marks; even a rough sketch of the two-eye geometry earns at least 1–2 marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist for Perception of Depth:**

- **Definition:** Depth perception is the visual ability to judge 3D spatial relationships from 2D retinal images and oculomotor signals.
- **Three big families:** Oculomotor (accommodation, convergence), Binocular (disparity, stereopsis), Pictorial (occlusion, perspective, texture, aerial, shadows, etc.).
- **Fourth family — motion:** Motion parallax.
- **IPD ≈ 6.3 cm** is the foundation of all binocular geometry.
- **Panum's fusional area ≈ ±10 arc-min** defines the limits of comfortable stereopsis.
- **Horopter** = the plane of zero disparity (where the eyes are currently fixating).
- **Crossed disparity** = nearer than horopter; **uncrossed disparity** = farther.
- **Vergence–accommodation conflict** is the single biggest VR comfort problem.
- **Oculomotor cues degrade beyond 6 m**; pictorial cues dominate beyond 10 m.
- **Motion parallax** is the body's biological LIDAR: $v_{\text{retinal}} = V_{\text{head}} / Z$.
- **6-DoF head tracking** is what distinguishes modern immersive VR from 3-DoF (mobile) and 3D cinema.
- **Diopter** = 1 / distance (in meters); VR conflicts are typically 0.5–2.0 D.
- **Critical flicker fusion ~ 90 Hz foveal** → VR refresh rates should exceed 90 Hz to avoid flicker detection.
- **Binocular disparity formula** $\delta = \text{IPD}(Z_f - Z_x)/(Z_x Z_f)$ is a **must-memorize** equation.
- **Strongest pictorial cue:** Occlusion (works at all distances, always wins over size cues).
- **Stereopsis** arises from disparity processing in V1/V2 of the visual cortex.
- **Convergence** is the inward rotation of both eyes; **divergence** is the opposite, used for very distant targets.

> **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Visual Perception: Perception of Depth**
> *Prepared for KTU 2024 Scheme — PBCNT504 Virtual Reality*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perception of Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT504 - VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Perception of Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Perception of Motion** in the context of Virtual Reality (VR) refers to the multi-sensory cognitive and physiological process by which the human nervous system detects, interprets, and responds to changes in the spatial position of objects (or the observer) over time. It is a unified construct combining **visual perception**, **vestibular sensation**, and **proprioceptive feedback** to construct an internal model of self-motion and object-motion within a virtual environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The perception of motion is fundamental to VR design because the brain's motion-cuing system must be coherently stimulated to produce the illusion of presence. A mismatch between visual and vestibular cues leads to **cybersickness** (simulator sickness).

### 1.2 Key Components of Motion Perception

| Sensory Channel | Function in Motion Perception |
|---|---|
| **Visual System** | Detects optical flow, parallax, and retinal image changes |
| **Vestibular System** (inner ear) | Detects linear (otolith organs) and angular (semicircular canals) accelerations |
| **Proprioceptive System** | Provides information about limb position and muscle tension |
| **Somatosensory System** | Detects pressure, vibration, and tactile cues |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine sitting in a stationary train at a platform when a neighboring train begins to move. For a brief moment, you feel **as if your own train is moving in the opposite direction**. This phenomenon, called **vection**, is the brain's best guess based on visual motion cues overriding the vestibular system's report of "no movement." VR systems deliberately exploit this same neural pathway to convince users they are flying, walking, or turning inside a synthetic world.

### 1.4 Formal Classification of Motion (KTU Terminology)

1. **Real Motion** — Actual physical displacement verified by the vestibular system and proprioceptors.
2. **Apparent Motion** — Illusory perception of motion created by sequential static stimuli (e.g., frames of a film).
3. **Induced Motion** — Perception of motion in a stationary object caused by the apparent motion of its surrounding frame.
4. **Implied Motion** — Static images (e.g., a running figure in a photograph) suggesting motion through pictorial cues.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Motion Perception Pipeline

The human brain processes motion through a hierarchical neural pipeline:

- **Stage 1 — Retinal Encoding:** Photoreceptors capture luminance changes. Motion-sensitive ganglion cells (parasol/M-cells) respond selectively to temporal contrast.
- **Stage 2 — V1 (Primary Visual Cortex):** Direction-selective simple and complex cells (Hubel & Wiesel) respond to oriented edges moving in preferred directions.
- **Stage 3 — MT/V5 (Middle Temporal Area):** Specialized for processing **optic flow**, velocity, and direction of moving patterns. Lesions here cause **akinetopsia** (inability to perceive motion).
- **Stage 4 — MST (Medial Superior Temporal Area):** Integrates visual motion with vestibular and proprioceptive signals to estimate self-motion.
- **Stage 5 — Parietal & Vestibular Cortex:** Multisensory integration, heading estimation, and postural response.

### 2.2 Visual Cues for Motion Perception

| Cue | Description | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Optical Flow** | Pattern of visual motion across the retina during self-movement | Used in flight simulators and driving VR for speed perception |
| **Motion Parallax** | Near objects move faster on the retina than distant ones | Depth cue; basis for stereo rendering in HMDs |
| **Vection** | Illusory self-motion induced by a moving visual field | Must be triggered carefully to avoid cybersickness |
| **Looming** | Expanding retinal image of an approaching object | Triggers collision-avoidance reflexes |
| **Phi Phenomenon** | Apparent motion from flashing adjacent lights sequentially | Used in LED display refresh and frame rate design |
| **Beta Movement** | Apparent smooth motion from discrete static images | Foundation of cinema and HMD frame sequencing |

### 2.3 The Vestibular System — The "Other Half" of VR Motion

The vestibular apparatus in the inner ear contains two principal subsystems:

- **Otolith Organs (Utricle & Saccule):** Detect **linear acceleration** and head tilt relative to gravity. They sense the gravito-inertial force (GIF), defined as:

$$ \vec{a}_{GIF} = \vec{g} - \vec{a}_{linear} $$

where $\vec{g}$ is gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**) and $\vec{a}_{linear}$ is the linear acceleration of the head.

- **Semicircular Canals (SCCs):** Three roughly orthogonal fluid-filled loops detect **angular acceleration**. Each canal's response is approximated by a **second-order torsion-pendulum model**:

$$ \ddot{\theta}_{cupula}(t) + \frac{b}{I}\dot{\theta}_{cupula}(t) + \frac{k}{I}\theta_{cupula}(t) = \alpha_{head}(t) $$

where $\theta_{cupula}$ is cupula deflection, $b$ is damping coefficient, $k$ is elastic stiffness, $I$ is moment of inertia, and $\alpha_{head}$ is head angular acceleration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The SCCs integrate angular acceleration to estimate angular velocity. They have a **time constant of ~5–7 seconds** — meaning if rotation continues uniformly, the endolymph catches up to the canal and the brain wrongly concludes rotation has stopped. This is critical in VR motion simulators.

### 2.4 Vection — The Cornerstone of VR Self-Motion Illusion

**Vection** is the illusion of self-motion induced by moving visual scenes. It has three measurable phases:

1. **Latency (200–800 ms):** Time before the illusion begins.
2. **Steady-State:** Sustained illusion magnitude.
3. **Aftereffect / Washout (1–5 s):** Residual illusion after visual stimulus stops.

A simplified model for vection onset latency $L$ as a function of visual field coverage $C$ (fraction of FOV):

$$ L(C) = L_{min} + (L_{max} - L_{min}) \cdot e^{-\lambda C} $$

where $L_{min}$ is the asymptotic minimum latency (~0.2 s), $L_{max}$ is the latency at zero coverage (~1.0 s), and $\lambda$ is an empirical rate constant (~2.0).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit | Equation / Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| $v$ | Object velocity (retinal) | deg/s | $v = \Delta \theta / \Delta t$ |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity of head | rad/s | From SCC integration |
| $\vec{a}_{GIF}$ | Gravito-inertial force | m/s² | $\vec{a}_{GIF} = \vec{g} - \vec{a}_{linear}$ |
| $\tau$ | SCC time constant | s | ~5–7 s |
| $L$ | Vection latency | s | $L = L_{min} + (L_{max}-L_{min})e^{-\lambda C}$ |
| $T$ | Time-to-contact | s | $T = \tau_{d} / (d\tau_{d}/dt)$ |
| $f_{c}$ | Critical flicker frequency | Hz | $f_c \approx 60$ Hz for HMD |
| $D$ | Display refresh rate | Hz | $D \geq 90$ Hz (modern VR standard) |
| $FOV$ | Field of view | deg | $F = 2 \arctan(d / 2f)$ |
| $IPD$ | Inter-pupillary distance | mm | Adult mean: **63.5 mm** |

### 2.6 Motion Sickness in VR — Sensory Conflict Theory

According to **Reason & Brand's Sensory Conflict Theory**, motion sickness arises when there is a mismatch between:

- **Expected sensory signals** (from the brain's internal model), and
- **Actual sensory signals** (from vision, vestibular, and proprioception).

$$ \text{Cybersickness Index} \propto \sum_{i} w_i \cdot \vert S^{expected}_i - S^{actual}_i \vert $$

where $w_i$ is the weighting for each sensory channel $i$, and $S_i$ is the signal magnitude.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World VR Engineering Utility:** This conflict model drives display design (high refresh rates ≥ 90 Hz), low-latency tracking (< 20 ms motion-to-photon), and motion-cueing algorithms in flight simulators. Companies like Oculus, Sony (PlayStation VR), and HTC have engineered their systems specifically to minimize this mismatch.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Semicircular Canal Transfer Function

The semicircular canal is modeled as a damped torsion pendulum. We derive its transfer function step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Newton's Second Law for Endolymph Rotation:**

The endolymph (inner ear fluid) experiences a moment opposite to the head's angular acceleration. Applying Newton's law for rotational dynamics:

$$ I \cdot \ddot{\theta}_{cupula} = -b \cdot \dot{\theta}_{cupula} - k \cdot \theta_{cupula} + I \cdot \alpha_{head} $$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to Standard Form:**

Move all left-hand terms to the left side:

$$ I \cdot \ddot{\theta}_{cupula} + b \cdot \dot{\theta}_{cupula} + k \cdot \theta_{cupula} = I \cdot \alpha_{head} $$

**Step 3 — Divide by $I$ to Normalize:**

$$ \ddot{\theta}_{cupula} + \frac{b}{I}\dot{\theta}_{cupula} + \frac{k}{I}\theta_{cupula} = \alpha_{head} $$

**Step 4 — Define System Parameters:**

Let $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/I}$ (natural frequency in rad/s) and $\zeta = b/(2\sqrt{kI})$ (damping ratio). The transfer function $H(s)$ in the Laplace domain becomes:

$$ H(s) = \frac{\Theta_{cupula}(s)}{\Alpha_{head}(s)} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2} $$

**Step 5 — Take the Laplace Transform of the Governing Equation:**

Starting from:

$$ I\ddot{\theta} + b\dot{\theta} + k\theta = I\alpha $$

Apply $\mathcal{L}$ with zero initial conditions:

$$ (Is^2 + Bs + K)\Theta(s) = I \cdot A(s) $$

Solving for $\Theta(s)/A(s)$:

$$ H(s) = \frac{I}{Is^2 + Bs + K} = \frac{1}{s^2 + (B/I)s + (K/I)} $$

**Step 6 — Steady-State Behaviour (DC gain):**

At $s = 0$, $H(0) = I/K$. This is the **cupula displacement per unit angular acceleration** at very low frequencies. For a long-duration constant rotation ($\alpha = 0$), $\theta_{cupula} \to 0$, confirming the **adaptation** behavior of SCCs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This transfer function explains why the human vestibular system acts as a **high-pass filter** for rotation — it is sensitive to changes in angular velocity (acceleration) but cannot sustain the perception of constant rotation for more than ~20–30 seconds. This is why sustained-rotation VR games cause vection to fade while still maintaining visual stimulation, leading to severe sensory conflict and cybersickness.

### 3.2 Derivation: Vection Threshold Based on Optical Flow

The threshold angular velocity $\omega_{min}$ for vection induction as a function of stimulus dot density $\rho$ is given by an empirical relation:

$$ \omega_{min}(\rho) = \omega_0 \left( 1 + \frac{\rho_{ref}}{\rho} \right) $$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

Given $\omega_0 = 1.5$ deg/s (baseline threshold) and $\rho_{ref} = 20$ dots/deg² (reference density), the threshold for $\rho = 40$ dots/deg² is:

$$ \omega_{min}(40) = 1.5 \left( 1 + \frac{20}{40} \right) = 1.5 \cdot 1.5 = 2.25 \text{ deg/s} $$

The vection is induced when the stimulus angular velocity $\omega \geq \omega_{min}(\rho)$. Lower density patterns require stronger motion to induce vection.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Motion Perception Cues and VR Implementations

| VR Application | Dominant Motion Cue | Required Hardware | Typical Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flight simulator | Optical flow + vestibular | Motion platform, HMD | $\omega_{min} \approx 1.5$ deg/s |
| Driving simulator | Looming + parallax | Steering wheel HMD | $T_{min} \approx 0.5$ s |
| Architectural walkthrough | Motion parallax | Stereo HMD, 6-DoF tracking | Parallax error < 1 arcmin |
| Roller-coaster VR | Vection + Inertial cues | Motion chair, HMD | Vection onset < 0.8 s |
| Surgical simulation | Haptic feedback + looming | Haptic gloves, HMD | Force feedback < 5 ms latency |

### 3.4 Derivation: Time-to-Contact (TTC) from Optical Expansion

The $\tau$ (tau) margin for an approaching object is:

$$ \tau(t) = \frac{r(t)}{\dot{r}(t)} $$

where $r(t)$ is the apparent radius of the object on the retina. For a sphere approaching at constant velocity $v$:

$$ r(t) = \frac{R \cdot f}{d_0 - vt} $$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$ \dot{r}(t) = \frac{R \cdot f \cdot v}{(d_0 - vt)^2} = \frac{v \cdot r(t)}{d_0 - vt} $$

Substituting into the $\tau$ equation:

$$ \tau = \frac{r}{\dot{r}} = \frac{(d_0 - vt)}{v} $$

This is the **time-to-contact**. When $\tau$ drops below ~0.7 s, the observer reflexively initiates an avoidance response — a fact exploited in VR collision-warning systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Motion Perception Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Visual Input - Retina] --> B[V1 - Direction Selective Cells]
    C[Vestibular Input - SCC and Otolith] --> D[MST - Multisensory Integration]
    E[Proprioceptive Input - Muscle Spindles] --> D
    B --> D
    D --> F[Parietal Cortex - Heading Estimation]
    D --> G[Cerebellum - Postural Control]
    F --> H[Internal Model of Self-Motion]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Output - Perception of Motion and Action]
    J[Conflict Detector] --> K[Cybersickness Response]
    H --> J
    A --> J
    C --> J
```

### 4.2 Vection Induction and Washout Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stimulus Onset] --> V1[Latency Phase 200 to 800 ms]
    V1 --> V2[Steady State Vection]
    V2 --> S2[Stimulus Offset]
    S2 --> V3[Aftereffect 1 to 5 s]
    V3 --> R1[Return to Stationary Perception]
    V2 --> CS[Trigger Cybersickness if Mismatch with Vestibular]
    CS --> SX[Sensory Conflict Output]
```

### 4.3 Sensory Conflict Architecture for VR Cybersickness

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Expected
        E1[Internal Model Predictions]
    end
    subgraph Actual
        A1[Visual Motion Signal]
        A2[Vestibular Signal]
        A3[Proprioceptive Signal]
    end
    E1 --> CMP{Comparator}
    A1 --> CMP
    A2 --> CMP
    A3 --> CMP
    CMP -->|Mismatch detected| OUT1[Cybersickness Triggered]
    CMP -->|Match| OUT2[Stable VR Experience]
    OUT1 --> SX[Nausea, Disorientation, Oculomotor Symptoms]
```

### 4.4 Vestibular Visual Integration Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Vestibular
        VS1[Semicircular Canals] --> VSP[Angular Velocity Estimate]
        VS2[Otolith Organs] --> LVP[Linear Acceleration Estimate]
    end
    subgraph Visual
        VL1[Optic Flow Processor] --> VOP[Heading and Speed Estimate]
        VL2[Motion Parallax] --> VOP
    end
    VSP --> FUS[Fusion Filter - Kalman]
    LVP --> FUS
    VOP --> FUS
    FUS --> OUT[Unified Self-Motion Estimate for VR]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The Fusion Filter block in the last diagram represents a **Kalman filter** implementation commonly used in inertial-odometric sensor fusion for VR head tracking. This is the same architecture used in modern INS/GPS systems and VR/AR inside-out tracking (e.g., Meta Quest 3, Apple Vision Pro).

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define vection and explain its significance in VR system design.**

**Model Answer:**
Vection is the illusion of self-motion induced by a moving visual field when the observer is physically stationary. It consists of three phases: latency (200–800 ms), steady-state, and aftereffect. In VR design, vection is the primary mechanism by which self-motion is conveyed in head-mounted displays. Designers must balance vection induction (for immersion) with vestibular mismatch (to avoid cybersickness). High field-of-view coverage, smooth motion gradients, and high refresh rates (> 90 Hz) are used to control vection onset and magnitude.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] What is the Sensory Conflict Theory of motion sickness?**

**Model Answer:**
The Sensory Conflict Theory (Reason & Brand, 1975) states that motion sickness arises when there is a mismatch between the **expected** sensory signals (as predicted by the brain's internal model) and the **actual** sensory signals from vision, vestibular organs, and proprioceptors. In VR, when the user sees motion on the HMD but the vestibular system reports no corresponding acceleration, the brain interprets this conflict as a sign of "neurotoxins" (an evolutionary hangover from poison-induced dizziness) and triggers nausea, sweating, and disorientation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the anatomy and function of the human vestibular system in the perception of motion. Discuss how each subsystem contributes to motion detection in a VR context. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the transfer function of the semicircular canal model and explain its high-pass filtering behavior with relevance to VR motion simulators. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The human vestibular system is located in the inner ear and consists of:
- **Three Semicircular Canals (SCCs):** Oriented roughly orthogonal to each other (anterior, posterior, horizontal). They detect **angular acceleration** in three planes. The endolymph inside lags behind head rotation, deflecting the cupula and stimulating hair cells.
- **Otolith Organs (Utricle and Saccule):** Detect **linear acceleration** and head tilt relative to gravity. The utricle responds to horizontal motion; the saccule to vertical. They contain calcium carbonate crystals (otoconia) on a gelatinous membrane whose inertia deflects hair cells during acceleration.

**VR Relevance:** In VR motion simulators, the platform's actuators must stimulate both systems to match visual motion. Optical flow in the HMD indicates rotation/translation; the motion platform must reproduce the corresponding inertial cues within ~5 ms to avoid conflict.

**(b)** See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation. The transfer function is:

$$ H(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2} $$

At high frequencies (rapid head turns), $|H(j\omega)| \to 1/(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)$ — the system responds strongly to changes. At DC (constant rotation), $H(0) = 1/\omega_n^2$ but with integration effects the cupula returns to rest, making the SCC a high-pass filter with cutoff frequency $\omega_c = \zeta\omega_n$. For VR, this means a stationary user in a sustained-rotation visual scene experiences decaying vestibular response, leading to **vection-augmented cybersickness**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the concept of optical flow and its role in self-motion perception. Discuss how optical flow is used in VR applications such as flight simulators. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A VR driving simulator displays a forward-moving scene with approaching vehicles. Derive the time-to-contact ($\tau$) for a vehicle approaching at 30 m/s, initially 90 m away. At what remaining distance will the driver reflexively initiate braking? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Optical flow is the pattern of apparent motion of objects, surfaces, and edges in a visual scene caused by the relative motion between the observer and the scene. It was first mathematically formalized by **J.J. Gibson (1950)**. The flow field contains:
- **Focus of Expansion (FOE):** The point from which all optical flow vectors appear to diverge during forward translation — it indicates the heading direction.
- **Radial Flow:** Outward from the FOE, with magnitude proportional to $1/Z$ (inverse depth).
- **Lamellar Flow:** Slow background flow for distant textures.

In **flight simulators**, optical flow provides critical cues for:
- Speed estimation (rate of flow across the visual field)
- Altitude perception (density of texture below)
- Collision avoidance (looming detection)

The simulator's visual database must update at ≥ 60 Hz to maintain smooth flow perception.

**(b)** **Step 1:** Using the derived equation from Section 3.4:

$$ \tau = \frac{d_0 - vt}{v} $$

**Step 2:** Initial conditions: $v = 30$ m/s, $d_0 = 90$ m.

**Step 3:** Time-to-contact at the start:

$$ \tau(0) = \frac{90 - 30 \cdot 0}{30} = \frac{90}{30} = 3.0 \text{ s} $$

**Step 4:** The reflexive braking response is triggered at $\tau_{brake} \approx 0.7$ s (Lee, 1976).

**Step 5:** Find distance at $\tau = 0.7$ s:

$$ d_{brake} = v \cdot \tau_{brake} = 30 \times 0.7 = 21 \text{ m} $$

**Step 6:** Verify by substituting back:

$$ 0.7 = \frac{d_0 - 30 \cdot t_{brake}}{30} \implies t_{brake} = 2.3 \text{ s} $$

Remaining distance: $d_{brake} = 90 - 30(2.3) = 90 - 69 = 21$ m. ✓

**Answer:** The driver will reflexively initiate braking when the vehicle is **21 m** away.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget to mention that the SCCs act as **high-pass filters**, not all-pass integrators. This costs 2–3 marks in derivations.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **vection** (illusory self-motion) with **optical flow** (perceived object motion). They are complementary, not identical.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Failing to cite the **Sensory Conflict Theory** when explaining cybersickness — this is a high-value key term in KTU marking schemes.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not specifying units in time-to-contact and velocity derivations. Always include **m/s**, **seconds**, and **meters**.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Drawing the vestibular system with only the SCCs. You must include **otolith organs** for full marks on anatomy questions.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Perception of motion** is a multi-sensory integration of visual, vestibular, and proprioceptive inputs.
- [x] **Vection** is the illusion of self-motion from visual stimuli alone — has three phases: latency, steady-state, and aftereffect.
- [x] The **vestibular system** comprises **SCCs** (angular acceleration) and **otolith organs** (linear acceleration and gravity).
- [x] SCC transfer function: $H(s) = 1 / (s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2)$ — a second-order high-pass system.
- [x] **Optical flow** (Gibson) provides heading, speed, and depth cues via the Focus of Expansion.
- [x] **Sensory Conflict Theory** (Reason & Brand) explains cybersickness as a mismatch between expected and actual sensory signals.
- [x] **Time-to-Contact ($\tau$)** = $r(t)/\dot{r}(t)$ — drives reflexive avoidance at $\tau \approx 0.7$ s.
- [x] Modern VR HMDs require refresh rate **≥ 90 Hz** and motion-to-photon latency **< 20 ms** to minimize sensory conflict.
- [x] **MT/V5 cortex** is the primary motion-processing area; lesions cause **akinetopsia**.
- [x] **Motion parallax** is a powerful depth cue exploited in stereo VR rendering pipelines.
- [x] **Looming** (expanding retinal image) is the strongest natural collision-warning cue.
- [x] The SCC time constant of **5–7 s** explains why prolonged constant rotation produces vestibular adaptation.
- [x] **Kalman filter fusion** is the standard engineering approach to combine visual and inertial motion estimates in modern VR/AR systems.
- [x] Always cite **Gibson (1950)** for optical flow and **Reason & Brand (1975)** for sensory conflict theory in exam answers.
- [x] Diagrams should clearly distinguish SCCs from otolith organs, and vection latency from vection magnitude on the time axis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perception of Color
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT504 - VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Perception of Color

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCNT504 – Virtual Reality), **Color Perception** is defined as the neuro-physiological and cognitive process by which the human visual system (HVS) detects electromagnetic radiation in the visible spectrum (approximately **380 nm to 750 nm**) and interprets it as distinct hue, saturation, and brightness attributes. It is a **psychophysical** phenomenon — meaning it is the joint product of a physical stimulus (light wavelength and intensity) and a perceptual interpretation carried out by the retina, the lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN), and the primary visual cortex (V1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Color perception is *not* a property of the physical world but a constructed sensation of the brain triggered by differential stimulation of three classes of cone photoreceptors (L, M, S) in the retina, modulated by neural opponency and contextual influences.

### 1.2 Visible Spectrum and Wavelength Ranges

| **Spectral Region** | **Wavelength Range** | **Approximate Perceived Color** |
|---------------------|----------------------|----------------------------------|
| Violet              | 380 – 450 nm         | Deep violet to blue              |
| Blue                | 450 – 495 nm         | Blue                             |
| Green               | 495 – 570 nm         | Green                            |
| Yellow              | 570 – 590 nm         | Yellow                           |
| Orange              | 590 – 620 nm         | Orange                           |
| Red                 | 620 – 750 nm         | Red                              |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Visible Spectrum** lies between ultraviolet (< 380 nm) and infrared (> 750 nm). The retina cannot respond to photons outside this band, although some near-IR VR eye-tracking systems exploit this blindness for unobtrusive illumination.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Three-Ingredient Recipe"**
> Imagine your retina is a master chef with **only three spice jars** labeled **Red**, **Green**, and **Blue**. The chef cannot see the actual ingredient (the wavelength); she can only taste three different chemical responses. Every color you see — including white, magenta, and chartreuse — is just a different *recipe* of these three spices. An object appears **yellow** to you not because yellow "paints" your eye, but because the chef mixed approximately **75% Red-spirit and 25% Blue-spirit**, producing the perceptual experience of yellow. This is precisely the **Trichromatic Theory** of color vision.

## 1.4 Why Color Perception Matters in Virtual Reality

In VR, color is the **single most information-dense visual channel**. Head-Mounted Displays (HMDs) such as Meta Quest, HTC Vive, and Apple Vision Pro rely on micro-OLED or LCD panels that emit **subpixels** (R, G, B) at varying intensities. If the rendering pipeline does not respect the HVS's color processing, the user experiences:

- **Color fatigue** (especially with over-saturated primaries),
- **Binocular color rivalry** (mismatched colors between left/right eyes),
- **Vergence–accommodation conflict** amplified by chromatic aberration,
- **Reduced immersion** and **simulator sickness**.

Therefore, a VR engineer must model color perception accurately, which is the focus of this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tripartite Architecture of Color Vision

The modern understanding of color perception rests on three integrated theories, each operating at a different level of the visual hierarchy:

### A. Trichromatic Theory (Young–Helmholtz, 1802 / 1852)
1. The retina contains **three types of cone photoreceptors** sensitive to **Long (L, ~564 nm)**, **Medium (M, ~534 nm)**, and **Short (S, ~420 nm)** wavelengths.
2. Every perceived color is a **linear combination** of the responses of these three cone types.
3. **Why it works:** Because cone spectral sensitivities overlap heavily, the brain can disambiguate wavelengths from the *ratio* of the three signals.

### B. Opponent-Process Theory (Hering, 1878)
1. Visual information is transmitted from cones to the **retinal ganglion cells** and the **LGN** via **opponent channels**.
2. The two primary opponent axes are:
   - **Red–Green (R–G)** channel
   - **Blue–Yellow (B–Y)** channel
3. A third **achromatic (Black–White)** channel carries luminance information.
4. **Why it works:** This explains why we **never perceive reddish-green or bluish-yellow** — the neural wiring is subtraction-based, not addition-based.

### C. Stage Theory (Hurvich & Jameson, 1957)
1. Combines (A) and (B) as a **two-stage model**: trichromacy at the photoreceptor level, opponency at the ganglion/LGN level.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** Both theories are correct — they describe **different stages** of the same visual pipeline. A 14-mark KTU question will often ask you to "reconcile" these theories. Your answer must explicitly mention the *stage theory* integration.

## 2.2 The VR Color Pipeline (Block-Level View)

1. **Scene Rendering**: 3D engine computes linear RGB radiance $L_{\text{linear}}$ for each pixel.
2. **Tone Mapping**: Compresses High Dynamic Range (HDR) to Low Dynamic Range (LDR) using a tone-mapping operator (Reinhard, ACES, etc.).
3. **Gamma Correction**: Applies the sRGB transfer function to convert linear-light to display-referred non-linear values.
4. **Display Emission**: Each subpixel of the HMD panel emits light at a luminance proportional to the encoded value.
5. **Retinal Capture**: The HVS integrates over time and space, producing perceived color.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam favorites. Memorize the forms, the constants, and the conditions of validity.**

### Table 2.1: Core Color Perception Formulas

| **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Variables / Units** | **Notes** |
|------------------|------------------------------|------------------------|-----------|
| Wave Speed | $c = \lambda \cdot f$ | $c$ = speed of light (≈ $3 \times 10^8$ m/s), $\lambda$ = wavelength (m), $f$ = frequency (Hz) | Foundation of colorimetry. |
| Photon Energy | $E = h \cdot f = \dfrac{h \cdot c}{\lambda}$ | $h$ = Planck's constant (≈ $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s) | Photons of 700 nm red carry ~1.77 eV; 400 nm violet carry ~3.10 eV. |
| sRGB Gamma Encoding | $V_{\text{out}} = \begin{cases} 12.92 \cdot V_{\text{linear}}, & V_{\text{linear}} \le 0.0031308 \\ 1.055 \cdot V_{\text{linear}}^{1/2.4} - 0.055, & V_{\text{linear}} > 0.0031308 \end{cases}$ | $V$ is normalized [0,1] | The non-linear encoding HMDs use. |
| Gamma Decoding (Display) | $V_{\text{linear}} = \left(\dfrac{V_{\text{out}} + 0.055}{1.055}\right)^{2.4}$ | $V$ is normalized [0,1] | Inverse of sRGB encoding. |
| CIE RGB → XYZ (sRGB) | $\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{M}_{\text{RGB→XYZ}} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} R_{\text{linear}} \\ G_{\text{linear}} \\ B_{\text{linear}} \end{bmatrix}$ | Matrix $\mathbf{M}_{\text{RGB→XYZ}}$ is the ITU-R BT.709 / sRGB matrix | The 3×3 matrix is mandatory to memorize. |
| Luminance (Rec.709) | $Y = 0.2126 R + 0.7152 G + 0.0722 B$ | $R, G, B$ are linear | Weighted by HVS photopic sensitivity. |
| CIELAB L\* | $L^* = 116 \cdot f\!\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - 16$ | $Y_n$ = reference white | Perceptually uniform lightness. |
| CIELAB a\*, b\* | $a^* = 500 \left[ f\!\left(\dfrac{X}{X_n}\right) - f\!\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_n}\right) \right]$ ; $b^* = 200 \left[ f\!\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - f\!\left(\dfrac{Z}{Z_n}\right) \right]$ | $f(t) = t^{1/3}$ for $t > \delta$ | $a^*$ red-green axis, $b^*$ blue-yellow axis. |
| CIE76 Color Difference | $\Delta E^*_{ab} = \sqrt{(L^*_2-L^*_1)^2 + (a^*_2-a^*_1)^2 + (b^*_2-b^*_1)^2}$ | Dimensionless | $\Delta E < 1$ is "just noticeable" to most observers. |
| Stevens' Power Law (Brightness) | $\psi = k \cdot (L - L_0)^n$ | $\psi$ = perceived magnitude, $L$ = luminance, $n \approx 0.33$ for brightness | The brain compresses physical intensity. |
| Weber's Law | $\dfrac{\Delta I}{I} = k$ | $\Delta I$ = just-noticeable difference (JND) | Constant fractional sensitivity. |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Pipe Symbol Warning:** In the table above, absolute values are written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ (rendered via LaTeX). Do **not** use the bare pipe character $\vert$ in markdown table cells, as it breaks the table.

## 2.4 Photoreceptor Spectral Sensitivity

The three cone types peak at the following wavelengths (CIE 2006 physiologically-relevant LMS fundamentals):

- **L-cones (Long):** peak at $\lambda_{\max} \approx 564$ nm
- **M-cones (Medium):** peak at $\lambda_{\max} \approx 534$ nm
- **S-cones (Short):** peak at $\lambda_{\max} \approx 420$ nm

> The S-cone population is only ~2% of all cones, which is why the human eye is **comparatively insensitive to blue** — a critical concern for VR display design where blue subpixels often dominate power consumption.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: sRGB Gamma Encoding Curve

The sRGB standard (IEC 61966-2-1) defines a piecewise function that maps linear scene-referred values to non-linear display-referred values. We derive each piece to understand *why* it exists.

### Step 1 — Recognize the Goal
Linear-light values are not how displays emit light. CRTs had a non-linear voltage-to-luminance relationship approximated by $L = V^{\gamma}$ with $\gamma \approx 2.2$. Modern displays preserve this non-linearity for **backward compatibility** and because the human eye is more sensitive to changes in dark regions than bright ones (Weber's law). The sRGB curve is the *standardized* version of this non-linearity.

### Step 2 — Linear Segment (Near Black)
For very small values, the curve must be **continuous** and have a **continuous derivative** at the join point $V_0 = 0.0031308$. A pure power function $V^{1/2.4}$ has infinite slope at zero, which is physically impossible for a display. Therefore a **linear segment** is used:

$$V_{\text{out}} = a \cdot V_{\text{linear}}$$

where $a$ is chosen so that the slope at the join matches the power-law slope.

### Step 3 — Slope Matching at the Join Point
The power law $V^{1/2.4}$ has derivative $\frac{1}{2.4} V^{(1/2.4) - 1}$. At $V = 0.0031308$:

$$\frac{1}{2.4} \cdot (0.0031308)^{(1/2.4) - 1} = 12.92$$

Therefore, $a = 12.92$, giving the linear segment:

$$V_{\text{out}} = 12.92 \cdot V_{\text{linear}}, \quad V_{\text{linear}} \le 0.0031308$$

### Step 4 — Non-Linear Segment (Above Threshold)
For larger values, we use a power function with a linear offset and gain:

$$V_{\text{out}} = A \cdot V_{\text{linear}}^{1/2.4} - B$$

### Step 5 — Solve for A and B
We impose two conditions:
- **Condition 1 — Value at the join:** $A \cdot (0.0031308)^{1/2.4} - B = 12.92 \cdot 0.0031308 \approx 0.04045$
- **Condition 2 — Slope at the join:** $A \cdot \frac{1}{2.4} \cdot (0.0031308)^{(1/2.4)-1} = 12.92$

From Condition 2, since $\frac{1}{2.4} \cdot (0.0031308)^{-0.5833} = 12.92$, we get:

$$A = 12.92 \cdot 2.4 = 31.008 \quad \text{(unnormalized)}$$

Refining with continuity, the standard sRGB constants are:

$$A = 1.055, \quad B = 0.055$$

### Step 6 — Final Assembled Formula

$$V_{\text{out}} = \begin{cases} 12.92 \cdot V_{\text{linear}} & \text{if } V_{\text{linear}} \le 0.0031308 \\ 1.055 \cdot V_{\text{linear}}^{1/2.4} - 0.055 & \text{if } V_{\text{linear}} > 0.0031308 \end{cases}$$

This is the sRGB transfer function used in every modern VR HMD's display controller.

## 3.2 Derivation: CIE RGB → XYZ Transform (sRGB / Rec.709)

The CIE 1931 XYZ color space was defined such that the **Y** component represents **luminance** (perceived brightness), and the entire visible gamut lies in the positive octant (X, Y, Z ≥ 0). The transform from linear sRGB to CIE XYZ is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4124 & 0.3576 & 0.1805 \\ 0.2126 & 0.7152 & 0.0722 \\ 0.0193 & 0.1192 & 0.9505 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} R_{\text{linear}} \\ G_{\text{linear}} \\ B_{\text{linear}} \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 1 — Verify Y is the Luminance Row
The second row, $(0.2126, 0.7152, 0.0722)$, sums to $0.9999 \approx 1.0$. These coefficients are the **photopic luminosity function** weights (Rec.709) — i.e., the relative brightness contributions of the three primaries to a standard observer.

### Step 2 — Verify Inverse Transform
The inverse (XYZ → linear sRGB) is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} R_{\text{linear}} \\ G_{\text{linear}} \\ B_{\text{linear}} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.2406 & -1.5372 & -0.4986 \\ -0.9689 & 1.8758 & 0.0415 \\ 0.0557 & -0.2040 & 1.0570 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example
Given a linear sRGB triplet $(R, G, B) = (0.5, 0.3, 0.1)$ (a muted orange), compute XYZ:

$$\begin{aligned} X &= 0.4124(0.5) + 0.3576(0.3) + 0.1805(0.1) \\ &= 0.2062 + 0.1073 + 0.0181 \\ &= 0.3316 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} Y &= 0.2126(0.5) + 0.7152(0.3) + 0.0722(0.1) \\ &= 0.1063 + 0.2146 + 0.0072 \\ &= 0.3281 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} Z &= 0.0193(0.5) + 0.1192(0.3) + 0.9505(0.1) \\ &= 0.0097 + 0.0358 + 0.0951 \\ &= 0.1405 \end{aligned}$$

Final CIE XYZ triplet: $(0.3316, 0.3281, 0.1405)$.

## 3.3 Derivation: CIELAB L\*a\*b\* Conversion

CIELAB is a **perceptually uniform** color space, meaning that equal Euclidean distances in $(L^*, a^*, b^*)$ correspond to equal perceived color differences. The conversion uses the cube-root compression of the CIE XYZ ratios:

### Step 1 — Define the Cube-Root Function
For $t > \delta = \left(\frac{6}{29}\right)^3 = 0.008856$:

$$f(t) = t^{1/3}$$

For $t \le \delta$, a linear segment is used to maintain continuity of derivative:

$$f(t) = \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{29}{6}\right)^2 t + \frac{4}{29}$$

### Step 2 — Compute L\*
Using reference white $(X_n, Y_n, Z_n) = (0.9505, 1.0000, 1.0890)$ for D65 illuminant:

$$L^* = 116 \cdot f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - 16 = 116 \cdot f\!\left(\frac{0.3281}{1.0000}\right) - 16$$

Since $0.3281 > \delta$:

$$f(0.3281) = 0.3281^{1/3} \approx 0.6890$$

$$L^* = 116(0.6890) - 16 \approx 63.92$$

### Step 3 — Compute a\*
$$a^* = 500 \left[ f\!\left(\frac{X}{X_n}\right) - f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) \right] = 500 \left[ f(0.3489) - f(0.3281) \right]$$

$$f(0.3489) = 0.3489^{1/3} \approx 0.7034$$

$$a^* = 500 (0.7034 - 0.6890) = 500 (0.0144) = 7.20$$

### Step 4 — Compute b\*
$$b^* = 200 \left[ f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - f\!\left(\frac{Z}{Z_n}\right) \right] = 200 \left[ f(0.3281) - f(0.1290) \right]$$

$$f(0.1290) = 0.1290^{1/3} \approx 0.5051$$

$$b^* = 200 (0.6890 - 0.5051) = 200 (0.1839) = 36.78$$

### Step 5 — Final CIELAB Triplet
$(L^*, a^*, b^*) = (63.92, +7.20, +36.78)$ — confirming a **warm, orange-yellow** color in the perceptually-uniform space.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Phenomena of Color Perception

| **Phenomenon**            | **Mechanism**                                     | **Implication in VR Design**                          |
|---------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|
| **Simultaneous Contrast** | A neutral gray appears tinted by the surrounding color | UI panels must use consistent neutral grays           |
| **Bezold–Brücke Shift**   | Hue changes with luminance                       | Saturation must be re-tuned at different brightness levels |
| **Helmholtz–Kohlrausch**  | Highly saturated colors appear brighter          | Saturated reds may break tone-mapping assumptions     |
| **Color Constancy**       | Perceived color is stable under varying illumination | VR lighting must match real-world expectations        |
| **Chromatic Adaptation**  | Visual system adjusts to prevailing white point  | Cross-platform VR requires white-point calibration    |
| **Afterimage / Adaptation** | Prolonged viewing fatigues cones (especially S) | VR scenes should avoid pure-saturated blue overlays   |
| **Troxler Fading**        | Stabilized images fade                            | VR reticles need micro-movements to stay visible      |
| **Binocular Color Rivalry** | Left/right eyes receive different colors         | HMDs must be calibrated per-eye for chromatic balance  |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** A common 14-mark question asks, "Explain the **perceptual phenomena** that VR designers must account for." The table above is your ready-made answer framework.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Human Visual Color Processing Pipeline

The following block diagram traces a photon of light from the external world through every stage of color processing in the human visual system, ending at conscious perception. This is the canonical block architecture used in VR perception textbooks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Photon Source Object 380 to 750 nm] --> B0[Cornea and Lens Optical Focusing]
    B0 --> C0[Retinal Image Photoreceptor Stimulation]
    C0 --> D0{Photoreceptor Layer}
    D0 --> D1[L Cones Peak 564 nm Long]
    D0 --> D2[M Cones Peak 534 nm Medium]
    D0 --> D3[S Cones Peak 420 nm Short]
    D0 --> D4[Rods Scotopic Vision]
    D1 --> E0[Horizontal Cells Center Surround Antagonism]
    D2 --> E0
    D3 --> E0
    E0 --> F0[Bipolar Cells]
    F0 --> G0[Retinal Ganglion Cells]
    G0 --> H0{Parvocellular P Pathway}
    G0 --> I0{Koniocellular K Pathway}
    H0 --> J0[Opponent Encoding Red Green and Blue Yellow Axes]
    I0 --> J0
    J0 --> K0[Optic Nerve]
    K0 --> L0[Lateral Geniculate Nucleus LGN Thalamus]
    L0 --> M0[Optic Radiation]
    M0 --> N0[Primary Visual Cortex V1]
    N0 --> O0[Higher Visual Areas V2 V3 V4 IT Cortex]
    O0 --> P0[Conscious Color Perception Hue Saturation Brightness]
    P0 --> Q0[VR Display Feedback Loop]

    classDef sourceNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef retinaNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef brainNode fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef perceptionNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class A0,B0,C0 sourceNode
    class D0,D1,D2,D3,D4,E0,F0,G0 retinaNode
    class H0,I0,J0,K0,L0,M0,N0,O0 brainNode
    class P0,Q0 perceptionNode
```

## 4.2 VR Color Reproduction Pipeline

The following block diagram maps the journey of color information from the 3D scene description in a VR engine to the photopic response of the human eye. This pipeline is essential for understanding *where* color management operations must occur.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Scene Linear RGB Radiance HDR] --> S1[Tone Mapping Operator ACES or Reinhard]
    S1 --> S2[Exposure Correction]
    S2 --> S3[sRGB Gamma Encoding]
    S3 --> S4[Display Panel Subpixel R G B Emission]
    S4 --> S5[HMD Optics Distortion and Chromatic Aberration]
    S5 --> S6[Retinal Image Formation]
    S6 --> S7[Photoreceptor LMS Cone Excitation]
    S7 --> S8[Opponent Processing in LGN]
    S8 --> S9[Perceived Color in VR HMD]

    classDef sceneClass fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef processClass fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef displayClass fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef visionClass fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class S0,S1 sceneClass
    class S2,S3,S5 processClass
    class S4 displayClass
    class S6,S7,S8 visionClass
    class S9 visionClass
```

## 4.3 Color Space Hierarchy and Conversions

The following diagram illustrates the relationships between the major color spaces used in VR engineering, from physical wavelengths to perceptually-uniform representations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W0[Physical Wavelength Lambda 380 to 750 nm] --> W1[CIE 1931 RGB Color Matching Functions]
    W1 --> W2[CIE XYZ Tristimulus Values via 3x3 Matrix]
    W2 --> W3{CIE Lab Conversion Cube Root Compression}
    W2 --> W4{Linear sRGB Conversion 3x3 Matrix Inverse}
    W3 --> W5[Perceptually Uniform CIELAB Lstar astar bstar]
    W4 --> W6[Gamma Encoded sRGB 0 to 255 Digital Count]
    W6 --> W7[HMD Display LUT Output Luminance]
    W5 --> W8[Color Difference Metric Delta E]
    W7 --> W9[VR Renderer Final Framebuffer]
    W8 --> W9
    W9 --> W10[User Perceived Color in HMD]

    classDef physicalClass fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef cieClass fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef perceptClass fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef outputClass fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class W0,W1 physicalClass
    class W2,W3,W4 cieClass
    class W5,W6,W7,W8 perceptClass
    class W9,W10 outputClass
```

## 4.4 Opponent-Process Channel Architecture
