The retinal ganglion cells and LGN organize cone signals into three opponent channels. This block diagram explicitly shows the linear-algebra combination of LMS signals into the perceptually-relevant channels.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0[L Cone Signal Long Wavelength] --> O0[Subtraction Node]
    M0[M Cone Signal Medium Wavelength] --> O0
    M0 --> O1[Subtraction Node]
    S0[S Cone Signal Short Wavelength] --> O1
    L0 --> O2[Additive Node L plus M]
    M0 --> O2

    O0 --> R0[Red Green Opponent Channel R minus G]
    O1 --> B0[Blue Yellow Opponent Channel B minus Y]
    O2 --> A0[Achromatic Luminance Channel]

    R0 --> C0[Visual Cortex V1]
    B0 --> C0
    A0 --> C0
    C0 --> P0[Perceived Color Hue Saturation Lightness]

    classDef coneNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef oppNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef brainNode fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF

    class L0,M0,S0,O2 coneNode
    class O0,O1,R0,B0,A0 oppNode
    class C0,P0 brainNode
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> For the topic "Perception of Color," students **most commonly lose marks** in the following ways:
> 1. **Confusing Trichromatic with Opponent-Process Theory.** Marks are awarded only when you explicitly say *trichromacy* operates at the **receptor level** and *opponency* at the **ganglion/LGN level**. A vague "color theory" answer scores 2 out of 7.
> 2. **Forgetting the stage-theory integration.** Always end a "compare and contrast" answer with the line: *"Modern vision science reconciles both via the Hurvich–Jameson Stage Theory."*
> 3. **Memorizing the sRGB matrix without units.** The matrix transforms **linear-light** values, not gamma-encoded values. Forgetting this loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up L\*a\*b\* with HSV.** HSV is a heuristic color picker. CIELAB is a perceptually uniform metric. Examiners WILL deduct marks if you equate them.
> 5. **Neglecting VR-specific implications.** Every theory must be tied back to a VR use case (HMD calibration, chromatic aberration, color constancy under headlight, etc.) to score full marks in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define color perception. List the three types of cone photoreceptors and their peak wavelength sensitivities.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Color perception is the psychophysical process by which the human visual system detects visible electromagnetic radiation (380–750 nm) via photoreceptors and interprets the resulting neural signals as distinct hue, saturation, and brightness attributes.

**Three Cone Types (2 Marks):**

| **Cone Type** | **Peak Sensitivity** | **Color Region** |
|---------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| L-cones (Long)  | ~564 nm            | Red-yellow         |
| M-cones (Medium) | ~534 nm            | Green              |
| S-cones (Short)  | ~420 nm            | Blue-violet        |

The relative populations are approximately L : M : S = 40% : 20% : 2%, with the remaining ~38% being rods.

---

### **Question 2: What is the difference between additive and subtractive color mixing? Give one VR example for each.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Additive Color Mixing (1.5 Marks):** Mixing of *emitted* light. When two or more colored lights overlap, the result is the **sum** of their spectral power distributions. Primary colors are **Red, Green, Blue (RGB)**. The sum of all three at full intensity is **white**. Used in HMD micro-OLED/LCD subpixels.

**Subtractive Color Mixing (1.5 Marks):** Mixing of *reflected* light via pigments/inks. Each pigment **absorbs** (subtracts) certain wavelengths and reflects the rest. Primary colors are **Cyan, Magenta, Yellow (CMY)**. The combination of all three is theoretically **black**. Used in physically printed VR tracking markers and reference cards.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has internal choice (Q. (a) OR Q. (b)). You must answer **one full question** worth 14 marks, typically split as 7 + 7 across two sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Trichromatic Theory** of color vision in detail. Describe its physiological basis, experimental support, and the limitations that led to the **Opponent-Process Theory**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **CIELAB color space** in detail. Derive the $(L^*, a^*, b^*)$ values for a linear sRGB color $(0.5, 0.3, 0.1)$ under the D65 illuminant. Compute the color difference $\Delta E^*_{ab}$ between this color and a second color with $(L^*, a^*, b^*) = (60, 8, 40)$. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Trichromatic Theory (Young–Helmholtz) — 4 Marks**

1. **Statement:** Color vision is mediated by exactly **three** types of cone photoreceptors in the retina, each containing a different photopigment with a distinct spectral absorption curve.
2. **Physiological basis:**
   - Discovered via microspectrophotometry of single cones.
   - The three cone types are the **L-cones** ($\lambda_{\max} \approx 564$ nm), **M-cones** ($\lambda_{\max} \approx 534$ nm), and **S-cones** ($\lambda_{\max} \approx 420$ nm).
   - All three cone types are present in the fovea, with a near absence of S-cones outside the central 20° of visual angle.
3. **Experimental support:**
   - Trichromacy predicts that any color can be matched by a linear combination of three suitably chosen primaries. This is the foundation of modern colorimetry and the CIE RGB matching experiments.
   - Color blindness (protanopia, deuteranopia, tritanopia) corresponds to the loss of one cone type, confirming the three-channel architecture.
4. **Mathematical form:** The perceived response is a 3-vector:

$$\mathbf{C} = \int_{\lambda} S(\lambda) \begin{bmatrix} L(\lambda) \\ M(\lambda) \\ S(\lambda) \end{bmatrix} d\lambda$$

where $S(\lambda)$ is the spectral power distribution of the stimulus and $L(\lambda), M(\lambda), S(\lambda)$ are the cone fundamentals.

**Opponent-Process Theory (Hering) — 2 Marks**

1. **Statement:** At the neural level (retinal ganglion cells and LGN), color information is encoded in **opponent channels**: **Red–Green**, **Blue–Yellow**, and an achromatic **Black–White** (luminance) channel.
2. **Motivation:** Explains why we **never** perceive a **reddish-green** or **bluish-yellow** hue — the neural wiring is difference-based, not addition-based.
3. **Reconciliation with Trichromacy:** The Hurvich–Jameson **Stage Theory** (1957) integrates both: trichromacy at the receptor level, opponency at the post-receptor neural level.

**VR Design Implications — 1 Mark**

In VR HMDs, color is encoded as RGB subpixel intensities (trichromatic), but perceptually-relevant differences are measured using opponent-like axes (e.g., the $a^*$ and $b^*$ axes of CIELAB). Color difference metrics used in VR calibration should therefore align with opponent axes.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**CIELAB Description (3 Marks)**

The CIE L\*a\*b\* color space (1976) is a **perceptually uniform** color space, meaning that a unit step in any direction corresponds to approximately the same perceived color difference. It is derived from CIE XYZ by applying a cube-root compression to the ratios with the reference white point. The axes are:
- **$L^*$:** Lightness (0 = black, 100 = white)
- **$a^*$:** Red–Green axis (positive = red, negative = green)
- **$b^*$:** Blue–Yellow axis (positive = yellow, negative = blue)

**Derivation of $(L^*, a^*, b^*)$ for the Given Color (3 Marks)**

This derivation is identical to Section 3.3 above. The key steps are:
1. Convert linear sRGB $(0.5, 0.3, 0.1)$ to CIE XYZ using the sRGB matrix:

$$\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4124 & 0.3576 & 0.1805 \\ 0.2126 & 0.7152 & 0.0722 \\ 0.0193 & 0.1192 & 0.9505 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3316 \\ 0.3281 \\ 0.1405 \end{bmatrix}$$

2. Use D65 reference white $(X_n, Y_n, Z_n) = (0.9505, 1.0000, 1.0890)$ and apply the cube-root function $f(t) = t^{1/3}$ for $t > 0.008856$:

$$L^* = 116 \cdot f(0.3281) - 16 \approx 63.92$$

$$a^* = 500 \cdot [f(0.3316/0.9505) - f(0.3281/1.0000)] = 500 \cdot [f(0.3489) - f(0.3281)] = 500 \cdot [0.7034 - 0.6890] = 7.20$$

$$b^* = 200 \cdot [f(0.3281/1.0000) - f(0.1405/1.0890)] = 200 \cdot [f(0.3281) - f(0.1290)] = 200 \cdot [0.6890 - 0.5051] = 36.78$$

**Color Difference Calculation (1 Mark)**

Given two colors: $C_1 = (63.92, 7.20, 36.78)$ and $C_2 = (60, 8, 40)$:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta E^*_{ab} &= \sqrt{(63.92 - 60)^2 + (7.20 - 8)^2 + (36.78 - 40)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(3.92)^2 + (-0.80)^2 + (-3.22)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{15.3664 + 0.6400 + 10.3684} \\ &= \sqrt{26.3748} \\ &\approx 5.14 \end{aligned}$$

Since $\Delta E^*_{ab} \approx 5.14$ exceeds the "just noticeable difference" threshold of 1.0, the two colors are **clearly distinguishable** to a standard observer.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **opponent-process theory** of color vision. List the three opponent channels and the neural locations where they are first encoded. Discuss why this theory is essential for VR **binocular color rivalry** mitigation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, describe the complete **VR color reproduction pipeline** from 3D scene radiance to perceived color in the HMD. Highlight **three** VR-specific color perception phenomena and their design remedies. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Opponent-Process Theory (Hering, 1878) — 3 Marks**

The opponent-process theory posits that the human visual system encodes color in three **antagonistic** channels rather than three independent primaries. The channels are:

1. **Red–Green (R–G) channel:** A positive signal encodes *redness*; a negative signal encodes *greenness*. The cell fires maximally when stimulated by long-wavelength light and is suppressed by medium-wavelength light.
2. **Blue–Yellow (B–Y) channel:** A positive signal encodes *blueness*; a negative signal encodes *yellowness*. S-cone input drives one sign; combined L+M input drives the other.
3. **Achromatic (Black–White) channel:** Carries the sum of L and M cone signals (luminance). It is a non-opponent channel that drives perceived brightness.

**Neural Locations (2 Marks)**

- The opponent channels are **first encoded** at the level of the **retinal ganglion cells**, specifically the **midget (parvocellular) ganglion cells** (R–G and luminance) and the **small bistratified ganglion cells** (B–Y).
- These axons project via the **optic nerve** to the **lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN)** of the thalamus, where the opponency is preserved and refined.
- From the LGN, the signals travel through the **optic radiations** to the **primary visual cortex (V1)**, where the first cortical color-processing area (the *blobs*) further processes them.

**VR Binocular Color Rivalry — 2 Marks**

In a stereoscopic HMD, the left and right eyes view slightly different images to produce binocular disparity (depth). If the **color balance** of the two eye-paths drifts apart due to:
- Differential aging of micro-LED subpixels,
- Mis-calibrated per-eye gamma curves,
- Unequal IPD (interpupillary distance) compensation,

then the user perceives a perceptual rivalry where one color dominates the other in alternation. To mitigate this:
- Each HMD must be **per-eye color-calibrated** at the factory and re-calibrated periodically.
- The VR engine must use a **color management pipeline** that explicitly tracks chromaticity per eye-buffer.
- The headset should expose a **white-point adjustment** that compensates for differential subpixel aging.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Block Diagram of VR Color Pipeline (4 Marks)**

The complete VR color reproduction pipeline is as follows:

1. **Scene Radiance (HDR Linear RGB):** The 3D engine computes physically-correct linear-light radiance $L_e(\mathbf{x}, \omega)$ at each pixel using PBR (physically-based rendering).
2. **Tone Mapping:** A tone-mapping operator (e.g., ACES, Reinhard, or Filmic) compresses HDR to LDR while preserving perceptual contrast. Output is a linear-light LDR value.
3. **Gamma Encoding:** The linear LDR is converted to non-linear sRGB via the transfer function in Section 3.1.
4. **Display Panel Emission:** The HMD's micro-display emits light at subpixel intensities proportional to the gamma-encoded values, modulated by the display's LUT and any color filter.
5. **HMD Optics:** The lenses introduce **chromatic aberration** (different wavelengths focus at different depths), **vignetting** (peripheral dimming), and **distortion** (barrel/pincushion).
6. **Retinal Image:** The corrected light forms a retinal image where LMS cones are stimulated.
7. **Perceptual Pipeline:** The HVS processes these stimuli through opponent channels and cortical mechanisms to produce a conscious percept.

**Three VR-Specific Color Perception Phenomena and Remedies (3 Marks)**

| **#** | **Phenomenon**                  | **VR Manifestation**                                 | **Design Remedy**                                              |
|-------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1     | **Chromatic Aberration**         | Color fringing at lens edges, eye strain             | Use achromatic doublet lenses; apply software pre-compensation|
| 2     | **Vergence–Accommodation Conflict** combined with chromatic defocus | Different wavelengths focus at different depths, worsening VAC | Use varifocal or holographic displays; narrow-band primaries  |
| 3     | **Color Constancy Failure**      | VR scene colors look "wrong" when room lighting changes | Render with assumed D65 white point; provide white-point UI   |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bonus Concept (1 mark):** The phenomenon of **Pulfrich effect** — a depth illusion caused by interocular difference in luminance (or color) — is also relevant. A darker view to one eye is interpreted as a delayed signal, producing a perceived depth slant. This means **unbalanced per-eye brightness** in a VR HMD can produce geometric depth distortions. Always balance luminance per eye.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- [ ] **Visible spectrum** is 380–750 nm. Photon energy $E = hc/\lambda$. Longer wavelength = lower energy.
- [ ] **Three cone types** peak at ~564 nm (L), ~534 nm (M), ~420 nm (S). Relative populations: L:M:S ≈ 40:20:2.
- [ ] **Trichromatic theory** operates at the **photoreceptor** level; **opponent-process** operates at the **ganglion / LGN** level. Modern view: **Stage Theory** integrates both.
- [ ] **Three opponent channels:** Red–Green, Blue–Yellow, Black–White (achromatic).
- [ ] **sRGB matrix** (linear sRGB → CIE XYZ) and its **inverse** — memorize the 3×3 matrix to 4 decimal places.
- [ ] **Luminance** $Y = 0.2126R + 0.7152G + 0.0722B$ (Rec.709 / sRGB).
- [ ] **sRGB gamma encoding** is a piecewise function with a linear segment below 0.0031308 and a power-law segment $1.055 \cdot V^{1/2.4} - 0.055$ above.
- [ ] **CIELAB** is the perceptually uniform color space; $L^*$ is lightness, $a^*$ is red-green, $b^*$ is blue-yellow.
- [ ] **CIE76 color difference** $\Delta E^*_{ab} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$. Threshold for "just noticeable" is ~1.0.
- [ ] **VR color pipeline order:** Scene radiance (HDR) → Tone mapping → Gamma encoding → Display emission → Optics → Retinal image → HVS processing → Percept.
- [ ] **VR-specific phenomena to know:** simultaneous contrast, Bezold–Brücke shift, Helmholtz–Kohlrausch, color constancy, chromatic adaptation, binocular color rivalry, Pulfrich effect, Troxler fading.
- [ ] **Stevens' Power Law** for brightness: $\psi = k (L - L_0)^n$ with $n \approx 0.33$ — the eye compresses physical intensity.
- [ ] **Weber's Law:** $\Delta I / I = k$ — fractional sensitivity is constant across a wide range of intensities.
- [ ] The **CIE 1931 XYZ** color space is defined so that the Y component is luminance and all visible colors have non-negative coordinates.
- [ ] The **CIE RGB primaries** at 700 nm (R), 546.1 nm (G), 435.8 nm (B) are not realizable by any physical light source — they are mathematical constructs.
- [ ] **Display gamma ≠ HVS gamma.** Display gamma (~2.2) encodes for storage; the HVS effective gamma is ~1.0 to 1.5 depending on adaptation level.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** For any 14-mark color perception question, structure your answer in three parts: **(1)** State the theory with its physiological basis (3–4 marks), **(2)** Derive or compute the relevant equations (3–4 marks), **(3)** Connect to a concrete VR application (3–4 marks). Examiners explicitly look for the VR linkage — without it, you will not score above 10/14.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interaction - Motor Programs and Remapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT504 - VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interaction - Motor Programs and Remapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Motor Programs** in Virtual Reality refer to the pre-structured, executable sequences of motor commands encoded in the human sensorimotor system (or pre-defined software libraries) that translate the user's intended physical actions into deterministic virtual interactions without requiring conscious deliberation for each sub-movement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensorimotor Remapping** is the systematic re-association, redirection, or transformation of the natural mapping between the user's motor output (kinesthetic, proprioceptive actions) and the resulting sensory input (visual, haptic, auditory feedback) in the virtual environment, in order to overcome physical limitations of the real workspace, hardware constraints, or to enhance presence and immersion.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the human operator's **central nervous system (CNS)** acts as a high-level controller that issues **motor programs**, while the **VR system** acts as a low-level executor that performs **remapping** between real and virtual spaces to ensure effective transfer of motor skills.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 – "The Steering Wheel Translator":** Imagine driving a foreign truck whose steering wheel, pedals, and gearshift are all reversed and repositioned. After a few minutes of *re-mapping* in your brain, you can drive naturally because your **motor program** ("to turn left, rotate wheel counter-clockwise") is automatically *remapped* to a new control scheme. VR remapping works the same way — it teaches the brain to issue familiar motions that produce different (or augmented) virtual outcomes.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 – "The Puppeteer":** Your real hands hold a pair of sensor gloves. The VR system is the *puppeteer* who attaches your real hand's strings to a *virtual giant's hands*. When you move your hand 5 cm, the giant's hand moves 50 cm. This stretching of the mapping is **motor remapping**. The motor program in your brain remains "move hand forward," but the effect in the virtual world is amplified.

### Key Performance Metrics in Remapping

- **Just Noticeable Difference (JND)**: The smallest remapping amount the human perceptual system cannot detect. For visual reorientation this is approximately **2°/s to 7.5°/s** of rotation gain change.
- **Curvature Gain ($g_c$)**: Ratio of real-to-virtual path curvature.
- **Translation Gain ($g_t$)**: Ratio of real-to-virtual linear displacement.
- **Rotation Gain ($g_\theta$)**: Ratio of real-to-virtual angular displacement.
- **Isomorphism**: The perfect, one-to-one, 1:1 mapping with **unity gain (1.0)** in all dimensions.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Interaction Modalities

| Class | Trigger Source | VR Output | Typical Hardware |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Selection** | Gaze, pinch, gesture | Object highlighting | Eye tracker, data glove |
| **Manipulation** | Hand pose, force | Object translation/rotation | Haptic glove, controller |
| **Navigation** | Walking, leaning, teleporting | Viewpoint change | Treadmill, omnidirectional platform |
| **System Control** | Voice, menu gesture | UI interaction | Microphone, gesture cam |
| **Symbolic Input** | Handwriting, sketching | 2D/3D strokes | Stylus, data glove |

---

## 1.4 Why Motor Programs Matter in VR

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without proper motor programming, users suffer from **cybersickness**, **reduced presence**, and **transfer failure** (where skills learned in VR do not transfer to the real world). The CNS uses **feedforward internal models** that predict the sensory consequences of motor commands. If the VR system violates these predictions (e.g., unnatural remapping), the mismatch produces **sensory conflict** and breaks immersion.

### The Closed Sensorimotor Loop in VR

The user issues a motor command → body executes real motion → sensors capture the motion → VR engine remaps & renders → sensory display presents feedback → user perceives & adjusts. This loop must operate within approximately **10–20 ms** to feel natural (the threshold for **real-time interactivity**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Motor Program Theory (Schmidt's Schema Theory)

Richard Schmidt's **Schema Theory** (1975) treats motor programs as abstract, generalized motor memories, not as rigid, muscle-specific scripts. A motor program contains four key elements:

1. **Response Specification (R-S)**: What movement to perform.
2. **Recognition Schema (R-R)**: The expected sensory consequences.
3. **Recall Schema (R-E)**: Converts intentions into parameters (force, speed, direction).
4. **Correction Mechanism**: Compares expected vs. actual sensory feedback for error correction.

> [!NOTE]
> In VR, this schema is exploited via **feedforward control**: the system predicts the next state, renders it speculatively (rendering in advance based on trajectory prediction), and updates upon actual sensor data.

## 2.2 Hierarchy of Motor Control in VR

| Level | Real Human Control | VR Equivalent | Latency Tolerance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strategic** | Decide to "pick up cup" | High-level task planner | > 1 s |
| **Tactical** | Choose grasp, approach angle | Gesture recognizer (e.g., LSTM, HMM) | 100–500 ms |
| **Operational** | Contract specific muscles | Joint actuator / haptic rendering | 1–10 ms |
| **Reflexive** | Stretch reflex | Collision response engine | < 1 ms |

## 2.3 Types of Remapping — The Three Core Categories

### 2.3.1 World Remapping
The virtual world itself is **geometrically warped** to accommodate real-world limitations.

- **Redirected Walking (RDW)**: The virtual scene is curved/rotated so the user walks in circles in reality but walks straight in VR.
- **Change Blindness Techniques**: Imperceptible scene modifications between saccades.
- **Dynamic Restraints**: Virtual walls or moving platforms that the user perceives as real.

### 2.3.2 Control Remapping
The mapping between input device and virtual effect is **algebraically transformed**.

- **Scaled Translations**: A 1 cm real hand motion → 10 cm virtual hand motion.
- **Index Mapping**: Button 2 in reality triggers Button 5 virtually.
- **Mode Switching**: Same gesture → different effects based on context.

### 2.3.3 Body Remapping
The user's **avatar body parts are reassigned** to different roles or sizes.

- **Rubber Hand Illusion (RHI)**: A virtual hand replaces the real hand perceptually.
- **Full-Body Ownership Transfer (Body Warping)**: The user embodies a giant, a child, or a non-human avatar.
- **Haptic Retargeting**: A real hand touches a passive prop, but the virtual hand appears to touch a completely different object in VR.

## 2.4 The Isomorphism Principle (Sheridan, 1992)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Isomorphism** demands that the geometric, kinematic, and dynamic relationships between the user's motor actions and the resulting sensory displays be preserved 1:1. Sheridan classified VR systems into **non-immersive, immersive, and** **mixed reality**, with **isomorphism as the gold standard** for training simulators.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Description | Standard Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $g_t$ | $g_t = \dfrac{\Delta x_{virtual}}{\Delta x_{real}}$ | Translation Gain | **0.86 – 1.26** (Steinicke et al.) |
| $g_\theta$ | $g_\theta = \dfrac{\Delta \theta_{virtual}}{\Delta \theta_{real}}$ | Rotation Gain | **0.67 – 1.24** |
| $g_c$ | $g_c = \dfrac{R_{real}}{R_{virtual}}$ | Curvature Gain | **–7.51 to +7.51 m⁻¹** |
| $g_v$ | $g_v = \dfrac{v_{virtual}}{v_{real}}$ | Velocity Gain | 0.9 – 1.1 typical |
| $D$ | $D = \dfrac{1}{g_t}$ | Scale Factor | dimensionless |
| $f_{update}$ | $f_{update} \ge 90$ Hz | Refresh Rate | Hz |
| $\tau_{latency}$ | $\tau_{latency} \le 20$ ms | End-to-end delay | milliseconds |
| $\text{PSNR}$ | $\text{PSNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{MAX_I}{\sqrt{MSE}}\right)$ | Image Quality | dB |
| $S_{presence}$ | $S_{presence} \propto \dfrac{1}{\tau_{latency} \cdot \lvert 1 - g_t \rvert}$ | Presence Score | qualitative |
| $E_{transfer}$ | $E_{transfer} = 1 - \dfrac{\lvert \text{skill}_{VR} - \text{skill}_{real} \rvert}{\text{skill}_{real}}$ | Skill Transfer Efficiency | 0 – 1 (1 = perfect) |

> [!WARNING]
> **No `|` characters in the above table** — uses `\vert` and `\lvert` macros to keep the markdown table parser intact.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Surgical Simulators (e.g., da Vinci Trainer)**: Isomorphic force-feedback mapping trains residents without risk to patients.
- **Flight Simulators**: 6-DOF motion platforms with inverse kinematic remapping to a 2 m hexapod that mimics 30 m of aircraft travel.
- **Rehabilitation Robotics**: Patients relearn grasp motor programs by remapping affected limbs to a virtual avatar.
- **Industrial CAD**: 1:1 hand scaling to inspect 200 m turbine blades in a 2 m room.
- **AR Glasses (Apple Vision Pro, Meta Quest 3)**: Pinch-and-drag gestures remapped to distant holographic widgets.

## 2.7 Detection Thresholds (Steinicke et al., 2008–2010)

Steinicke's experimental findings form the foundation of undetectable remapping limits:

$$\Delta g_{t,\text{detect}} \approx 0.14 \quad \text{(14% change in translation gain is just noticeable)}$$

$$\Delta g_{\theta,\text{detect}} \approx 7.5^\circ / \text{s} \quad \text{(rotation injection threshold)}$$

$$\Delta g_{c,\text{detect}} \approx 1 / 7.51 \text{ m}^{-1} \quad \text{(curvature threshold for straight walking)}$$

These thresholds are the **operational envelope** for designers: stay within them and the remapping is *imperceptible*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Curvature Gain Relationship for Redirected Walking

We want to derive the relationship between real-space path curvature and virtual-space path curvature for **smooth, gentle redirection**.

### 3.1.1 Geometric Setup

Let a user walk in the real world along a **circle of radius** $R_{real}$. The virtual scene is rendered as if the user walks along a circle of radius $R_{virtual}$. Define:

$$g_c = \frac{R_{real}}{R_{virtual}}$$

**Step 1**: Express the angular velocity of the user about the real circle center.

Let the user walk a **real distance** $s_{real} = R_{real} \cdot \theta_{real}$ in time $t$, where $\theta_{real}$ is the swept angle in radians.

$$\omega_{real} = \frac{d\theta_{real}}{dt} = \frac{v_{real}}{R_{real}}$$

**Step 2**: Express the angular velocity about the virtual circle center.

$$s_{virtual} = R_{virtual} \cdot \theta_{virtual}$$

By the **isomorphism rule** with respect to translation, the real distance walked is rendered as virtual distance with translation gain $g_t$:

$$s_{virtual} = g_t \cdot s_{real}$$

**Step 3**: Equate the two expressions for $s_{virtual}$.

$$R_{virtual} \cdot \theta_{virtual} = g_t \cdot R_{real} \cdot \theta_{real}$$

**Step 4**: Differentiate both sides with respect to time.

$$R_{virtual} \cdot \omega_{virtual} = g_t \cdot R_{real} \cdot \omega_{real}$$

**Step 5**: Solve for $\omega_{virtual}$ in terms of $\omega_{real}$.

$$\omega_{virtual} = g_t \cdot \frac{R_{real}}{R_{virtual}} \cdot \omega_{real}$$

**Step 6**: Recognize the curvature gain definition.

$$\omega_{virtual} = g_t \cdot g_c \cdot \omega_{real}$$

**Final Identity — Curvature Gain Equation:**

$$\boxed{\,g_c = \frac{\omega_{virtual}}{g_t \cdot \omega_{real}} = \frac{R_{real}}{R_{virtual}}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** To make the user *believe* they are walking straight in VR ($\omega_{virtual} = 0$) while they are actually turning in reality ($\omega_{real} \neq 0$), we set:

$$\omega_{virtual} = 0 \implies g_c \cdot \omega_{real} = 0 \implies R_{virtual} = \infty$$

This is the **straight-walking illusion** achieved by **infinite virtual radius** with bounded real turning.

### 3.1.2 Numerical Worked Example

A user walks 5 m in real space. The real path is a circle of radius 4 m. The virtual scene is rendered as a straight hallway ($R_{virtual} = \infty$, $g_t = 1.0$).

**Given:**
- $R_{real} = 4$ m
- $R_{virtual} = \infty$
- $g_t = 1.0$

**Step 1:** Compute real turning angle.

$$\theta_{real} = \frac{s_{real}}{R_{real}} = \frac{5}{4} = 1.25 \text{ rad} \approx 71.6^\circ$$

**Step 2:** Compute curvature gain.

$$g_c = \frac{R_{real}}{R_{virtual}} = \frac{4}{\infty} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Compute virtual turning angle.

$$\theta_{virtual} = g_t \cdot g_c \cdot \theta_{real} = 1.0 \cdot 0 \cdot 1.25 = 0 \text{ rad}$$

The user is **fully redirected** to believe they walked straight, while physically executing a 5 m arc.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Effective Velocity Gain in Haptic Retargeting

In **Haptic Retargeting**, the real hand reaches for a passive prop placed at position $P_{real}$. The virtual hand appears to touch a different object at $P_{virtual}$ in the virtual world. We derive the equivalent **grip transformation matrix**.

**Step 1:** Define real hand position vector.

$$\vec{H}_{real}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} x_{real}(t) \\ y_{real}(t) \\ z_{real}(t) \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Define virtual hand position vector through remapping function $f: \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^3$.

$$\vec{H}_{virtual}(t) = f(\vec{H}_{real}(t))$$

**Step 3:** For **linear remapping**, choose $f$ as an affine transform.

$$f(\vec{H}) = M \cdot \vec{H} + \vec{b}$$

where $M \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ is the **remapping matrix** and $\vec{b} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ is the **offset vector**.

**Step 4:** For an **isomorphic, scaled** remapping along the line of sight.

$$M = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \lambda \end{pmatrix}, \quad \vec{b} = \vec{0}$$

where $\lambda$ is the **depth remapping factor**.

**Step 5:** The Jacobian gives the **instantaneous velocity gain** at the hand.

$$J = \frac{\partial \vec{H}_{virtual}}{\partial \vec{H}_{real}} = M = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \lambda \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 6:** Magnitude of velocity transformation.

$$g_v = \lVert J \vec{v}_{real} \rVert / \lVert \vec{v}_{real} \rVert$$

For a pure depth motion $\vec{v}_{real} = (0, 0, 1)^T$:

$$g_v = \lambda$$

**Final Result:** The depth remapping factor $\lambda$ is the **velocity gain along the remapped axis**. For a value $\lambda = 0.7$, a 10 cm real hand motion is rendered as 7 cm in the virtual world, allowing a single real prop to serve as haptic proxy for multiple virtual objects.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Trade-offs of Each Remapping Type

| Remapping Type | Pros | Cons | Best Use Case | Detection Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Redirected Walking** | Natural locomotion, large VEs | Requires tracking space | Architecture, military training | $g_c \in [-7.51, 7.51]$ m⁻¹ |
| **Haptic Retargeting** | Single physical prop serves many virtual objects | Visual-haptic mismatch risk | Shape manipulation | $\lvert 1 - \lambda \rvert \le 0.13$ |
| **World-in-Miniature** | Whole world navigable from wrist | Cognitive load | Map design, CAD | N/A (visual scale) |
| **Voodoo Dolls** | Precise object positioning | Learning curve | Assembly tasks | N/A |
| **Body Warping** | Strong embodiment, body transfer | Motion sickness if rapid | Rehabilitation, empathy training | $\lvert 1 - g_t \rvert \le 0.14$ |
| **Travel via Leaning** | Intuitive for seated users | Fatigue, limited range | Cockpit, racing | $\theta_{lean} \le 30^\circ$ |

---

## 3.4 Mathematical Model of Sensory Conflict in Remapping

When the motor program predicts a sensory outcome $S_{predicted}$ and the actual rendered outcome is $S_{rendered}$, the **conflict signal** $C$ is:

$$C(t) = S_{rendered}(t) - S_{predicted}(t)$$

The user's **sense of presence** $P$ decreases monotonically with the time-integrated absolute conflict:

$$P = P_{max} - \alpha \int_0^T \lvert C(\tau) \rvert \, d\tau$$

where $\alpha$ is an empirically derived **conflict penalty coefficient** (typically $\alpha \approx 0.1$ per second for VR locomotion).

**Key insight:** To maximize presence, the remapping must satisfy:

$$\int_0^T \lvert C(\tau) \rvert \, d\tau \le \int_0^T \lvert C_{threshold}(\tau) \rvert \, d\tau$$

i.e., the integrated conflict must remain **below the perceptual detection threshold at all times**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define "Motor Program" in the context of Virtual Reality. How does Schmidt's Schema Theory apply to VR interaction design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Motor Program** is a pre-structured set of motor commands that, once initiated, can be executed with minimal conscious feedback. In VR, it represents the abstract representation of a movement (e.g., grasp, point, push) that the human CNS issues without focusing on individual muscle contractions.

**Schmidt's Schema Theory** (1975) applies to VR as follows:

- **Recall Schema** stores the *initial conditions* and *parameters* (force, speed) — analogous to the VR gesture library (e.g., grasp template with joint angles).
- **Recognition Schema** stores *expected sensory outcomes* — analogous to the VR system's predicted haptic/visual response used for **feedforward control**.
- VR design must align **recall schema (motor output)** with **recognition schema (sensory feedback)** for **transfer of training** without cybersickness.

*(Mark split: 1 mark definition + 2 marks schema theory mapping)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the THREE primary categories of Sensorimotor Remapping in VR.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Control Remapping** (1 mark): Reassigns the relationship between *input device actions* and *virtual effects*. Example: scaling a 1 cm real hand motion to 10 cm virtual hand motion (translation gain).

2. **World Remapping** (1 mark): Geometrically warps the *virtual environment* to accommodate the limited *real workspace*. Example: Redirected Walking where the user walks a small real circle but perceives a straight path.

3. **Body Remapping** (1 mark): Reassigns the *user's avatar body parts* to different roles, sizes, or even non-human forms. Example: Body Warping, where a user sees themselves as a 10-meter giant in a virtual room.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **omit diagrams** for "Remapping" questions — expect to lose 2 marks.
> - **Numerical values of detection thresholds** (e.g., 0.14 for translation, 7.5°/s for rotation) are **frequently asked** as 2-mark sub-parts. Memorize them.
> - Vague statements like "remapping improves immersion" without citing the **isomorphism principle** or **Steinicke's thresholds** lose 1–2 marks.
> - Always mention **feedforward control** and **sensory conflict** when discussing remapping.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Long Answer**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Motor Programs** in human motor control. Discuss how **Schmidt's Schema Theory** governs the design of VR interaction systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat block diagrams, classify and explain the **three categories of sensorimotor remapping** in VR. Provide at least **one real-world application** for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

**1. Definition of Motor Program (2 marks):**
A motor program is a pre-structured, abstract neural representation of a movement that can be executed with minimal conscious attention. In VR, it corresponds to **pre-defined gesture libraries** that the system can interpret to perform meaningful actions (e.g., a "pinch" gesture mapped to object selection).

**2. Schmidt's Schema Theory (3 marks):**
Schmidt proposed **two schemas**:
- **Recall Schema** — stores motor parameters (force, speed, direction). In VR, this is the *gesture parameterization* module (e.g., for a "throw" gesture, it stores wrist acceleration thresholds).
- **Recognition Schema** — stores expected sensory outcomes. In VR, this corresponds to *predicted sensory feedback* used in **feedforward rendering** (speculative rendering of expected post-movement state).
- **Error Correction**: The Recognition Schema compares expected vs. actual feedback and triggers **motor adaptation**, which in VR corresponds to dynamic recalibration of remapping gains.

**3. Application to VR Design (2 marks):**
VR designers structure interaction around the user's existing motor programs to leverage **transfer of training**. Example: In a surgical simulator, the resident's existing **grasp-and-retract** motor program is reused, so skills transfer directly from VR to the operating room.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**Three Categories of Remapping:**

**1. Control Remapping (2.5 marks):**
- Definition: Alters the algebraic relationship between input and output.
- Example: A 1:1 hand-to-hand mapping scaled to 1:5 for inspecting small parts.
- Application: **VR CAD inspection** of microchips using scaled hand motion.

**2. World Remapping (2.5 marks):**
- Definition: Warps the virtual world geometry.
- Example: Redirected Walking — user walks 4 m circle in real, perceives straight 40 m corridor in VR.
- Application: **Military training simulators** in small VR labs.

**3. Body Remapping (2 marks):**
- Definition: Reassigns body parts to new roles/sizes.
- Example: Haptic Retargeting — single physical prop serves as 4 different virtual objects via dynamic remapping.
- Application: **Rehabilitation** — stroke patients relearn grasp by mapping a healthy hand's motion to a virtual disabled hand.

> Examiner's Note: A block diagram of the **Three-Layer Remapping Architecture** (see Section 4.2 of these notes) earns full credit if included.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Long Answer**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the **curvature gain equation** $g_c = R_{real} / R_{virtual}$ for a Redirected Walking system. Explain the **detection threshold** of $g_c$ as per Steinicke. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Isomorphism** in VR. Compute the **virtual turning angle** if a user walks 6 m along a real circle of radius 3 m, with translation gain $g_t = 1.0$ and the virtual scene rendered as a straight hallway. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Set up geometric definitions:
Real path: $s_{real} = R_{real} \cdot \theta_{real}$, where $\theta_{real}$ is the real swept angle.
Virtual path: $s_{virtual} = R_{virtual} \cdot \theta_{virtual}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply translation gain $g_t$:
$s_{virtual} = g_t \cdot s_{real}$
$R_{virtual} \cdot \theta_{virtual} = g_t \cdot R_{real} \cdot \theta_{real}$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Differentiate wrt time:
$R_{virtual} \cdot \omega_{virtual} = g_t \cdot R_{real} \cdot \omega_{real}$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Solve for $\omega_{virtual}/\omega_{real}$:
$\dfrac{\omega_{virtual}}{\omega_{real}} = g_t \cdot \dfrac{R_{real}}{R_{virtual}}$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** By definition, $g_c = R_{real} / R_{virtual}$, hence:
$\boxed{g_c = \dfrac{\omega_{virtual}}{g_t \cdot \omega_{real}}}$

**Detection Threshold (2 marks):**
Steinicke's experiments (2010) established that the threshold for **just noticeable curvature** is approximately $1/7.51$ m⁻¹. Curvature gains within $[-7.51, +7.51]$ m⁻¹ are **imperceptible**, allowing safe redirection. Beyond this, users detect the bending and report discomfort.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Isomorphism Definition (3 marks):**
Isomorphism is the property where the geometric, kinematic, and dynamic relationships between the user's real actions and the virtual sensory feedback are preserved in a **one-to-one (1:1) mapping**. Introduced by Thomas Sheridan (1992), it is the *gold standard* for VR simulators used in training (e.g., flight simulators, surgical trainers) where skill transfer is critical.

**Numerical Computation (4 marks):**

**Given:**
- $s_{real} = 6$ m
- $R_{real} = 3$ m
- $g_t = 1.0$
- Virtual scene = straight hallway → $R_{virtual} = \infty$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the real swept angle.

$$\theta_{real} = \frac{s_{real}}{R_{real}} = \frac{6}{3} = 2.0 \text{ rad} \approx 114.6^\circ$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply translation gain to virtual distance.

$$s_{virtual} = g_t \cdot s_{real} = 1.0 \times 6 = 6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute virtual swept angle.

$$\theta_{virtual} = \frac{s_{virtual}}{R_{virtual}} = \frac{6}{\infty} = 0 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Interpretation.

The user has physically turned 114.6° in the real world, but in VR perceives walking **6 m straight** in a straight hallway. The system is applying **full curvature redirection** with $g_c = 0$ (since $R_{virtual} = \infty$).

**Final Answer:** $\theta_{virtual} = 0$ rad; the user believes they walked 6 m straight, while they actually walked a 6 m arc of a 3 m radius circle.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision List — Pin this on your study wall:**

- **Motor Program** = pre-structured movement; in VR, it is the *gesture library template* executed with minimal conscious control.
- **Schmidt's Schema Theory** has **Recall Schema** (parameters) + **Recognition Schema** (expected sensory feedback) + **Error Correction** loop.
- **Isomorphism** = perfect **1:1 mapping** (Sheridan, 1992); the gold standard for training simulators.
- **Three Remapping Categories**: **Control**, **World**, **Body** — memorize with one example each.
- **Translation Gain** $g_t \in [0.86, 1.26]$; **Rotation Gain** $g_\theta \in [0.67, 1.24]$; **Curvature Gain** $g_c \in [-7.51, +7.51]$ m⁻¹ (Steinicke thresholds).
- **Redirected Walking** lets users traverse infinite VEs in finite real space by warping virtual curvature.
- **Haptic Retargeting** uses a single real prop as proxy for multiple virtual objects via dynamic depth remapping ($\lambda$).
- **Body Warping** enables full-body ownership transfer (e.g., embodying a giant) — used in empathy training and rehabilitation.
- **Sensory Conflict** $C(t) = S_{rendered}(t) - S_{predicted}(t)$ must remain below detection threshold at all times.
- **Feedforward control** uses internal models to predict and pre-render expected states, reducing effective latency.
- **Presence** is inversely proportional to latency and gain deviation: $P \propto 1 / (\tau \cdot \lvert 1 - g \rvert)$.
- **Real-time threshold** = **90 Hz refresh, ≤ 20 ms end-to-end latency**.
- **Skill Transfer Efficiency** $E_{transfer} = 1 - \lvert \text{skill}_{VR} - \text{skill}_{real} \rvert / \text{skill}_{real}$.
- **Rubber Hand Illusion** is the canonical body remapping demonstration; use it in exam answers for extra credit.
- **Hierarchy of motor control**: Strategic → Tactical → Operational → Reflexive.
- **Feedforward vs. Feedback**: Feedforward = predictive; Feedback = reactive. VR primarily uses feedforward with feedback correction.

---

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Locomotion in Virtual Reality (VR)** is defined as the set of techniques, hardware mechanisms, and software algorithms that enable a user to **translate (move through) and rotate (orient within)** a computer-generated virtual environment (VE), independent of, or in coordination with, the user's actual physical motion in real space.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, locomotion is treated as a sub-problem of **immersive interaction**, sitting alongside selection, manipulation, and system control. The central engineering challenge is the **mapping problem**: determining how a 6 Degrees-of-Freedom (6-DOF) or 3-DOF physical movement vector of the user should be transformed into a corresponding virtual movement vector in the simulated world.

Formally, let the user's physical motion be described by the state vector:

$$
S_{real} = (x, y, z, \psi, \theta, \phi)
$$

and the resulting virtual motion be described by:

$$
S_{virtual} = f(S_{real}, \text{map}, \text{gain}, \text{context})
$$

The function $f$ is the **locomotion transfer function**, and designing $f$ is the core of any VR locomotion system.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Locomotion

| Pillar | Engineering Concern | Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **Spatial Translation** | Mapping physical movement to virtual displacement | Move the user from point A to B in the VE |
| **Rotational Orientation** | Mapping physical head/body rotation to virtual view rotation | Update the user's gaze direction |
| **Stability & Comfort** | Suppressing sensory mismatch (vection vs. vestibular) | Avoid **cybersickness** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Magic Carpet vs. The Treadmill

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine two ways to "visit" Paris without leaving your room:
>
> - **The Magic Carpet** is *Teleportation* — you instantly appear at the Eiffel Tower. Fast, no fatigue, but breaks immersion ("I was just in London...").
>
> - **The Treadmill** is *Real Walking* — you actually walk on a treadmill, and the scenery moves past you. Highly natural, but you only cover 3 m² of Paris no matter how long you walk.
>
> VR locomotion is the eternal engineering tug-of-war between the **Magic Carpet** (artificial, free, but disorienting) and the **Treadmill** (natural, but bounded by physical space).

## 1.4 Why Locomotion is a Hard Problem in VR

The locomotion problem is non-trivial because of three hard physical constraints:

1. **The Physical Space Problem:** The real-world tracking area (typically **2 m × 2 m** to **10 m × 10 m** for consumer HMDs) is dramatically smaller than the virtual worlds users wish to explore (which can span square kilometers).

2. **The Vection–Vestibular Conflict:** When the eyes (visual system) report movement but the inner ear (vestibular system) reports stillness, the brain interprets this as **poisoning**, triggering **simulator sickness / cybersickness**.

3. **The Cognitive Load Problem:** The user must perform the *primary task* (navigating to a goal) without being overwhelmed by the *meta-task* (operating the locomotion mechanism).

## 1.5 The Gold Standard Metric

The industry-standard benchmark for natural locomotion is the **Walking Speed** of a healthy adult, measured as:

$$
v_{walk} = \mathbf{1.4 \ m/s}
$$

Any locomotion technique that achieves a virtual speed of ~1.4 m/s with sub-threshold sensory conflict is considered *high fidelity*.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Locomotion Techniques

VR locomotion techniques can be classified along **two orthogonal axes**: (a) whether motion is *real* or *virtual*, and (b) whether the user controls velocity or position.

### 2.1.1 Physical (Real) Locomotion

> [!NOTE]
> The user actually moves their body. Highest presence, smallest infinite-VE coverage.

- **Real Walking (Natural Walking):** Direct 1:1 mapping of physical steps to virtual steps. Requires room-scale tracking; considered the *gold standard* for presence and the lowest cybersickness rate.
- **Walking-in-Place (WIP):** The user walks in a stationary spot; foot sensors, hip accelerometers, or foot-tracker gait patterns detect the stepping cadence and translate it to forward virtual velocity. Solves the space problem while preserving gait-based proprioception.
- **Slip Walking / Shoe-Based Treadmills:** Specialized omnidirectional treadmills (e.g., Virtuix Omni, Cyberith Virtualizer) use low-friction surfaces and a harness to allow the user to actually walk while staying in place.

### 2.1.2 Virtual (Artificial) Locomotion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The user's body remains still; the virtual viewpoint is manipulated by an input device. Maximizes VE coverage; trades presence for freedom.

- **Steering / Continuous Movement:** A joystick, thumbstick, or gamepad analog input drives the virtual camera forward at a constant velocity. The most common consumer technique (e.g., SteamVR Smooth Locomotion). Highest cybersickness risk.
- **Teleportation (Point-and-Click):** The user points a controller at a target location, presses a button, and is instantly translated there. Best for comfort, worst for presence.
- **Grabbing the Air / "Pull" Locomotion:** Grab motion is mapped to a translation vector. Highly natural for short jumps ("ski-pole metaphor").
- **Arm-Swinging Locomotion:** Forward motion is mapped to the speed of arm swinging, exploiting the natural gait-coupling reflex.
- **Vehicle-Based:** The user pilots a virtual vehicle (car, boat, drone). The vehicle's frame of reference absorbs vestibular conflict.
- **Flying / Free Movement:** Vertical and horizontal translation are decoupled, simulating superhuman flight.

### 2.1.3 Hybrid / Manipulated Locomotion

> [!NOTE]
> Combines real walking with imperceptible re-orientation, expanding the effective play area.

- **Redirected Walking (RDW):** Imperceptibly rotates, scales, or curves the virtual scene so the user walks in arcs in reality while perceiving straight-line travel in the VE.
- **Resetting Techniques:** When the user drifts toward a physical wall, a "reset" is triggered (e.g., freeze-and-rotate, 2:1 turn).

## 2.2 The Locomotion Transfer Function (Mathematical Foundation)

For a continuous steering locomotion, the transfer function in 1-D can be modeled as a first-order system. Let $u(t)$ be the joystick deflection (normalized to $[-1, +1]$), $g$ be the system gain (m/s per unit deflection), and $v(t)$ the resulting virtual velocity:

$$
v(t) = g \cdot u(t - \tau)
$$

where $\tau$ is the **input latency** (typically **20–80 ms** in commercial HMDs). The virtual position is then:

$$
x_{virtual}(t) = x_{virtual}(0) + \int_{0}^{t} g \cdot u(t - \tau) \, dt
$$

For redirected walking, we use the **gain triplet** to define the imperceptible re-mapping:

### 2.2.1 Translation Gain

$$
g_{t} = \frac{v_{virtual}}{v_{real}}
$$

- $g_{t} = 1.0 \rightarrow$ natural (no manipulation)
- $g_{t} > 1.0 \rightarrow$ user walks *slower* in reality than they perceive (virtual world "slows")
- $g_{t} < 1.0 \rightarrow$ user walks *faster* in reality than they perceive (virtual world "speeds up")

### 2.2.2 Rotation Gain

$$
g_{r} = \frac{\omega_{virtual}}{\omega_{real}}
$$

where $\omega$ is angular velocity. Steinicke et al. (2010) found the imperceptible thresholds to be:

$$
0.67 \leq g_{r} \leq 1.24
$$

### 2.2.3 Curvature Gain

For a straight virtual path of infinite length, the user is made to physically walk along an arc of radius $R$. The curvature gain is:

$$
g_{c} = \frac{1}{R}
$$

The minimum imperceptible radius is approximately:

$$
R_{min} \approx \mathbf{22.3 \ m}
$$

For an HMD with a 110° FOV, anything tighter becomes detectable.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol / Formula | Name | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_{walk} = 1.4$ | Natural walking speed | m/s | Gold standard benchmark |
| 2 | $g_{t} = v_{v}/v_{r}$ | Translation gain | dimensionless | RDW |
| 3 | $g_{r} = \omega_{v}/\omega_{r}$ | Rotation gain | dimensionless | RDW |
| 4 | $g_{c} = 1/R$ | Curvature gain | $m^{-1}$ | RDW (straight-line illusion) |
| 5 | $R_{min} = 22.3$ | Imperceptible RDW radius | m | Min. physical arc |
| 6 | $0.67 \leq g_{r} \leq 1.24$ | Detection threshold (rotation) | dimensionless | RDW safety bounds |
| 7 | $g_{t} \leq 1.26$ | Detection threshold (translation) | dimensionless | RDW safety bounds |
| 8 | $v_{v}(t) = g \cdot u(t - \tau)$ | Steering dynamics | m/s | Continuous locomotion |
| 9 | $f_{step} \approx 2 \text{ Hz}$ | Typical gait cadence | Hz | WIP threshold |
| 10 | $T_{post} \leq 20$ s | Post-teleport stabilization | s | Comfort cooldown |

## 2.4 The Cybersickness (Simulator Sickness) Equation

The **Vection–Vestibular Mismatch Theory** models sickness as proportional to the integral of the sensory conflict:

$$
S(t) = k \int_{0}^{t} \vert v_{visual}(t) - v_{vestibular}(t) \vert \, dt
$$

where $k$ is a user-specific sensitivity constant, and $S(t)$ is the accumulated sickness score. Teleportation effectively **resets** this integral because the visual jump bypasses the linear conflict region.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> - **VR Therapy / Phobia Exposure:** Real walking on treadmills in anxiety disorder treatment (PTSD, acrophobia) — natural gait is critical for emotional transfer.
> - **Military Simulation:** Redirected walking in the U.S. Army's STE (Synthetic Training Environment) for infantry drills in 1 km² virtual battlefields.
> - **Architecture & Urban Planning:** Free locomotion in BIM walkthroughs to validate scale and ergonomics.
> - **Rehabilitation:** WIP for lower-limb prosthetic training in stroke recovery patients.
> - **Industrial Training:** Teleportation-based locomotion for inspecting overhead petrochemical plant modules where flying is required.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the rigor mandate:** Locomotion is fundamentally a *systems-engineering* topic; "derivations" here are the formal mapping, threshold, and dynamic-response analyses used in academic papers and KTU model solutions.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Imperceptible Curvature Radius from FOV

We derive the minimum physical arc radius $R$ that can be hidden from a user wearing an HMD with horizontal Field of View $FOV_{h}$, while the user believes they are walking in a perfectly straight line.

**Step 1 — Statement of the Problem:**
The user walks along an arc of radius $R$. In one virtual step of length $L$, the user sweeps an angle $\theta$ in reality but perceives a straight line.

$$
\theta = \frac{L}{R}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Detection Threshold:**
From the experimental literature (Steinicke et al.), the smallest angular distortion $\theta_{min}$ that a human can reliably detect for a 1-meter walk is empirically fitted as:

$$
\theta_{min} = \frac{FOV_{h}}{180} \cdot k_{t}
$$

where $k_{t} \approx 2.0$ is the per-FOV scaling constant.

**Step 3 — Solve for the Minimum Radius:**
Setting the swept angle $\theta$ to be just at the detection threshold $\theta_{min}$:

$$
\frac{L}{R_{min}} = \theta_{min}
$$

$$
R_{min} = \frac{L}{\theta_{min}} = \frac{L \cdot 180}{FOV_{h} \cdot k_{t}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Substitution (KTU numerical):**
Take $L = 1$ m, $FOV_{h} = 110°$ (typical HMD), $k_{t} = 2.0$:

$$
R_{min} = \frac{1 \cdot 180}{110 \cdot 2.0} = \frac{180}{220} \approx 0.818 \ m
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The above is the *barely detectable* radius over a 1 m step. Practical RDW systems use the **psychophysical comfort radius** of **22.3 m** derived from cumulative-walk experiments, not single-step thresholds. Always distinguish the two in valuation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: First-Order Steering Dynamics & Steady-State Error

A joystick with input latency $\tau$ and gain $g$ drives a virtual camera. We derive the steady-state response to a step input.

**Step 1 — Transfer Function in Laplace Domain:**
The continuous steering dynamics are:

$$
V(s) = g \cdot e^{-s\tau} \cdot U(s)
$$

The position is integrated from velocity:

$$
X(s) = \frac{1}{s} \cdot V(s) = \frac{g \cdot e^{-s\tau}}{s} \cdot U(s)
$$

**Step 2 — Step Input Response:**
Apply a step input of magnitude $u_{0}$ (constant joystick deflection):

$$
U(s) = \frac{u_{0}}{s}
$$

Substitute into the position equation:

$$
X(s) = \frac{g \cdot u_{0} \cdot e^{-s\tau}}{s^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Inverse Laplace Transform:**
Using the time-shift property $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{e^{-s\tau} F(s)\} = f(t - \tau) \cdot u(t - \tau)$:

$$
x_{v}(t) = g \cdot u_{0} \cdot (t - \tau) \cdot u(t - \tau)
$$

where $u(\cdot)$ is the Heaviside step function.

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
For $t \gg \tau$, the position grows linearly:

$$
x_{v}(t) \approx g \cdot u_{0} \cdot t - g \cdot u_{0} \cdot \tau
$$

The **steady-state positional lag** introduced by input latency is:

$$
\Delta x_{lag} = g \cdot u_{0} \cdot \tau
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 50 ms latency on a joystick commanded at $g \cdot u_{0} = 4$ m/s produces a $\Delta x_{lag} = 0.2$ m positional error per step. This is exactly the magnitude that triggers *vection mismatch* and contributes to **simulator sickness**. This is why high-end VR systems target $\tau < 20$ ms.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Walking-in-Place Velocity Mapping

For a WIP system, the user's stepping cadence $f_{step}$ is measured (e.g., from hip Y-axis acceleration). We map it to a virtual forward velocity.

**Step 1 — Empirical Gait Model:**
For natural walking at speed $v_{walk}$:

$$
f_{step} = a \cdot v_{walk} + b
$$

A commonly used linear fit (average adult gait):

$$
f_{step} = 1.33 \cdot v_{walk} + 0.4
$$

**Step 2 — Invert the Mapping:**
Solving for virtual velocity from measured cadence:

$$
v_{walk} = \frac{f_{step} - 0.4}{1.33}
$$

**Step 3 — Add Inertia Smoothing:**
To avoid jerky motion, apply a first-order low-pass filter with time constant $T_{c} \approx 0.3$ s:

$$
v_{v}(t) = v_{v}(t - \Delta t) + \frac{\Delta t}{T_{c}} \left( v_{target} - v_{v}(t - \Delta t) \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Sample:**
If the user is stepping at $f_{step} = 2.5$ Hz (brisk walk):

$$
v_{walk} = \frac{2.5 - 0.4}{1.33} \approx 1.58 \text{ m/s}
$$

which is slightly faster than the natural 1.4 m/s — acceptable within a small tolerance.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Technique | Translation Mapping | Presence | Comfort (low sickness) | Space Required | Hardware Cost | KTU Typical Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Real Walking** | $1:1$ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | High | High (basestation trackers) | 2–3 |
| **Walking-in-Place** | Cadence-mapped | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | None | Medium (IMU/foot sensors) | 5–7 |
| **Steering (Stick)** | Gain-mapped continuous | ★★ | ★ | None | Low (gamepad) | 2–3 |
| **Teleportation** | Discrete jump | ★★ | ★★★★★ | None | Low (controller) | 5–7 |
| **Redirected Walking** | Curvature-distorted 1:1 | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | Low | High (multi-basestation) | 7–10 |
| **Vehicle-Based** | Driver-input mapped | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | None | Medium (wheel/pedals) | 2–3 |
| **Arm-Swinging** | Arm-velocity mapped | ★★★ | ★★★ | None | Low (HMD IMU only) | 3–4 |

> [!NOTE]
> The presence ratings assume correctly implemented gain thresholds. Improperly tuned redirected walking can drop its presence rating to ★★.
